title: Nezha-FX: A Morphing HAUV Designed Based on Balanced Cross-Domain Performance Optimization
authors: Yulin Bai, Xuwang Song, Xiqiao Han, Hexiong Zhou, Zheng Zeng
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: As emerging multirotor-type hybrid aerial underwater vehicles capable of both flight and underwater operations, numerous conceptual prototypes have been proposed in recent years. However, existing designs lack a balanced consideration of cross-medium stability, underwater maneuverability, and mode-switching reliability within a single configuration, and there remains no unified evaluation criterion for cross-domain performance. To compensate for the limitations of conventional optimization-based design, this work leverages key constraints identified through experiments and employ a multiobjective optimization approach to achieve a balanced design among these competing requirements. A lightweight and reliable foldable-arm system is further developed to support the implementation of the optimized configuration. In addition, through systematic integration of aerial propulsion bench test data with vehicle design parameters, we develop novel static quantitative metrics (cross-domain thrust margin@50%) to comprehensively assess overall design rationality. Finally, simulation and field tests show that the underwater damping of the final prototype has decreased by 55.2%. Compared with the previous generation, the cross-domain average power consumption is reduced by 68.9%.

title: RIHP-Net: Rotation-Invariant Hyperspectral Image Classification Via Harmonic Fiber Pooling and Rotation-Conditioned Spectral Fusion
authors: Arafat Al-Jawari, Li Fang, Metwalli Al-Selwi, Huifang Shen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) can degrade under in-plane rotations because spatial structures change orientation and the learned spectral–spatial coupling in patch-based models becomes less stable. Most existing approaches improve robustness mainly through data augmentation, without explicitly linking geometric invariance with spectral calibration in an end-to-end manner. To address this limitation, we propose RIHP-Net, a dual-path framework that models rotation as a shared latent factor for coordinated spectral and spatial processing. In the spectral path, we proposed a Rotation-Conditioned Spectral Fusion (RCSF) branch that learns band interactions and predicts a compact rotation code to adaptively calibrate per-band responses. In contrast, in the spatial path, we proposed the Harmonic Fiber Spatial Pooling (HFSP) branch to rotation-consistent geometric cues via an equivariant backbone and harmonic pooling to obtain a rotation-invariant spatial descriptor. A bi-directional rotation-gating module then fuses both representations by conditioning feature aggregation on the predicted rotation code. We evaluate RIHP-Net across multiple benchmarks with different training-data regimes, including both extremely limited-label and higher-label settings, and report consistent gains in rotation robustness while maintaining competitive accuracy under standard (non-rotated) evaluation. The source code is available here <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Arafat-Aljawari/RIHP-Net</uri>.

title: Real-Time Streamable Generative Speech Restoration with Flow Matching_supp1-3696215.pdf
authors: Simon Welker
publish: N/A
abstract: Diffusion-based generative models have greatly impacted the speech processing field in recent years, exhibiting high speech naturalness and spawning a new research direction. Their application in real-time communication is, however, still lagging behind due to their computation-heavy nature involving multiple calls of large DNNs. Here, we present Stream.FM, a frame-causal flow-based generative model with an algorithmic latency of 32 milliseconds (ms) and a total latency of 48 ms, paving the way for generative speech processing in real-time communication. We propose a buffered streaming inference scheme and an optimized DNN architecture, show how learned few-step numerical solvers can boost output quality at a fixed compute budget, explore model weight compression to find favorable points along a compute/quality tradeoff, and contribute a model variant with 24 ms total latency for the speech enhancement task. Our work looks beyond theoretical latencies, showing that highquality streaming generative speech processing can be realized on consumer GPUs available today. Stream.FM can solve a variety of speech processing tasks in a streaming fashion: speech enhancement, dereverberation, codec post-filtering, bandwidth extension, STFT phase retrieval, and Mel vocoding. As we verify through comprehensive evaluations and a MUSHRA listening test, Stream.FM establishes a state-of-the-art for generative streaming speech restoration, exhibits only a reasonable reduction in quality compared to a non-streaming variant, and outperforms our recent work (Diffusion Buffer) on generative streaming speech enhancement while operating at a lower latency.

title: Study on Structural and Magnetic Properties of Cobalt Substituted Permalloy Amorphous Nanocrystalline Ribbons
authors: Euiyeol Cho, Kwiyoung Lee, Jonghyeok Ahn, Hansol Lee, Jongryoul Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: This study investigates the structural and magnetic evolution of Co-substituted Fe-Ni amorphous-nanocrystalline ribbons. The addition of Co significantly enhances thermal stability and saturation magnetization by promoting the formation of the B2 ordered Fe-Co phase. However, this improvement is accompanied by an increase in coercivity, which is attributed to interfacial stress, lattice constant distortion, and the formation of precipitated compounds during the FCC-to-BCC phase transition. These findings indicate that while Co substitution effectively improves high-temperature stability and saturation magnetic flux, precise compositional control is essential to mitigate structural distortions that can deteriorate soft magnetic properties.

title: Moment-Reenacting: Inverse Motion Degradation With Cross-Shutter Guidance
authors: Xiang Ji, Guixu Lin, Zhengwei Yin, Jiancheng Zhao, Yinqiang Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Motion degradation, manifested as blur in global shutter (GS) images or rolling shutter (RS) distortion in RS counterparts, remains a fundamental challenge in computational imaging, especially under fast motion or low-light conditions. While prior works have treated blur decomposition and RS temporal super-resolution as separate tasks, this separation fails to exploit their intrinsic complementarity. In this paper, we propose a unified framework to invert motion degradation and reenact imaging moment by jointly leveraging the complementary characteristics of GS blur and RS distortion. To this end, we introduce a novel dual-shutter setup that captures synchronized blur-RS image pairs and demonstrate that this combination effectively resolves temporal and spatial ambiguities inherent in both modalities. For allowing flexible performance-cost trade-offs, we further extend this dual-shutter setup to a stereo Blur-RS configuration with a narrow baseline. In addition, we construct a triaxial imaging system to collect a real-world dataset with aligned GS-RS pairs and ground-truth high-speed frames, enabling robust training and evaluation beyond synthetic data. Our proposed network explicitly disentangles motion into context-aware and temporally-sensitive representations via a dual-stream motion interpretation module, followed by a self-prompted frame reconstruction stage. Extensive experiments validate the superiority and generalizability of our approach, establishing a new paradigm for realistic high-speed video reconstruction under complex motion degradations.

title: Reactive and Decoupled Control of Mobile Manipulators via Spatially Modulated Velocity Fields
authors: Tadej Petrič, Leon Žlajpah
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Mobile manipulators enable robotic systems to operate flexibly in extended, dynamic, and unstructured environments. A key challenge in such systems is achieving coordinated and adaptive behavior between the mobile base and the manipulator without relying on global localization or predefined plans. This paper presents a reactive and fully local control strategy for mobile base based on spatially modulated velocity fields defined in the mobile base’s frame. The method enables real-time end-effector tracking across the full workspace by exploiting only the internal kinematics of the arm. Using distance- and angle-based activation thresholds, the controller generates smooth linear and angular velocity commands for mobile base that support context-aware transitions between stationary, extension, and retreat modes. Importantly, the control framework is decoupled from the manipulator, allowing compatibility with autonomous planning, teleoperation, or physical guidance. The proposed architecture is particularly well suited for dynamic and collaborative manufacturing scenarios, where mobile robots must operate safely, robustly, and without dependence on global pose estimates. Experiments with a real mobile manipulator demonstrate stable and precise behavior across diverse trajectories, confirming the suitability of the method for adaptive, robust, and autonomous robotic systems.

title: VAPE-Net: An Extrinsic-Pose-Agnostic Viewpoint-Aware Progressive Enhancement Framework for Pure-Visual Vehicle-Infrastructure Cooperative Perception
authors: Jiankun Peng, Luwei Wang, Di Wu, Shen Li, Shuangzhi Yu, Ziyu Guo, Xiaoshuang Che, Chunye Ma
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Camera-based Vehicle-Infrastructure Cooperative 3D object detection (VIC3D) faces challenges including cross-view feature misalignment caused by viewpoint discrepancies, as well as semantic degradation and redundancy introduced by direct feature concatenation. To alleviate these issues, a pure-visual 3D object detection framework, VAPE-Net, is proposed for VIC3D. The framework comprises two key modules: (1) A Viewpoint-Aware Enhancement (VAE) module is introduced on both the vehicle-side and infrastructure-side branches. Instead of requiring precise extrinsic matrices for rigid cross-view geometric alignment, it captures semantic and geometric consistency by modeling feature correlations across adjacent and cross-view frames while only conditioning on static camera metadata, thereby improving robustness under moderate calibration perturbations. (2) A Stage-wise Cascaded Feature Fusion (SCFF) module is used to fuse voxel features in multiple stages, with a balancing mechanism to reduce information loss and suppress noise during fusion. Experiments on the real-world DAIR-V2X dataset demonstrate that VAPE-Net has a significant advantage over common fusion methods, achieving 16.05% AP3D and 22.03% APBEV. This research expands the model paradigm for cooperative perception dominated by vision, providing new insights and support for the theoretical framework of VIC3D.

title: A Time-Space Fusion Early Diagnostic Framework for PEM Fuel Cell Faults with Deep Learning
authors: Shengxiang Fu, Dongeon Kim, He Qi, Namwook Kim, Chunhua Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: An accurate fault diagnostic method of proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells can guide adjustments for control strategies and enable the system to operate under optimal conditions. However, the performance of existing PEM fuel cell fault diagnostic methods remains subject to uncertainty and limited verifiability due to the lack of real fault data, and the consideration of non-uniformity inside the stack is insufficient under faulty conditions. To address this issue, a time-space fusion early diagnostic framework for PEM fuel cell faults is proposed in this study, which is designed to provide early warnings at the onset of electrochemical mechanism deviation rather than the manifestation of performance degradation. Polarization tests are conducted to extract the related abnormalities data, and three temporal latent indicators are constructed for developing a deep learning (DL)-based method to predict the internal dynamic state of the stack. Besides, two spatial indicators are defined to characterize cell-level voltage non-uniformity as complementary information for early fault detection. By integrating the states of predicted temporal latent indicators and spatial indicators, a set of early diagnostic rules for membrane dry-out, flooding, and oxygen starvation is ultimately formulated. The results show that the proposed framework has higher credibility and mechanism consistency.

title: Gait adaptation to asymmetric foot-ground compliance applied by robotic footwear
authors: Mark Price, Wouter Hoogkamer, Meghan E. Huber
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Perturbing foot-ground interaction dynamics has shown promise for eliciting adaptations to inter-limb weight-bearing symmetry, a critical target for rehabilitation of asymmetrical neuromotor deficits affecting gait. To date, this perturbation paradigm has been delivered via adjustable stiffness treadmills, in which the treadmill deck displaces under the walker’s foot during the stance phase of gait. Recently, we developed robotic footwear capable of delivering asymmetrical ground stiffness perturbations during walking, offering a portable experimental platform for asymmetrical ground stiffness perturbations, allowing them to be executed overground and on conventional treadmills. In this study, we quantified kinetic and spatio-temporal gait adaptation to a foot-ground compliance perturbation delivered by the novel robotic footwear. We found that participants changed their vertical ground reaction force impulse, vertical push-off peak, and peak braking force to favor the unperturbed-side limb after the perturbation was removed, indicating a shift in neuromotor control elicited by the perturbation. We conclude that the robotic footwear can elicit weight bearing adaptations similar to adjustable stiffness treadmills, and that the dissipative mechanical properties of the shoes likely play a key role in the direction of the adaptation.

title: Momentum Gradient Descent with Kinematic Prediction-based Initialization for High-Accuracy Star Sensor Attitude Estimation
authors: Huijuan Zhang, Yizhuo Zhu, Changlin Wang, Yuanjin Yu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The traditional gradient descent method for attitude estimation is prone to local optimum, and the real-time performance is poor in dynamic environments. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved attitude estimation algorithm based on star sensor, which integrates momentum gradient descent (MGD) with kinematic prediction-based initialization (KPI). Firstly, the momentum term is introduced to accumulate the historical gradient information, so that the iterative direction could be optimized and the iterative oscillations could be effectively suppressed. Furthermore, the initial value prediction mechanism is designed based on the continuity of spacecraft dynamics. The attitude quaternion of the previous moment is fused with the angular velocity measured by the gyroscope, and the recursive estimation of the initial value is dynamically achieved through the attitude kinematic equation. Key MGD parameters are determined via dedicated sensitivity analysis to balance convergence speed and accuracy. During the attitude maneuver mode, MGD reduces the MAE by 73.28% and the RMSE by 72.48% compared with GD. For attitude stability control mode, MGD decreases the MAE by 72.20% and the RMSE by 72.09% relative to GD. Furthermore, in contrast to MGD with temporal recursive initialization, the proposed MGD-KPI further reduces the MAE by 79.86% and the RMSE by 79.76%, while achieving the shortest execution time. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed MGD with KPI algorithm achieves significant improvements in accuracy and computational efficiency for attitude estimation.

title: Field-agnostic Beam Focusing and Tracking for ISAC
authors: Hyunwoo Park, Donggeon Kim, Andrea Conti, Sunwoo Kim, Moe Z. Win
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Fast beam beam focusing and tracking (BFT) serves as a key enabler for delivering precise location awareness and high communication capacity in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) for next-generation wireless networks. However, major challenges arise from user mobility and the inherent coexistence of near field and far-field regions in extremely large-scale antenna arrays, leading to critical field-model mismatches and prohibitively high computational complexity, which demand efficient and adaptive tracking solutions. This paper proposes a method that addresses the coverage discontinuity between the near-field and far-field, as well as the computational difficulties associated with BFT. To achieve computationally efficient field-agnostic BFT, which implies being agnostic to near/far-field coverage discontinuities, two strategies are proposed. First, an optimization problem for radar-like echo signal processing of integrated sensing and communication is formulated to enable field-agnostic BFT across fields while reducing beam training overhead. Next, a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) framework is used to provide a computationally efficient solution for real-time execution. Results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves robust and precise tracking across all fields with low complexity, outperforming conventional tracking methods. Further more, the proposed method is more computationally efficient than existing methods, thus enabling real-time BFT for next-generation wireless networks.

title: Curvilinear Particle-in-Cell Simulations of Plasma Sheath Effects on Impedance of VLF Antenna in the Magnetosphere
authors: Kevin A. Shipman, Patrick L. Colestock, Gian Luca Delzanno, Mark A. Gilmore, Quinn R. Marksteiner, Daniil Svyatsky, Nikolai Yampolsky
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: In this paper we investigate how a magnetospheric plasma sheath effects the impedance of a spaceborne very low frequency (VLF) dipole. It has been suggested that a VLF antenna can be used to generate whistler waves to remediate magnetically trapped high-energy electrons from Earth’s radiation belts. The magnetosphere is composed of a low-density plasma that forms a nonlinear plasma sheath around the antenna, changing the impedance of the antenna and therefore changing how the effective antenna is able to couple radiation to the background environment. Therefore it is important to characterize the plasma sheath behavior in terms of impedance and sheath structure. We use a three-dimensional electrostatic curvilinear particle-in-cell (CPIC) code to simulate a electrically short dipole antenna operating in the Earth’s inner magnetosphere. The impedance of the sheath is approximated and is compared to existing radiation impedance models and the plasma sheath structure and time-behavior is characterized.

title: Investigating Key Meteorological Factors That Affect Atmospheric Transmittance via Simulation
authors: Beixi Jia, Chuanhui Wang, Shiru Han
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This work calculates atmospheric transmittance (AT) along the heliostat-receiver path using the SMARTS model. Input data consist of hourly observations (2016-2023) from 170 meteorological stations across China, including temperature (T2m), air pressure, relative humidity (RH), and visibility (VIS). Sensitivity experiments focusing on VIS and RH are carried out at the Beijing station. For a heliostat-receiver distance of 1 km, the national mean AT is found to lie mostly between 70% and 90%. Higher AT values-exceeding 85%-appear in solar-energy-rich areas: the Tibetan Plateau, Northwest China, Inner Mongolia, and parts of Northeast China. At every representative station, AT experiences a rapid drop after sunrise followed by a slow increase, with morning hours recording the lowest values. A larger daily range of AT corresponds to a lower long-term mean visibility at that station. A clear seasonal pattern also emerges: AT is higher in the April to September and lower in the October to March.

title: Online Damage-Updated Lifetime Equalization for a Si IGBT/SiC MOSFET Hybrid Parallel Inverter
authors: Weiwu Zhang, Deli Lin, Yu Zheng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Lifetime mismatch between Si and wide-bandgap devices is a critical reliability challenge in hybrid partial-power-processing converters. This paper proposes an online damage-updated lifetime-equalization strategy for a Si IGBT/SiC MOSFET hybrid parallel inverter. A preconstructed safe operating area is first used to constrain the feasible combinations of switching frequency and current-sharing ratio. An online lookup-table updating mechanism is then introduced to shift the optimal operating point according to the real-time damage states of the two device branches. Experimental validation is performed on a 10-kW prototype. For the undamaged initial operating point <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E$</tex>, the proposed method reduces the lifetime-difference index from 0.69 to 0.0256 and increases the limiting cycle count from 236173 to 536107. For the unequal-damage initial operating point <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F$</tex>, the online-updated strategy further reduces the lifetime-difference index from 0.147 to 0.035. The results demonstrate that the proposed method can maintain lifetime equalization under different damage combinations and significantly improve lifetime utilization of the hybrid parallel inverter.

title: Research on Underwater Construction Monitoring Device for Submarine Cable Repair in High-Turbidity Water Bodies
authors: Yingjie Wei, Weixiong Zhu, Xiaojun Li, Degao Zhu, Shiguan Zhang, Yuanhong Chen, Deping Zhao, Linzhuo Wang, Zhengri Tang, Weijia Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This electronic document is a “live” template and Abstract-Submarine cables are core infrastructure for offshore wind power grid integration and cross-sea power transmission, yet their in-situ repair faces severe imaging degradation in highturbidity water. Conventional underwater monitoring devices suffer from sharp accuracy drop, high missed detection rate, and poor real-time performance in turbid environments, which has become a key bottleneck for intelligent submarine cable repair. This work develops an integrated underwater construction target monitoring device with a dedicated optical system and lightweight edge-side enhancement algorithm. The device enhances the SNR of valid optical signals via blue-green band auxiliary illumination, and achieves end-to-end high-turbidity underwater image restoration and target monitoring via a lightweight codec network. Pool and real-sea tests verify that the device can reliably identify 20 mm targets within 0.3 m in 400 NTU turbid water, meeting the engineering requirements of submarine cable repair.

title: A Study on Dynamic and Reliable Assessment of Dispatchable Capacity from Electric Vehicles and Risk Early Warning for High-Penetration Renewable Energy Integration
authors: Yan Han, Hui Zhou, Liyang Jiang, Zhi Zhu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Under high-penetration renewable energy scenarios, V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) from electric vehicles is key to grid integration; however, the random nature of charging makes it difficult to assess dispatchable capacity, and the decline in aggregation efficiency limits reliable dispatch. To address these issues, this paper proposes a method for dynamic credibility assessment and risk warning of electric vehicle dispatchable capacity for high-penetration renewable energy integration. First, a user participation intention prediction model based on TCNTransformer is constructed to achieve temporal probability predictions of EV cluster grid connection/disconnection times and dispatchable capacity. Second, a dynamic credibility assessment model for dispatchable capacity that accounts for spatio-temporal correlations is established, incorporating conditional value at risk (VaR) to quantify the risk of dispatch failure caused by prediction errors. Based on this, a two-tier risk early warning mechanism is constructed: the grid side evaluates the confidence level of dispatchable capacity, while the aggregator side establishes a risk hedging model for reserve capacity failure. Case study results indicate that the proposed method effectively improves the accuracy of dispatchable capacity assessment, reduces the number of dispatch failures by 78.6 %, and increases the renewable energy integration rate by 6.8 percentage points, providing theoretical support for the reliable dispatch of EV resources in scenarios with high proportions of renewable energy.

title: Demo: Deploying Maneuver Coordination System in Real-Life Autonomous Driving Scenarios
authors: Mauro Filho, Andreia Figueiredo, Pedro Rito, Miguel Luís, Susana Sargento
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Cooperative maneuvering is essential for safe autonomous driving in connected environments. The European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) has defined the Maneuver Coordination System (MCS), which enables negotiation of cooperative actions via Vehicle to Everything (V2X) communication. However, its validation in real-world scenarios remains limited. This demonstration presents an MCS integrated with an autonomous driving stack, enabling real-time coordination between an Autonomous Vehicle (AV) and a Vulnerable Road User (VRU). Both participants exchange messages to negotiate and execute an overtaking maneuver on a test track. The system closes the loop between communication, decision-making, and vehicle control, ensuring that coordinated actions result in physical motion. The results demonstrate the feasibility of cooperative maneuver execution in a heterogeneous environment, deployed in the real world with autonomous driving participants.

title: MINISA: Minimal Instruction Set Architecture for Next-gen Reconfigurable Inference Accelerator
authors: Jianming Tong, Devansh Jain, Yujie Li, Charith Mendis, Tushar Krishna
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Modern reconfigurable AI accelerators rely on rich mapping and data-layout flexibility to sustain high utilization across matrix multiplication, convolution, and emerging applications beyond AI. However, exposing this flexibility through fine-grained micro-control results in prohibitive control overhead of fetching configuration bits from off-chip memory. This paper presents MINISA, a minimal instruction set that programs a reconfigurable accelerator at the granularity of Virtual Neurons (VNs), the coarsest control granularity that retains flexibility of hardware and the finest granularity that avoids unnecessary control costs. First, we introduce FEATHER+, a modest refinement of FEATHER, that eliminates redundant on-chip replication needed for runtime dataflow/layout co-switching and supports dynamic cases where input and weight data are unavailable before execution for offline layout manipulation. MINISA then abstracts control of FEATHER+ into three layout-setting instructions for input, weight, and output VNs and a single mapping instruction for setting dataflow. This reduces the control and instruction footprint while preserving the legal mapping and layout space supported by the FEATHER+. Our results show that MINISA reduces geometric mean off-chip instruction traffic by factors ranging from $35 \times$ to $\left(4 \times 10^{5}\right) \times$ under various sizes under 50 GEMM workloads spanning AI (GPT-oss), FHE, and ZKP. This eliminates instruction-fetch stalls that consume $96.9 \%$ of micro-instruction cycles, yielding up to $31.6 \times$ end-to-end speedup for $16 \times 256$ FEATHER+. Our code: https://github.com/maeri-project/FEATHER/tree/main/minisa.

title: Development of Transient Electromagnetic Field Transmitter with No-Load Shutdown
authors: Boheng Zhao, Xuhong Wang, Ruiqi Li, Muhan Liu
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: The Transient Electromagnetic Method (TEM) transmitter constitutes a critical component of TEM exploration, where the quality of the transmitted TEM waveform directly impacts the precision of exploration outcomes. This paper addresses the core issues of output waveform distortion and efficiency reduction in TEM transmitters under rapid switching conditions between no-load and load states, which arise due to abrupt changes in load impedance. A novel no-load turn-off type TEM transmitter scheme is proposed and implemented. By analyzing the TEM detection mechanism, the waveform quality requirements of high peak current, low turn-off overshoot, and minimal oscillation are explicitly defined. Building upon this foundation, a power topology based on a full-bridge inverter and auxiliary switches is designed. Moreover, a fuzzy PID dual closedloop control algorithm incorporating real-time feedback of load impedance is introduced, enabling precise control of the TEM waveform under no-load turn-off conditions and effectively suppressing voltage overshoot and ripple. Additionally, an active damping circuit is designed to eliminate inductive oscillations during the turn-off transient, thereby optimizing the quality of the current falling edge. Experimental results demonstrate that the developed prototype, under conditions of a 20Ω load impedance and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 8 m H}$</tex> inductance, can generate high-quality bipolar pulses with a peak current of 45 A, a turn-off time of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$34 \mu \mathrm{s}$</tex>, and a maximum transmission power of 45 kW. Furthermore, it exhibits significant superiority over traditional schemes in terms of waveform fidelity, load adaptability and overall energy efficiency, providing effective hardware support for enhancing the accuracy and efficiency of TEM detection.

title: A Novel Torque Distribution Strategy Balancing Energy Consumption and Stability for Four-Wheel Independent Drive Electric Vehicles
authors: Wei Xu, Yuan Zhou
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: To achieve multi-objective torque optimization balancing energy economy and driving safety for FWIDEVs, this paper proposes a torque distribution strategy. This strategy improves the lateral handling stability of the vehicle by optimizing the tire load rate, while minimizing the energy loss from in-wheel motors and tire slip. The performance of three strategies, namely the average distribution (AD) strategy, the motor loss and stability optimization (MHD) strategy, and the proposed MHSD strategy, is compared under the double-lane change maneuver. Simulation results demonstrate that the MHSD strategy achieves the optimal energy-saving performance on the wet road surface (adhesion coefficient μ=0.5). Compared with the AD strategy, MHSD reduces the total energy consumption by 1.73%, the tire slip loss energy by 11.5%, and the motor loss energy by 0.44%. Meanwhile, it significantly lowers the tire load rate with smaller fluctuations, which effectively enhances the lateral handling stability of the vehicle.

title: Log What Matters: Safety-Aware Adaptive Logging for CAVs in Dynamic Conditions
authors: Johora Akter Polin, Yichen Luo, FNU Sumaiya, Zheng Dong, Sidi Lu
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Although machine learning (ML) models for connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs) report steadily improving accuracy on curated benchmarks, these gains often fail to translate to onboard, in-field deployments. A primary reason is that the models are engineered around clean, information-dense inputs, while operational sensing data is imperfect and dominated by redundant or low-salience frames. This wastes scarce compute, creates pipeline backlog, and forces decisions to rely on stale perception and prediction outputs, degrading accuracy and increasing end-to-end latency. To bridge this gap, we propose CAAL-VLM, an adaptive logging pipeline that removes redundant and non-informative frames while preserving task-relevant content, thereby improving accuracy and timeliness. CAAL-VLM incorporates two designed reinforcement learning (RL) logging policies: one for camera data, which leverages SigLIP similarity and Qwen-VL semantics to retain safety-critical visual evidence while suppressing redundancy; and one for LiDAR data, where scene similarity and odometry stability guide adaptive logging while maintaining geometric fidelity. Building on adaptive logging, we further introduce a VLM-guided sensing module to maintain perception accuracy under challenging scenarios by adapting sensing decisions to scene context. Extensive experiments show that CAAL-VLM reduces I/O overhead by ~27% and perception latency by ~42%, while improving perception robustness under adverse conditions.

title: Hybrid CART Model for Adaptive Earthquake Risk Forecasting Under Uncertainty
authors: K Senthil Kumar, Thelu Santhosha, A. Thangam, P Joel Josephson, Shanmuga Priya S, Muralidharan J
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The challenges in adaptive earthquake and seismic risk assessment underpin the exploration of issues, models and methodologies in Adaptive Earthquake Risk Forecasting, wherein statistical techniques utilise probability quantification to estimate the likelihood of earthquakes within defined spatial, temporal and magnitude parameters, necessitating that each location in a study area be defined by a time-varying conditional intensity. Key building attributes, such as construction materials, number of floors and age, were examined to assess the potential for earthquake-induced damage. Machine learning models were employed, utilising oversampling techniques, specifically Random Oversampling and SMOTE, to address class imbalance and improve predictive accuracy. The findings demonstrate that the CART model, particularly when integrated with SMOTE oversampling, outperformed other methods, attaining a macro F1-score of 95.88. In comparison, Random Oversampling also yielded significant enhancements, achieving a macro F1-score of 92.67 relative to the imbalanced baseline. This study elucidates the comparative strengths and weaknesses of various machine learning methodologies for predicting earthquake damage, underscoring the significance of feature selection and data balancing techniques. It provides critical insights for policymakers and engineers aiming to enhance earthquake preparedness and foster resilient infrastructure design through adaptive data-driven seismic risk forecasting.

title: Architectural Design of Low-latency and Highbandwidth 3D NAND with Split-Stacked Row Decoder for LLM Inference
authors: Youngjun Park, Chanho Kim, Seunghwan Song, Wonbo Shim
publish: 2026 IEEE International Memory Workshop (IMW)
abstract: In this work, we present an architectural design of 3D NAND dies and stacks tailored to LLM inference, aiming to significantly increase memory bandwidth compared to conventional NAND for storage applications. Based on the key factors affecting 3D NAND read latency, a predictive read latency model is developed for various plane sizes and block configurations. In addition, by exploiting hybrid bonding technology, we introduce a split-stacked row decoder (SSRD) structure that reduces the row decoder area by half and significantly improves bit density. Using the proposed low read latency 3D NAND die, a High-Bandwidth NAND stack is designed based on the physical dimensions of HBM3 12-Hi to achieve high bandwidth. Accounting for the area and thickness differences between DRAM die and 3D NAND die, the optimal stack configurations were explored, and the resulting capacity and maximum internal read bandwidth were estimated. The designed High-Bandwidth NAND stack achieves over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \times$</tex> the capacity of recent HBM stack while providing an internal read bandwidth per die exceeding 1 TB/s.

title: TeethSMOTE: A Feature Neighborhood Fusion and Enhancement method for Dental Image Analysis
authors: Xiaoying Pan, Yifan Wang
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Aiming at the severe class imbalance problem in dental image analysis, particularly in the diagnosis of ectopic eruption in pediatric panoramic radiographs, this study proposes a novel feature neighborhood fusion and enhancement method TeethSMOTE. Experiments based on a self-constructed pediatric dental curved tomography dataset demonstrate that this method can significantly improve the recognition accuracy of deep learning models for scarce categories as well as their overall generalization ability. TeethSMOTE exhibits stable and significant performance improvements across different model architectures, including traditional CNNs, modern convolutional networks, and Transformers, validating its effectiveness and robustness as a model-agnostic data preprocessing solution.

title: STCO Evaluation of High Bandwidth Storage for Cost-Efficient LLM Training
authors: Takashi Maeda, Aakash Patel, Khakim Akhunov, Julien Ryckaert, Alessio Spessot, Dwaipayan Biswas
publish: 2026 IEEE International Memory Workshop (IMW)
abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) requires extremely large capacity, typically provided by HBM in current systems, which significantly increases system cost. Recently, high-bandwidth storage (HBS) has been proposed as a costefficient alternative memory for AI workloads. However, HBS exhibits long access latencies, asymmetric read/write performance, and limited endurance, making its practical deployment challenging. In this study, we employ an analytical performance model to evaluate the feasibility of HBS for LLM training. We analyze the impact of bandwidth and latency on system efficiency, as well as endurance requirement for offloading workloads in LLM training. Our results indicate that mitigating latency bottlenecks requires HBS to adopt extensive parallelism of 128 planes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{1 6}$</tex> dies. Endurance analysis reveals that offloading workloads demand significantly lower endurance than HBM, enabling cost optimization through improving HBS reliability. Finally, we explore HBM-HBS trade-offs under a footprint constraint, demonstrating that balanced configuration achieves optimal performance. These findings provide design guidelines for cost-efficient, highperformance LLM training system with HBS.

title: Novel EEG Autoencoding Framework using HSDBN with Adaptive Feature Learning for Health
authors: M. Rama, T Nalini, Arulanantham G
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The non-stationarity, high dimensionality and noise contamination of the electroencephalography (EEG) data make the analysis is highly problematic in terms of feature representation, which limits the effectiveness of the conventional deep learning methods in brain-computer interfaces and clinical diagnoses to good health and well-being. To enhance EEG autoencoding and classification, this research paper presents a new Hierarchical Sparse Deep Belief Network with Adaptive Compression (HSDBN-AC) framework which is a combination of improved deep belief network and stacked sparse autoencoders. To specify network parameters, the design applies unsupervised pre-training with limited Boltzmann machines. This is followed by a fine-tuning under adaptive sparsity constraints that dynamically determine a trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and dimensionality reduction. Although a compression module changes the level of sparsity according to the real-time reconstruction error feedback, multi-scale convolutional layers extract the local temporal structure and global brain activity at multiple frequency bands. The framework has an accuracy of 99.1 % and precision of 98.9 % with recall of 99.4 % and a 0.989 AUC value, which surpasses baseline methods by 3.9 %. Having a mean absolute error of 0.17 and a signal to noise ratio of 18.8 dB, the proposed model has outstanding reconstruction properties with a compression ratio of 16: 1 to be transmitted and stored efficiently. The HSDBN-AC system offers a powerful foundation of real-time EEG analysis in the clinical use of brain-computer interfaces, cognitive state monitoring, and seizure prediction.

title: ASIC Design for GenAI Inference
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)

title: LSTM - XGBoost Based Intelligent Handover Optimization Framework for 5G Networks Using Real-World Mobility Data
authors: Varsha S, Harini Elangovan, R. Mohandas, Pandimeena R., Vinodhini, S Senthil Kumar
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Usage of communication is increasing which makes it very critical to improve it. Currently communication networking system has been upgraded to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5}^{\text{th}}$</tex> generation which makes communication stronger and more effortless. Yet, some properties of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5^{\text{th}}$</tex> generation communication system generate handover problem which develops disturbances and interruptions in communication. Therefore, the main objective of this research is to make sure that the existence of a smooth communication network. In order to achieve our goal, we have opted to implement machine learning. Employment of machine learning resulted in optimization of handover in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5^{\text{th}}$</tex> generation communication network technology. Machine learning namely XGBoost and LSTM are utilized. Implementation of machine learning aids to predict the best target cell to be linked with the User equipment, predicts the mobility of the user by analyzing the mobility patterns of the user, load balancing in cells of the nearby tower, dynamic parameters optimization and prediction of handover failures. Real-time mobility and radio signal data are collected using the G-NetTrack app and are used as model to verify the accuracy of the result of our proposed method. Our proposed method achieves efficiency of 99.5 %. This research furthermore guides young scientists and researchers working on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6^{\text{th}}$</tex> generation communication technology to design and develop a greater version of communication technology.

title: A Gate-All-Around Back-Gated Junctionless 3D NAND Structure for Improved Switching Efficiency and Variability Suppression
authors: Seah Min, Sohee Kim, Jong Kyung Park, Yun-Heub Song
publish: 2026 IEEE International Memory Workshop (IMW)
abstract: As 3D NAND technology scales vertically to ultrahigh layer counts, cell current degradation caused by the low mobility of poly-silicon channels has emerged as a critical bottleneck. Junctionless (JL) devices employing heavily doped channels have been introduced to boost cell current through bulk conduction. However, such devices suffer from degraded cut-off characteristics and increased threshold voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(V_{\text{th}})$</tex> variability due to random dopant fluctuation (RDF) and grain boundary segregation. In this paper, we propose a gate-allaround with back-gate (GAAB) junctionless 3D NAND structure to address these limitations. By introducing a backside control gate in planar test devices, we experimentally demonstrate that applying a negative back-bias induces a virtual thin-body effect. This effect effectively suppresses backchannel leakage and improves the subthreshold swing (SS), while preserving the high current drive capability of the junctionless channel. Furthermore, 3D TCAD simulations on a 64-word-line string confirm that the proposed structure significantly enhances switching efficiency and reduces variability compared with conventional schemes, offering a robust solution for ultra-high stack 3D NAND technology.

title: Overcoming Erase Limitations of Amorphous Oxide Semiconductor Channels Through Dual Source/Drain Structures for Flash Memory
authors: Sukin Kang, Jaewon Ham, Yonghee Lee, Sahngik Aaron Mun, Jinheon Choi, Juneseong Choi, Hyungjeung Kim, Shihyun Kim, Subin Moon, Cheol Seong Hwang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Memory Workshop (IMW)
abstract: In this work, amorphous oxide semiconductors (AOS), which offer high carrier mobility, excellent uniformity, and atomic layer deposition (ALD) process compatibility, are proposed as promising channel materials for three-dimensional (3D) NAND flash memory. The large bandgap of AOS results in low erase performance in charge-trap flash (CTF) devices. To address this issue, a dual-source/drain (S/D) structure with highand low-work-function metals is introduced. The feasibility of the proposed structure is validated through TCAD simulations and experimental results. By employing S/D electrodes with different work functions, both high on-current and enhanced erase characteristics are simultaneously achieved. This study demonstrates that the erase-limited performance of AOS channels can be effectively addressed through structural engineering, thereby highlighting the potential of AOS as a nextgeneration channel material for 3D NAND flash memory.

title: MAPLE: Multi-session Anchor-based Point cloud Loop-closure for Efficient Merging
authors: Ren Zhong, Tianyang Chen, Weisong Shi
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Multi-session LiDAR mapping is common in real deployments (multi-day or multi-route), but merging independently built submaps is failure-prone in cities: cross-session revisits are sparse, drift can exceed local search ranges, weakly synchronized GPS is unreliable as a tight constraint, and frame drops can introduce faulty edges that deform the map. We present MAPLE, a practical multi-session submap merging pipeline that uses weak GPS only as a coarse index; uses OpenStreetMap (OSM) topology to select a compact set of intersection anchors for scalable candidate retrieval; and uses a quality-control (QC) gate to accept only geometrically consistent cross-session constraints. MAPLE verifies candidates via coarse-to-fine registration and applies a dual-metric QC rule based on fitness and inlier RMSE; accepted constraints are inserted incrementally to improve optimization stability. On HeLiPR Town, MAPLE detects injected faulty edges with 0.958 precision and 0.969 F1, and improves Town02 merging accuracy across 2–8 submaps, reducing trajectory APE RMSE from 11.62 m (baseline) to 5.26 m (best).

title: Research on High-Precision Indoor Navigation System Based on Improved Federated Filtering and Tightly-Coupled Fusion
authors: Zhenyu Shi, Jianan Liu, Shiying Kou, Pengliang Song
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Aiming at the bottlenecks of insufficient accuracy and poor robustness of single-sensor navigation systems in indoor GPS-denied environments, this paper proposes a multi-source sensor fusion navigation scheme integrated with high-precision IMU, millimeter-level UWB, and LiDAR. The core innovation lies in the construction of an improved federated filtering architecture based on adaptive weight allocation and the design of an IMU/UWB deep tightly-coupled fusion model. Experimental results demonstrate that the system achieves a static positioning Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<=0.8 \text{mm}$</tex>, a dynamic navigation average error <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<=2 \text{mm}$</tex>, and a calculation frequency 100 Hz. Compared with the traditional EKF loosely-coupled scheme, the accuracy is improved by 89%, maintaining excellent robustness in complex scenarios. This scheme provides reliable millimeter-level navigation support for precision manufacturing and micro-robotics.

title:
A 0.01-mm
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    10-bit 5-GS/s Current-Steering DAC Using Replica Current Generator for Cryogenic Applications
authors: Yi-Hsuan Chiu, Shen-Iuan Liu
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: A 10-bit 5-GS/s current-steering digital-to-analog converter (DAC) is presented for cryogenic applications. This DAC adopts two current elements to achieve a compact area. The proposed replica bias generator is used to calibrate the currents of these two current elements. To further mitigate the gradient mismatch errors and reduce the routing path length, a circular diagonal routing technique is proposed. The DAC is fabricated in 40nm CMOS process. It achieves >39 dBc and >28 dBc SFDR over the entire Nyquist bandwidth at 5 GS/s under 300 K and 4K, respectively. Including the direct digital frequency synthesizer (DDFS), this DAC occupies 429 μm × 292 μm and consumes 67 mW at 300 K and 65 mW at 4 K, respectively. Excluding the DDFS, the core area of the DAC is 168 μm × 292 μm with the power of 22 mW at both temperatures.

title: Exploring the Energy Storage and Voltage Control Unit Design Space in Battery-Less IoT
authors: Lukas Liedtke, Espen Holsen, Per Gunnar Kjeldsberg, Frank Alexander Kraemer, Magnus Jahre
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Battery-less Internet of Things (IoT) devices are emerging because it will be environmentally and economically unsustainable to power billions of IoT devices with batteries. Battery-less devices harvest energy from their environment, but the energy supplied by many such sources varies considerably over time, and there is hence a need for buffering surplus energy. This is enabled by the Energy Storage and Voltage Control Unit (ESVU), and the state-of-the-art ESVUs are REACT and CapDYN. We observe that an ideal ESVU (i) wastes minimal energy (efficiency), (ii) enables the application System-on-Chip (SoC) quickly when energy becomes available (responsiveness), (iii) can support a large variety of SoCs (applicability), and (iv) requires few or cheap components and occupies minimal area and volume (overhead). Through detailed circuit-level simulations, we demonstrate that no existing ESVU ticks all the boxes. More specifically, we find that CapDYN and REACT can provide high efficiency and responsiveness, but they fall short in applicability and overhead, respectively, and we thus propose Coulombix to fill this gap. To understand how ESVU design affects performance at the system level, we conduct a case study in which we implement hardware prototypes of REACT and Coulombix and measure the throughput and response time of a diverse set of IoT benchmarks with a solar energy harvester across three seasons. The case study supports the insights of the simulation-based study, while also exposing interesting second-order effects, highlighting that end-to-end analysis is critical when evaluating ESVUs.

title: FYI: A Foundational Counter Architecture for Building CPI Stacks on Out-of-Order Processors
authors: Silvio Campelo de Santana, Lieven Eeckhout, Magnus Jahre
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: The end of Dennard scaling and the imminent end of Moore’s law is making it increasingly critical to write software that fully utilizes the hardware compute resources. Software developers hence need to gain insight into the inefficiencies of their applications, and a typical first step is to obtain applicationlevel Cycles-Per-Instruction (CPI) stacks, for example by using a Top-Down methodology. Unfortunately, existing approaches have only been validated against processors with idealized microarchitectural resources. Although this approach adds confidence that the generated CPI stacks make intuitive sense, it does not conclusively document that the CPI stacks break down total execution time into components in an architecturally sound manner. Our goal in this paper is to bridge this knowledge gap. We first define a complete and mutually exclusive reference that accounts for all dispatch slots in all clock cycles across all microoperations ($\mu$ ops), i.e., a $\mu$ op is either (i) dispatching, (ii) stalled while waiting for a structural back-end stall, (iii) not available due to a front-end issue, or (iv) mistakenly executed due to misspeculation. If the reference is also architecturally sound, i.e., it attributes each dispatch slot to the CPI stack component that accurately represents the impact on effective dispatch bandwidth, we refer to it as foundational; second-order overlap effects typically mean that an architecture has multiple foundational references. We derive foundational references for the BOOM out-of-order core, and, surprisingly perhaps, find that they can be implemented in hardware with less than 1 KB of additional state. We hence propose the Foundational Yet Implementable (FYI) counter architecture for CPI stacks that are, by design, an exact match for the target reference.

title: A 28 nm 16 Mb Embedded RRAM IP with Hybrid Array Structure for OTA Update Application
authors: Xiangchao Ma, Shan Gao, Di Wang, Hanyu Ren, Tianhui Xu, Changzhao Xin, Kun Wang, Jianshi Tang, Bin Gao, He Qian, Huaqiang Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Memory Workshop (IMW)
abstract: In this work, we present a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 8 n m} \mathbf{1 6 M b}$</tex> embedded resistive random-access memory (RRAM) IP, ideally suited for automotive over-the-air (OTA) update application. Our design features a 1T1R/2T2R hybrid array structure that enhances the reliability and system compatibility. To address the IR-drop issue and improve cell uniformity, a load balancing approach is implemented. A hierarchical column selection strategy is also employed to suppress the leakage current in the write path, optimizing write operations at high temperature. Furthermore, reliability test results demonstrate the RRAM IP achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 K}$</tex> cycles and 10 years of retention at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 5}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$</tex> in the 1T1R mode, and at least 200 K cycles and 10 years of retention at 125 °C in the 2T2R mode. These silicon test results pave the way for the application of RRAM in automotive OTA scenarios.

title: Implicit Chinese Compound Sentence Relation Classification Based on BSGGT Hybrid Model
authors: Xinwei Ma, Yuan Li, Fuxue Wu, Hualong Xiao
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Implicit relation recognition in Chinese compound sentences is a crucial task in Chinese language processing. Due to the semantic diversity and syntactic flexibility of such constructions, this task poses a significant challenge in natural language processing. In this paper, we propose the BSGGT hybrid model, which effectively leverages both structural and semantic information of compound sentences. The model first employs SeqGAN for data augmentation and then feeds both the original and augmented data into BERT to obtain word embeddings. Subsequently, using the Language Technology Platform (LTP) and the resulting embedding sequences, we construct three types of graphs: a dependency syntactic graph, a semantic dependency graph, and an additional word co-occurrence graph. Each of these graph representations is then processed independently by a Graph Transformer, and features extracted from the augmented data are fused. Finally, the fused features are combined with BERT’s predictions through weighted fusion to produce the final classification output. Experimental results on the Chinese Compound Clause Corpus (CCCS) show that our model achieves an accuracy of 82.35% and a macro-F1 score of 82.35%, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Ferroelectric Memories for Storage and Computing
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)

title: Research on a Text Classification Model Based on Multi-Level Feature Fusion
authors: Simeng Chen, Dongming Xu, Liangmin Zhong, Yimeng Wang
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Text classification is a core task in natural language processing and supports many real applications, such as public opinion monitoring, recommendation, and information filtering. Chinese text classification is still challenging because of word segmentation ambiguity, long-range semantic dependency, and flexible syntactic structure. Existing deep models, including pre-trained language models such as BERT, usually rely on the [CLS] token for sentence-level prediction and pay limited attention to complementary syntactic information. To address these issues, we propose a dual-branch multi-level feature fusion model for Chinese text classification. The semantic branch adopts a BERT-BiGRU architecture to capture contextual semantics and long-range dependencies, while the structural branch employs a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to encode explicit dependency relations. A Gated Feature Alignment and Fusion (GFAF) module further aligns heterogeneous features in a shared space and adaptively balances semantic and structural cues through a dynamic gating mechanism. Experiments on benchmark Chinese datasets show that our model consistently outperforms the BERT-CLS baseline, with especially clear gains on the fine-grained Fudan dataset. These results confirm the benefit of combining semantic and structural signals through gated fusion and demonstrate the robustness of the proposed method.

title: Flood Mod: A Flood Based Emergency Response System with Multi Model LLM Pipeline for Emergency Call Triage & Resource Allocation
authors: Tasneem I, Thejuswini R, Johny Elma K
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Current disaster response system used for floods face various issues, some of which include poor coordination, uneven resource allocation and long manual procedures causing huge delays for effective relief operations. This research work proposes Flood Mod, an intelligent way for effective flood response management. It utilizes an LLM pipeline for resource allocation and deep learning for incident prioritization. This provides the relief operator with a way to generate a resource allocation plan for every incident that was reported and logged in the system. The incidents logged into the system are processed with a bidirectional Long Short-Term memory (LSTM) model which helps rank incidents by assigning priority scores. The allocation plans suggested by the LLM can be reviewed & modified by the relief operator, hence ensuring human moderation. The prioritization model achieved an RMSE of 0.086, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 0.9188 and MAE of 0.0628. The LLM was evaluated with GEval. This system helps reduce response time through prioritization & quicker resource allocation.

title: A Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Lung Diseases Classification
authors: Guddeti Kalyani, T. Aditya Sai Srinivas, Seelam Guru Sunitha Reddy, Shaik Arshiya, Kuruva Himaja, K. Dora Babu
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: In this project, the proposed solution identifies lung-related ailments with the aid of chest X-ray images with the application of sophisticated deep-learning methods. Pneumonia, tuberculosis, and COVID-19 are among the diseases that get complicated in case they are not detected at an early stage, so timely and precise diagnosis is critical. Historically, radiol- ogists have been studying each X-ray manually, and it is time-consuming and burdensome, particularly during large patient periods. To overcome this, the suggested system employs deep-learning architectures that evaluate thousands of medical images and acquire nuanced patterns that cannot be easy to pick by the human eye. The models such as ResNet, DenseNet, EfficientNet, CoAtNet, and Vision Transformers are used due to their efficacy in capturing both small and big scale features. With a lot of precision, the system can categorize X-rays as either healthy or diseased after training. This will reduce human error, hasten diagnosis, help in clinical decision-making, and eventually help lead to better patient outcomes. Altogether, the project presents one of the most reliable and up-to-date AI-based solutions to improving the efficiency and precision of the lung disease identification.

title: Bi-Level Optimization-Based Intelligent Clinical Decision Support for Pharmacogenomic-Guided Antimicrobial Therapy
authors: Chandana Mareddy, Thota Rashmi, Patil Bhuvan Theja Reddy, T. V. Smitha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: This paper hypothesizes a new and improved bi level optimization model in antimicrobial stewardship based on pharmacogenomic. The current antibiotic decision-support systems are mostly inert, rule-based, and this is a major factor that causes poor treatment decisions to be made thus resulting in the growing issue of antimicrobial resistance. In a bid to counter these shortcomings, the proposed framework will incorporate knowledge engineering and sophisticated learning methods, in order to facilitate adaptive and personal clinical decision-making. The higher level in this framework aims at the global healthcare objectives, such as better patient outcomes, decreased side effects, lesser antimicrobial resistance, and better cost efficiency. The lower level focuses on the individual patient reactions to antibiotics by integrating the information of pharmacogenomic results and gives a more precise and tailored decision in terms of treatment. The clinical decision support system (CDSS) is able to produce adaptive recommendations by using both level of information with the application of advanced learning methods, including the best antibiotic choice, the best dose of medication, and the termination or continuation of therapy. Simulation evaluation proves the fact that the proposed approach substantially enhances the success rates of treatment, minimizes the time to start effective therapy and reduces the probability of toxicity of drugs used in the case of the current methods. These findings suggest that AI-driven, knowledge-based optimization frameworks can improve clinical decision support systems and support progress in precision medicine. This study is consistent with several United Nations Sustainable Development Goals. By supporting patient safety and improved treatment outcomes through individualized antimicrobial therapy, this work aligns with SDG 3: Good Health and Well-Being. The use of AI-based decision support systems can support SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure) by improving innovation in healthcare infrastructure and helping systems run in a more efficient and resilient way.

title: Cocktail Security Scheme for Patching the Bus Vulnerability of Chiplet ICs
authors: Jui-Hsien Wang, Zheng-Hao Wang, Shi-Yu Huang, Chi-Kang Chen
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: In a modern chiplet-based IC, the communication bus wires are exposed to security attacks during assembly. It is susceptible that unauthorized circuits could be illegally inserted and attached to the communication bus to steal confidential information. As a result, extra security measures are needed to safeguard the information sent over the communication bus. However, existing crypto-hardware may introduce an area overhead too high for a small die. Considering this, we propose a security enhancement framework that is especially suited for the communication bus in a chiplet-based IC. Our framework mixes and matches multiple layers of simple yet effective security schemes, such as simple die authorization, salted hash function, XOR-based encryption and decryption, and Linear-Feedback Shift-Register (LFSR) based dynamic keys. Overall, it introduces a very low area overhead and only a 1-cycle performance penalty, and thus, is suitable for even a very small die while still providing adequate protection from illegally inserted dice.

title: Vertical aligned Hexagonal Boron Nitride with Ballistic Thermal Conductivity
authors: Ping Che Lee, Diego Contreras Mora, Mingeun Choi, SeongUk Yun, Dipayan Pal, Amy Ross, Mark Clark, Larry Chen, Haripin Chandra, Ravindra Kanjolia, Mansour Moinpour, Satish Kumar, Andrew Kummel
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) is attractive for BEOL integration due to its wide bandgap, low dielectric constant, and high intrinsic thermal conductivity; however, achieving crystalline BN below 400 °C remains challenging. We demonstrate a 350 °C atomic layer annealing ALD (ALA-ALD) process using BBDMAB and −25 V Ar<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> substrate bias plasma to form vertically aligned, impurity-free h-BN. The ion-assisted crystallization reorients BN from layered to columnar domains, enabling ballistic phonon transport in sub-60 nm films. The resulting films an effective thermal boundary conductance of 149 MW m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup> K<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>, a five-fold improvement over PEALD BN. This process provides a scalable pathway for nanoscale heat-spreading applications.

title: Adaptive AI Financial Ecosystem: Proactive Crisis Prediction and Policy Simulation Using GNN and Reinforcement Learning
authors: V. Naveen, G. Ashwitha, B. Varshika, J. Mohana, M. Sakthivel, R. Asha
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: As financial systems continue to grow more complex and interconnected, they are also becoming more prone to disruption and systemic crises. This paper presents an Adaptive AI Financial Ecosystem which aims at identifying instabilities early before they arise. It uses Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to represent dynamic interdependencies between banks, fintech platforms, and markets, and timeseries forecasting models to analyse past and current indicators to produce early-warning signals. An agent of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in a Digital Twin simulation environment evaluates and suggests the best policy behavior in changing situations. The techniques of explainable AI (XAI) also provide transparency and regulatory interpretability. The empirical findings indicate that the proposed system is able to achieve an F1-score of 0.82, achieve a 11-day lead time on crises, and a 61 -percent decrease in systemic stress, which is greater than the traditional surveillance methodologies. The results highlight the possibility of adaptive, self-education AI systems to enhance financial stability and build a resilient and data-driven economy.

title: Optimized Random Forest–Based Soft Computing Pipeline for Multi-Class DDoS Detection in Cloud Environments
authors: Mridul Sikder, Sourav Mohanta, Sayon Chakraborty, Anindya Rudra
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks pose a severe threat to the reliability of online services by leveraging large botnets to overwhelm server and network resources. Traditional detection methods struggle to distinguish complex attack patterns, especially in multi-class scenarios. This paper proposes a systematic machine learning pipeline utilizing an optimized Random Forest classifier for robust multi-class DDoS detection. Using the BCCC-cPacket-Cloud-DDoS-2024 dataset comprising over 700,000 network flows and 324 features, the pi peline applies comprehensive data preprocessing, feature reduction, and hyperparameter tuning via GridSearchCV. The optimized model achieves 94.52% test accuracy and a weighted F1-score of 0.9415, demonstrating statistically significant improvement over the baseline configuration and competitive performance compared to existing Random Forest-based approaches. Feature importance analysis identifies 65 key features, enabling dimensionality reduction for deployment. Experimental results confirm high robustness (cross-validation standard deviation < 0.001), effective differentiation among Attack, Benign, and Suspicious traffic, and provide insights into handling class imbalance. These findings demonstrate the practical potential of a soft-computing-based machine learning pipeline for real-time, resource-aware DDoS defense in modern cloud and edge environments.

title: A Multi-Model Deep Learning Framework for Hate Speech Detection in Code-Mixed Low-Resource Languages
authors: M Mohanasundari, S.S. Boopathy, C. Kotteeswari, Manikandan M, Aswin K S, Kishore S
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Rapid expansion of social media platforms has magnified ethical, social, and cultural issues, allowing for a concurrent rise in inappropriate and harmful material, specifically hate speech, in regional languages. Detection of hate speech in Dravidian languages - in particular, Tamil (TA-AT-v2), Telugu, and Malayalam (ML-AT-v2) - is extremely difficult given the high level of linguistic variability, code-mixing of English with regional languages, and critical lack of annotated data. This paper addresses this gap by undertaking a comparison of deep learning techniques by applying three state-of-the-art multilingual transformer models: mDeBERTa-v3-base, MPNet (paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2), and the lightweight MiniLM-L6v2(Minimal Language Model). To address the severe class imbalance and scarcity (for example, the Tamil dataset has 514 samples with only 33 samples of a Political hate speech category), an efficient data augmentation strategy to increase the usable dataset Tamil to 740 samples. Investigation made on these different architectures' abilities to create an efficient, languagesensitive systems and show that combining their complementary abilities within a final Optimized Multi-Model Classification / Prediction / Analysis (MCPA) Framework, creates better and more reliable performance within this low-resource environment. Final result contributes robust, scalable system architecture to build a safer online experience for users of Dravidian languages.

title: 2025 CERN Compendium of Radiation Effects in Candidate Electronics for High-Energy Particle Accelerator Systems
authors: Rudy Ferraro, Alessandro Zimmaro, Antonio Scialdone, Gilles Foucard, Eleftheria Vasileiadi, Spyridon Georgakakis, Vasja Pirc, Salvatore Danzeca, Alessandro Masi
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: The sensitivity of a variety of components for particle accelerator electronics has been analyzed against Single Event Effects, Total Ionizing Dose and Displacement Damage. The tested parts include analog, linear, digital, and mixed devices.

title: Performance Study of Artificial Neural Network Based Control for Electric Vehicle to Vehicle Energy Transfer
authors: Vikash Kumar Gupta, Saket Yeolekar
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: This paper presents a data driven control approach for direct vehicle to vehicle (V2V) energy exchange, where an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) regulates the current delivered between two electric vehicles. The control scheme supports energy sharing through onboard converter hardware while ensuring stable current tracking under diverse operating conditions. A detailed simulation model is developed in MATLAB/Simulink to capture nonlinear behavior associated with battery characteristics, switching devices and converter interactions. System responses from a wide range of operating states are collected to form a training dataset, enabling the ANN to learn the relationship between measured variables and the required duty cycle. After training, the ANN controller replaces conventional control logic within the V2V model to achieve closed loop current regulation across different transfer modes. Simulation results indicate quicker settling, lower overshoot and improved current tracking. Overall, the study highlights ANN based control as a practical option for enhancing reliability and adaptability in emerging V2V charging systems.

title: Optimizing Photovoltaic Efficiency Through Underground Cooling: A Comparative Review of Water and Air-Based Systems
authors: Rindi Wulandari, Iwa Garniwa, Faiz Husnayain
publish: 2026 IEEE 6th International Conference in Power Engineering Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: High operating temperature is one of the main factors that reduces the efficiency and power output of photovoltaic (PV) systems. An increase in PV module temperature can result in an efficiency reduction of approximately 0.4–0.65% for every 1°C increase, making temperature control an important aspect of PV performance optimization. One approach that has gained increasing attention is underground cooling systems, which utilize relatively stable subsurface soil temperatures as a natural heat sink to dissipate excess heat from PV modules. This study presents a systematic literature review of underground cooling technologies for photovoltaic systems using the PRISMA methodology. The literature search was conducted using the Scopus database with the keyword "underground cooling for PV", covering publications from 2020 to 2026 and limited to English-language research articles. The selection process resulted in 11 relevant studies consisting of both experimental investigations and numerical simulations with various system configurations, including underground heat exchangers, PV/T systems integrated with geothermal loops, underground air tunnels, and hybrid cooling configurations. The review results indicate that underground cooling systems can reduce PV module temperatures by approximately 10–22°C and improve electrical efficiency by about 6–11%. Water-based systems generally provide higher heat extraction capability, whereas air-based systems offer simpler configurations with lower operational complexity. However, most studies remain limited to short-term experiments or steady-state simulations, and long-term validation of soil thermal dynamics and seasonal performance is still limited. This study provides a technical synthesis of the potential, limitations, and future research directions of underground cooling systems as a strategy to improve photovoltaic system efficiency.

title: Single-Event Effects in Commecrical Photonic Transceivers
authors: George N. Tzintzarov, Jenna Ocheltree, Gregory R. Allen, Carl Boone, Amanda Bozovich
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: Low-cost standard form-factor pluggable optical transceivers were exposed to heavy ions. Single-event functional interrupts (SEFIs) and multiple-bit upsets (MBUs) were observed with various part numbers. It was found that the transmitter and receiver optical sub-assemblies do not exhibit any SEFIs as those parts consist only of analog circuits. Furthermore, targeting the laser assembly did not yield any single-event effects (SEEs). The culprit for SEFIs on all transceivers was the microcontroller (i.e., main controlling chip) as that has I2C interface and other control logic.

title: Design and Analysis of a Low-Cost Gripper with Compliant, Tendon-Driven Continuum Fingers
authors: Avni Mahajan
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: The general-purpose deployment of object manipulators relies heavily on their ability to handle a wide variety of objects. The end-effector design plays a crucial role in determining what types of objects can be grasped, influenced by factors such as weight, size, shape, compressibility, and surface texture. These requirements underscore the potential of general-purpose grippers inspired by continuum robots, which offer externally actuated designs, lightweight structures, high compliance, and infinite degrees of freedom-traits well-suited for manipulating diverse objects. This paper presents a low-cost (under $ 20), mechanically intelligent, tendon-driven gripper featuring underactuated continuum fingers with spring backbones. The gripper has a four-fingered design with tapered fingers, each actuated by two tendons routed in opposing directions. It is easy to fabricate and requires minimal computation, planning, or perceptual input for operation. Experiments were conducted to evaluate the gripper's ability to handle objects of different sizes, shapes, textures, compressibility, and weights. The gripper demonstrated impressive performance by lifting a maximum weight of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 5 0}$</tex> grams. It also demonstrated a high success rate with everyday objects, achieving 100 % with simple objects such as plastic balls, bottles, and complex shapes like bulbs, shuttle cocks, and capsicums.
