title: UAV Swarm-Based Dynamic Differential GNSS with Collaborative Multipath Mitigation: Low-Altitude Augmentation System for Emergency Positioning
authors: Yanyan Zhao, Lilin Yan, Yibo Wu, Lang Mi, Hongwei Zhao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: GNSS differential augmentation assumes precisely known reference coordinates and controllable multipath. When emergency scenarios force reference stations onto UAV swarms, this premise collapses: multipath errors exhibit non-Gaussian heavy tails (kurtosis 5–15), biasing least-squares corrections; UAV coordinates carry 0.3–0.5 m uncertainty propagating to all users; and independent estimation in sparse formations amplifies uncertainty. The core finding is that correction-side robustification benefits all users through the “one-to-many” architecture, and probabilistic distribution fusion guarantees optimality under worst-case perturbations. A three-layer cascaded framework is constructed: the maximum correntropy criterion (MCC) replaces least-squares for clock bias estimation, with the analytical optimal kernel bandwidth derived under a Gaussian mixture multipath model and a MAD adaptive strategy designed; a precision-weighted multi-source correction distribution fusion framework is established, proved distributionally robust optimal via Wasserstein uncertainty sets; James-Stein shrinkage injects formation geometry constraints, with an analytical upper bound derived linking shrinkage gain to the geometry matrix condition number. Three-scenario vehicle experiments yield a horizontal RMSE of 0.45 m in open areas, consistent with the theoretical lower bound, an average 51.0% improvement over the best baseline (53.3% in urban canyons), and initialization under 5 minutes. Ablation experiments confirm MCC contributes 36.7% error reduction, and the cascaded gain significantly exceeds the best pairwise combination.

title: Enhancing Cell-Free SWIPT IoT Networks By Active RIS
authors: Zaixin Lu, Yao Zhang, Hao Fang, Peipei Chen, Shaowei Jiang, Jianrong Bao, Longxiang Yang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In the context of Internet of Things (IoT) networks, this work investigates an active reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-aided cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) system and establishes an integrated analytical frame-work that rigorously characterizes both the inherent double-fading effect of RIS-assisted propagation and the power-gain coupling introduced by the active RIS. A weighted sum-rate (WSR) maximization problem is then formulated under the constraints of access point (AP) power budgets, the RIS power consumption limitations, and the receiver energy harvesting requirements. To manage the resulting highly non-convex structure, the original problem is equivalently reformulated as a weighted minimum mean squared error (WMMSE) minimization problem, which enables the development of an efficient alternating optimization algorithm. At each iteration, the MMSE decoding vectors are firstly updated in closed form, while the energy signal covariance matrices, the AP information beamformers, and the active RIS reflection coefficients are subsequently optimized via semi-definite programming, successive convex approximation, and augmented Lagrangian method, respectively. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the adoption of an active RIS effectively mitigates the double-fading effect and achieves performance comparable to that of passive RIS with significantly fewer reflecting elements, while the proposed joint optimization strategy yields substantial WSR gains and further enlarges the performance gap between the optimized active and passive RIS schemes.

title: Tactile-Sensor-Embedded Treadmill for Deep Learning-Based Step-Length Measurement
authors: Sejun Park, Jaehyeon Baik, Yunho Choi, Kyung-Joong Kim, Hosu Lee
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Step-length is a widely used metric for assessing health and disease status. However, existing step-length measurement systems are often expensive or require body-attached sensors, while vision-based alternatives struggle with reliable foot tracking. To address these limitations, we propose a deep-learning-based approach for step-length estimation using a tactile-sensor-embedded treadmill. Baseline step-length was extracted from pressure data using noise filtering and a stacking algorithm. Then, stepwise features were used to refine the estimates through regression modeling, and multiple models were compared using leave-one-out cross-validation. The deep neural network model achieved the best performance, exhibiting a percentage error lower than those of previously reported approaches. These findings suggest that the proposed method offers a cost-effective, nonwearable alternative for accessible quantitative gait analysis.

title: DB-CoGNN: A Dual-Branch Synergistic Contrastive Graph Neural Network for Robust Small-Signal Stability Assessment
authors: Hailiang Li, Zhaoming Song, Shiyuan Zhang, Jinghan He
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Conventional small-signal stability assessment methods based on graph neural network face limitations in capturing the cross-scale dynamic features of power systems. Message-passing models easily suffer from over-smoothing when stacked deeply to capture long-range dependencies, while global self-attention models, despite directly modeling long-range relationships, tend to lose local details and incur high computational costs. Addressing these issues, this paper proposes a Dual-Branch Synergistic Contrastive Graph Neural Network (DB-CoGNN). The architecture designs a parallel dual-branch structure: a local encoder preserves crucial topological inductive bias to precisely capture neighborhood dynamics, while a global encoder employs a sparse multi-hop mechanism to effectively capture long-range dependencies while reducing computational complexity. These two branches dynamically synergize through a gated cross-attention mechanism, facilitating interaction between local and global information. Furthermore, an innovative multi-level contrastive learning framework acts as a self-supervised regularizer, enforcing physical consistency between the dual representations and significantly enhancing the model’s generalization ability to unseen operating conditions and topologies. Validations on the Texas 2000-bus system demonstrate that DB-CoGNN exhibits significant performance and robustness advantages over classical GNN models.

title: Compact High-Linearity and Wide-Range Permittivity Measurement Phase-Variation Sensor Based on Electronically Tunable Structure
authors: Lili Qu, Jiawei Wang, Shuie Shi, Mengyao Li, Yumiao Wang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This article presents a compact phase- variation sensor that achieves high linearity and a large phase variation at a single frequency for wide-range permittivity measurements. The sensing principle operates by translating variations in the dielectric constant of the material under test (MUT) into a measurable phase shift of the reflection coefficient at the operating frequency, which serves as the output variable. To prioritize high linearity over extreme sensitivity, the sensing part utilizes a two- cascaded different‑impedance coupled-line (TCDICL) structure, where a controllable phase slope is created by adjusting the width of the second line. The measurement range is further extended by using two varactors placed at the TCDICL terminals, allowing the sensor to cover four distinct dielectric constant ranges: 1–5, 5–10, 10–17, and 17–23 with good impedance matching and a large phase variation. The proposed design was validated using a fabricated prototype sensor, which demonstrated a maximum linearity of 0.97 and a phase variation range of 637° across the dielectric constant range of 1 to 23. Due to its exceptionally compact overall and sensing area dimensions, the proposed sensor achieves a very high figure of merit (FoM = 13255°/λ²). This FoM is defined as the ratio of maximum sensitivity to the sensing area size, expressed in terms of the squared guided wavelength λ². Experiments reveal that, compared with other highly sensitive phase-variation sensors, the proposed device is more suitable for wide-range measurement of low dielectric constant materials.

title: Digital Twin-Empowered Vehicle–Road Cooperation: A Perception and Virtualization Paradigm for Connected and Automated Vehicles
authors: Wenwei Yue, Zifan Sha, Di Zhou, Peibo Duan, Fa Zhu, Moustafa Youssef
publish: IEEE Vehicular Technology Magazine
abstract: As the crown jewel of intelligent transportation, connected and automated vehicles (CAVs) have sparked a phenomenal burst of interest to ultimately achieve the accident-free transportation system with high control capacity. However, current CAVs rely mainly on vehicle onboard equipment and edge devices for sensing and communication, and their accuracy and reliability are severely restricted by complex road environments and arbitrary human behavior. Additionally, the testing costs for intelligent vehicle–road collaboration systems and automated vehicles are exorbitant, coupled with extremely high deployment and operational complexities. Moreover, improper decision making can easily precipitate traffic accidents, thereby reducing the safety and efficiency of transportation systems. In lieu of this, we propose a digital twin (DT)-enabled vehicle–road cooperation system (VRCS) with the large-scale deployment of roadside Internet of Things (IoT) devices, which enables the ubiquitous perception and virtualization of CAVs. It also provides real-time digital mapping and intelligent control for the coordination between CAVs and smart road infrastructure. Specifically, we first propose an integrated “device-edge-DT” vehicle–road cooperation architecture to achieve the optimum utilization and digitalization of both vehicle onboard and roadside resources. Furthermore, a DT-driven sensor scheduling framework is employed to satisfy the information requirements for the VRCS. Finally, simulations show that the proposed DT-enabled vehicle–road cooperation architecture outperforms the baseline with more than 15.6% greater robustness, 53.6% higher traffic efficiency, and 8.3% better real-time responsiveness, advancing the vision of safe, efficient, and intelligent transportation.

title: Pathwise guessing in categorical time series with unbounded alphabets
authors: Jean-René Chazottes, Sandro Gallo, Daniel Y. Takahashi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: The following learning problem arises naturally in various applications: Given a finite sample from a categorical or count time series, can we learn a function of the sample that (nearly) maximizes the probability of correctly guessing the values of a given portion of the data using the values from the remaining parts? Unlike classical approaches in statistical inference, our approach avoids explicitly estimating the conditional probabilities. We propose a non-parametric guessing function with a learning rate independent of the alphabet size. Our analysis focuses on a broad class of time series models that encompasses finite-order Markov chains, some hidden Markov chains, Poisson regression for count processes, and one-dimensional Gibbs measures. We provide a margin condition that controls the rate of convergence for the risk. Additionally, we establish a minimax lower bound for the convergence rate of the risk associated with our guessing problem. This lower bound matches the upper bound achieved by our estimator up to a logarithmic factor, demonstrating its near-optimality.

title: On the role of stubbornness in Nash equilibrium problems
authors: Barbara Franci, Filippo Fabiani, Lorenzo Zino
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: We consider Nash equilibrium (NE) seeking in partial-decision information regimes where the data circulating across the agents’ communication network are affected by the agents’ stubborn behavior. Unlike traditional linear averaging approaches, the communication protocol is here based on a nonlinear, state-dependent dynamics that captures the agents’ biases, which hinders the direct application of standard techniques. Building on the explicit characterization of the convergence rate of the opinion dynamics resulting from the proposed scheme, and leveraging operator theory, we embed the communication pattern into two novel NE seeking algorithms for which we derive rigorous convergence guarantees.

title: Feature Map Compression for Split Learning in Mobile Wireless Environment: Theory and Practice
authors: Wenxuan Zhou, Zhihao Qu, Baoliu Ye, Shen-Huan Lyu, Miao Cai, Mingtao Ji, Song Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: Split Learning (SL) enables collaborative model training by distributing computation between resource constrained mobile edge devices and a central server. In this distributed framework, mobile edge devices compute the early layers of the model and transmit feature maps to the server. However, due to practical mobile wireless bandwidth limitations, these feature maps should be compressed before transmission. In this paper, we propose a lightweight Mask-Encoded Spar sification (MS) compressor that enhances Top-k sparsification with a narrow-bit mask, significantly reducing compression error while improving computational and communication efficiency. Our theoretical analyses reveal that feature map compression generally induces estimator bias in the gradients. Furthermore, we establish the relationships between compression error and convergence, as well as between the choice of cutlayer and output error. The first relationship demonstrates that reducing compression error facilitates convergence, confirming the advantage of MS, whereas the second provides theoretical guidance for determining the cutlayer, indicating that earlier layers are more sensitive to compression errors. Extensive experiments on vision and language models demonstrate that our method outperforms existing compression techniques in terms of compression error and model performance, achieving up to a 21.38× speedup in communication efficiency under wireless environments.

title: Stability and Performance Enhancement of IoT-Enabled Dual-Axis Solar Tracker Using Red Colobuses Monkey Optimization Algorithm
authors: Pasala Gopi, S. Venkateswarlu, Meisam Mahdavi
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: This study focuses on the design, development, and performance evaluation of the dual-axis solar tracker (DaST) system to maximize solar energy harvesting. The proposed system rotates solar panels along both horizontal and vertical axes, dynamically adjusting their orientation to follow the sun’s trajectory using a NodeMCU ESP8266 microcontroller. A novel Internet of Things (IoT) framework enables real-time system monitoring and performance optimization. A fully functional prototype of the IoT-enabled solar tracking system (STS) has been developed and rigorously tested to validate its performance and effectiveness in real-world applications. This research develops a transfer function model of the DaST to analyze the system dynamics under different control schemes. The stability and robustness of the DaST system with uncertainty, including the proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller, are evaluated through performance degradation curves. The coefficients of the PID controller are optimized using the red colobuses monkey optimization (RCMO) algorithm. Finally, a comparative analysis was conducted to assess the performance of the DaST system with the RCMO Algorithm (RCMOA)-based PID controller against the firefly algorithm (FA), cuckoo search algorithm (CSA), and conventional PID controllers. The results indicate that the RCMO algorithm exhibits better stability and performance across all uncertainty levels compared to the other methods.

title: Adaptive Feature Enhancement for SAR-based Ship Detection
authors: Weiyang Chen, Mao Ye
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) enables reliable maritime surveillance, yet ship detection remains challenging due to limited training data and complex ocean clutter. To address these issues, we propose a concise yet effective two-fold enhancement framework for robust SAR ship detection. First, a target-driven data enhancement strategy is introduced by extracting ship targets and synthesizing them with pure ocean backgrounds to reduce the impact of ship wakes and complex land backgrounds on ship data generation. Second, an adaptive feature enhancement mechanism based on a Mixture of Experts (MoE) is designed, where a gating network dynamically selects specialized expert sub-networks to refine multi-scale feature representations. This adaptive routing enables spatially aware feature enhancement and improves discriminability under complex maritime conditions. Extensive experiments on benchmark SAR datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving superior detection accuracy and robustness. These results validate the effectiveness of combining data-level enhancement with expert-driven feature refinement for SAR ship detection.

title: Scanpath Prediction in Panoramic Videos Via Expected Code Length Minimization
authors: Mu Li, Kanglong Fan, Kede Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Scanpath prediction in panoramic videos is a challenging task due to the spherical geometry and multimodality of the input, and the inherent uncertainty and diversity of the output. To give a complete treatment of these characteristics, we first present a simple criterion for scanpath prediction based on principles from lossy data compression. This criterion suggests minimizing the expected code length of quantized scanpaths, corresponding to fitting a discrete conditional probability model via maximum likelihood. We condition the probability model on two modalities: a viewport sequence as the deformation-reduced visual input and a set of relative past scanpaths projected onto respective viewports as the aligned path input. Furthermore, we parameterize it by a product of discretized Gaussian mixture models to capture the uncertainty and diversity of scanpaths from different humans. In doing so, the training of the probability model does not rely on the specification of “ground-truth” scanpaths for imitation learning. We also introduce a proportional–integral–derivative (PID) controller-based sampler to generate realistic human-like scanpaths from the learned probability model. Experimental results demonstrate that our method consistently produces better quantitative scanpath results in terms of prediction accuracy (by comparing to the assumed “ground-truths”) and perceptual realism (through machine discrimination) over a wide range of prediction horizons. We additionally verify the perceptual realism improvement via a formal psychophysical experiment and the generalization improvement on several unseen panoramic video datasets.

title: Improved YOLOv8-Based Small Target Defect Identification in Substations
authors: Hengzhe Shi, Zhengnan Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the challenges in equipment detection within substation environments-namely complex operational conditions and scarce defect samples-this study proposes an improved model, YOLOv8-BFT. Without altering the core structure of the YOLOv8 network, the model integrates a class-frequency-based long-tail resampling strategy at the image level, combined with a high-resolution fine-tuning mechanism to enhance detection performance for low-frequency classes and small targets. Experimental results on a real-world substation dataset comprising 17 equipment categories, with variations in lighting, weather, and viewing angles, demonstrate that YOLOv8-BFT achieves precision, recall, mAP@0.5, and mAP@0.5:0.95 scores of 0.6911, 0.5016, 0.5285, and 0.3621, respectively. These metrics outperform baseline models YOLOv5 and YOLOv8, verifying its robustness in complex operational conditions and offering a feasible solution for practical engineering applications.

title: PQV Modeling and Voltage Regulation Strategy for Distributed Photovoltaic Systems Under Reverse Power Flow Conditions
authors: Zhenhuan Xu, Liang Cao, Hongwei Zhang, Bing Fan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the extensive integration of distributed photovoltaic (PV) systems into low-voltage distribution networks, reverse power flow is likely to occur when the PV output exceeds the local load demand during the daytime, leading to voltage rise at the end nodes of the lines. To address the voltage limit violation risk under reverse power flow conditions in 380 V low-voltage distribution networks, this paper starts from the active powerreactive power-voltage (PQV) coupling relationship and establishes a PQV mathematical model under reverse power flow conditions. It analyzes the mechanism of how active and reactive power affect the voltage variation at nodes. Based on this, considering the regulation characteristics of energy storage batteries, supercapacitors, and controllable reactive power compensation devices, a PQV-based active-reactive power coordinated voltage regulation strategy is proposed. This strategy takes voltage limit violation as the trigger condition, prioritizing active power absorption to suppress the voltage rise caused by reverse power flow, and complementing it with reactive power regulation for precise voltage control. The proposed model and regulation strategy are verified through MATLAB simulation. The results show that, without reducing the PV output, this method can effectively control the node voltage within the allowable range under reverse power flow conditions during the daytime, demonstrating good engineering application prospects.

title: Novel Structure and Control Strategy for Electrolysis Hydrogen Powered by Offshore Wind Power
authors: Xiaoyan Zhang, Yiping Yang, Xurui Huang, Yujing Zhang, Ying Yang, Dingrong Ou, Ziyu Yu, Yingyuan Zhu, Bosen Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The utilization of offshore wind power is confronted with a series of problems such as expensive cables. Using offshore wind power to produce hydrogen is a solution to overcome these barriers. Furthermore, the produced green hydrogen is also an important factor in carbon neutrality goals. Without cables to be connected to the power grid, the biggest problem is the power balance and how to achieve maximum power point tracking of the wind turbine. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel system structure and establishes its model. Furthermore, a power closed-loop control strategy is proposed, which is developed based on the natural output characteristics and the rotor speed of the wind turbine. Therefore, it substantially reduces engineering complexity, which is of paramount importance in the offshore scenario. MATLAB/Simulink simulations validate the stability of the system and the effectiveness of the strategy.

title: Horizontal Transfer Strategy Based Collaborative Optimization for Power Grid Emergency Resource Scheduling
authors: Jiahua Liu, Huizhong Shi, Nan Ye, Chengyue Yang, Mingyu Ma, Chuanmin Mi, Ningna Liao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In power system emergency management, timely resource scheduling is critical for rapid recovery and loss reduction. However, traditional vertical allocation has high crossregional barriers, poor collaboration, inaccurate supply-demand matching, and delayed response, restricting post-disaster recovery efficiency. To solve these problems, this paper proposes a collaborative scheduling optimization method with horizontal allocation for grid emergency resources. It realizes accurate demand matching via cross-regional resource sharing, and optimizes the scheme using an incentive adaptive genetic algorithm (IAGA) to minimize cost and maximize efficiency. Experiments show that compared with the traditional vertical model, the proposed method reduces total emergency scheduling cost by 16.86%, improves resource utilization by 20%, and shortens response time by 18%. It significantly enhances allocation accuracy and efficiency, verifies feasibility and effectiveness in power system emergency management, and provides an innovative strategy and theoretical support for the timeliness and economy of grid emergency responses, suitable for post-disaster management of power systems and smart grids.

title: RL-Enabled LoS Blockage Avoidance for Visible Light Robot Localization
authors: Lyall Sathishkumar, Rathikadevi Mani, Saravana Ram Srinivasan, Xiao Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Modern warehouse automation requires scalable, collision-free, adaptive navigation for multi-robot fleets in dense indoor environments. Conventional SLAM or fixed-path methods suffer from high computational cost, limited flexibility, and multi-robot interference under dynamic logistics conditions. This paper presents RLORN, a Reinforcement Learning-enabled Optical Robot Navigation framework combining LED-guided positioning with multi-robot reinforcement learning in a physics-based PyBullet simulation. The system uses ceiling-mounted LED anchors for self-localization guidance, while robots learn obstacle avoidance, path planning, and delivery tasks via policy learning. We model the task of mitigating line-of-sight blockage for continuous visible light-based self-localization as a Markov Decision Process, and extend it to a multi-agent formulation to enable coordinated decision-making under shared warehouse constraints. The simulation supports ray-cast perception, velocity control, collision dynamics, and realistic physical interactions. Extensive simulations evaluate task success rate, collision frequency, convergence, and scalability. Results show stable policy convergence, fewer collisions than baseline methods, and enhanced multi-robot coordination efficiency. The proposed framework offers a scalable, simulation-based approach for LED-assisted warehouse robots and supports real-world deployment with decentralized learning.

title: Thermal Drift Correction of NV Current Sensors via Marine Predators Algorithm
authors: Ximing Gao, Dong Feng, Zhaohui Zhang, Maoheng Jing, Gulin Xia, Chun Li, Sijing He, Hongyuan Li
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: This work presents a temperature-drift compensation method for diamond NV-center quantum current sensors operating in thermally dynamic power-electronic environments. The nonlinear coupling between temperature, zero-field splitting, and ODMR resonance frequency is modeled through a parameterized correction function, whose coefficients are optimized using the Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA). The uncompensated sensor exhibits clear temperature-dependent bias despite maintaining linearity at each temperature. After MPA optimization, the reconstructed current achieves consistent accuracy across a wide temperature range, and the convergence behavior confirms the algorithm's stability and efficiency. The results demonstrate that MPA-based optimization provides an effective and lightweight approach for enhancing the thermal robustness of NV-center quantum current sensing.

title: RACER: A Caching Layer based Optimization for Accelerating Reinforcement Learning Workloads
authors: Kailash Gogineni, Yongsheng Mei, Karthikeya Gogineni, Peng Wei, Tian Lan, Guru Venkataramani
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) learns optimal decision-making policies from experiential (transition) datasets and maximizes the RL agent’s cumulative rewards. In order to improve the RL training efficiency, prior works have studied how to selectively sample certain critical transitions that ultimately lead to better policies and rewards. However, RL workloads still face significant challenges from a systems perspective, particularly when the agent iteratively accesses batches of data from transition datasets, whose growing sizes continue to challenge the memory hierarchy. This results in frequent and costly memory transfers between caches and Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM), which negatively impacts the overall training time.In this paper, we propose RACER, our novel caching layerbased optimization for RL training workloads. Firstly, recognizing that the RL agent repeatedly accesses large transition batches from growing datasets, we design a storage-cache that prioritizes critical transitions to fit within the hardware cache hierarchies. This design reduces the memory access times by sampling from a subset of critical transitions and minimizes the costly memory trips to DRAM. Second, we demonstrate how to smartly leverage key metrics (viz., temporal difference error and advantage weighting) to quantify the importance/relevance of transitions during policy optimization and to identify the transition data that would need to be spilled out of (or filled into) the caching system. We also introduce dynamic optimizations to our caching system that minimize the prospect of discarding the critical transitions. Our performance evaluation across three state-of-the-art RL algorithms, under various task environments, and on three different systems, demonstrates that RACER achieves significant optimization time improvements to the RL transition data sampling phase (a speedup of $6 \times$) and end-to-end training time (up to $2 \times$) with comparable rewards.

title: Monolithic Integration of SiC Gate Driver and VDMOS with P-Bottom Isolation and Recess Contact
authors: Chia-Lung Hung, Chuan-Han Chen, Yu-Chin Ting, Ming-Han Wang, Yi-Kai Hsiao, Hao-Chung Kuo, Bing-Yue Tsui
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: We successfully fabricated a 1.7 kV-class 4H-SiC VDMOSFET monolithically integrated with a 20 V inverter-chain gate driver (GD) featuring P-bottom isolation (PISO) and a recess contact structure. The DC and AC switching characteristics of the gate driver were demonstrated at a minimum working voltage of 12 V and a maximum undistorted working frequency of 200 kHz. The 1.7 kV VDMOS exhibited an on-state specific resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on,sp</inf>) of 3.23 mΩ-cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and a breakdown voltage (BV) of 1917 V. Finally, the high-temperature response of the integrated GD and VDMOS was also demonstrated up to 200°C. Therefore, we believe the integrated SiC GD+VDMOS can be effectively utilized in power modules to offer a smaller form factor and higher power efficiency compared to conventional solutions using a discrete Si packaged IC.

title: Infrared Small Target Detection Method Based on Progressive Active Learning and Dynamic Negative Augmentation
authors: Guofeng Cheng, Dongming Xu, Liangmin Zhong, Zhengyang Wang
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Single-frame infrared small target (SIRST) detection plays a pivotal role in infrared search and track (IRST) systems, yet existing methods suffer from high annotation costs, limited samples, and poor model generalization. To address these challenges, we propose PAL-NA, a framework that fuses Progressive Active Learning (PAL) with Negative-sample Augmentation (NA). The framework adopts a three-stage progressive training pipeline-model pre-bootstrapping, model enhancement, and model refinement-to curb over-evolution of labels under point-level supervision and stabilize training. In addition, a noise-modeling-based strategy is introduced to generate negative samples, alleviating data scarcity and class imbalance. Experiments on four public datasets, including SIRST3 and NUAA-SIRST, show that PAL-NA outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving intersection-over-union (IoU) by 3.2% to 8.7%, detection probability (Pd) by 1.1% to 3.5%, and reducing false alarm rate (Fa) by 15.3% to 28.6%.

title: Detecting Attribute Changes in Entity Records Using Cross-Attention Mechanisms
authors: Bijo Thomas, Phanindra Reddy Madduru
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Detecting attribute changes in entity records is a challenge across diverse applications including knowledge base maintenance, content management systems, database integrity validation, and temporal data consistency monitoring. While traditional approaches rely on similarity metrics or embedding based similarities, real-world scenarios often involve partial attribute modifications where changes are subtle and incomplete and requires advanced methods when early detection is crucial. We present a novel approach using cross-attention mechanisms [1], [2] for attribute change detection that directly compares token-level representations across entity records. Our method addresses a key limitation of conventional pooling-based architectures: premature aggregation of information before comparison, which causes models to emphasize semantically salient features rather than discriminative differences. We demonstrate that bidirectional cross-attention with tied projections achieves superior performance by preserving fine-grained token-level information and focusing on discriminative attributes. Evaluated on a dataset with synthetic attribute modifications, our approach achieves 0.9847 PR-AUC on high-confidence examples, outperforming CLS-based pooling by 0.6% (0.9790 baseline). On the full dataset including challenging edge cases, cross-attention achieves 0.9665 PR-AUC, a 1.4% improvement over the 0.9531 CLS baseline. We provide detailed interpretability analysis showing how cross-attention explicitly identifies differentiating tokens while down-weighting shared attributes, making it particularly effective for challenging partial modification cases.

title: A Reasoning-Based Robotic Bolt Fastening System with a Dexterous Hand
authors: Tao Tao, ChunYun Zhang, HuaiDong Zhou, FuChun Sun
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Robotic bolt fastening with dexterous hands requires effective integration of perception, reasoning and manipulation. This paper proposes a reasoning-based robotic bolt fastening system that employs a dexterous hand as the end-effector. The system integrates 6D pose estimation with CAD model topology information to accurately obtain the target bolt poses. In addition, a knowledge base for bolt fastening scenarios is constructed to provide structured prior knowledge. Based on this, the system leverages vision-language models (VLMs) to perform task reasoning and execution planning. During task execution, tactile feedback from the dexterous hand is utilized for closed-loop adjustments, improving grasp stability and manipulation reliability. Experimental results on a real robotic platform demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed system in practical bolt fastening tasks.

title: Pneumonia Detection Using a Hybrid CNN and Swin Transformer Architecture
authors: Suresh Babu P, Koduru Sasidhar Reddy, Settipalli Harsha Vardhan, Pillakadupu Poorna Sai, Pemmala Raghunath Reddy
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Pneumonia remains a serious respiratory infection for which early identification can improve clinical outcomes. Yet, reading chest radiographs is time-consuming and can vary across observers, especially where experienced radiologists are scarce. This work introduces an automated pneumonia screening model that merges a ResNet-50 convolutional feature extractor (for local patterns) with a Swin Transformer component (for global context reasoning). We evaluate the approach on the Kaggle Chest X-ray Pneumonia dataset using 5,216 training images and 624 test images, obtaining 83.65 % accuracy and 92.05 % recall, which indicates strong sensitivity for triage-oriented use. For interpretability and practical deployment, Grad-CAM heatmaps are generated and a lightweight web interface is provided. Key implementation choices and hyperparameters are documented to support reproducibility and further development.

title: BFA-YOLO: Steel Surface Defect Detection with Bi-Level Routing Attention and Frequency-Aware Feature Fusion
authors: Anpeng Jin, Zhaoming Xu
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Steel surface defect detection is a critical step in ensuring product quality and production safety. While lightweight models such as YOLO11 offer a favorable speed-accuracy balance, dense global attention at the backbone's final stage lacks content-aware focusing for spatially sparse defects, and the neck's nearest-neighbor interpolation with simple concatenation struggles to preserve high-frequency defect detail. To address these issues, we introduce Bi-level Routing Attention (BRA) and the FreqFusion module into YOLO11, forming C2BRA and FreqFFPN: the former achieves efficient global modeling via region-level routing and token-level sparse attention; the latter realizes frequency-complementary multi-scale fusion through adaptive high-pass and low-pass filtering. On the NEU-DET dataset, the proposed method achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$77.33 \% \text{mAP} {@} 0.5$</tex>, surpassing the YOLO11n baseline by 5.34 percentage points, with both parameter count <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(2.49 \mathrm{M})$</tex> and FLOPs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(6.03 \mathrm{G})$</tex> lower than the baseline <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{2. 5 8 M}, \text{6. 3 0 G})$</tex>.

title: Robotic Scanning Path Planning Based on K-Means Clustering and PSO
authors: Sheng Gao, He Jiang, Peng Xu, Bing Li
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: In order to achieve automated scanning of complex parts, this paper proposes a model-based robotic scanning path planning algorithm, which mainly consists of viewpoint planning and path planning. Viewpoint planning is implemented based on the point cloud obtained by sampling the 3D CAD model, and the evaluation of candidate viewpoints is accomplished using the principle of ray casting. The scanning path of the robotic arm is obtained through the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm using the viewpoints generated from viewpoint planning. A smooth and executable scanning trajectory is generated by applying B-spline interpolation and spherical linear interpolation. Simulation verification is conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed robotic scanning path planning method.

title: Research on Object Detection Using YOLOv5 in Haze Conditions
authors: Li Wei, Gao Jiajun
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Under clear, unobstructed conditions, models like YOLOv5 can accurately detect objects in images. However, in extreme weather such as smog, rain, or snow, detected objects are prone to being obscured or blurred, leading to a significant drop in accuracy. To enhance object detection accuracy in smoggy conditions, this paper proposes an improved YOLOv5 model incorporating the Transformer module, CMA module, and S1 attention module. The integrated YOLOv5 model achieves a 3.03% improvement in average precision and a 4.45% increase in recall rate. This substantially enhances object detection accuracy in hazy conditions, providing valuable insights for future research in this field.

title: Optimal Recloser Placement in Radial Distribution Networks Using LSTM-Based Fault Risk Pattern and Artificial Bee Colony Algorithm
authors: Muhammad Fauzan Nur, Muhammad Bachtiar Nappu, Salama Manjang, Ardiaty Arief
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Determining the best locations for reclosers in distribution networks requires a method capable of dynamically detecting fault patterns. This study presents a technique that integrates LSTM-based fault pattern predictions with the ABC optimization algorithm to determine optimal recloser placements. The main goal is to model fault risk dynamically and apply it directly to recloser placement decisions. The approach involves two stages: first, training the LSTM model on historical fault time-series data to assess risk for each feeder, indicating the vulnerability of network segments; second, using the ABC algorithm to find optimal recloser locations by minimizing a weighted objective function that includes SAIDI, SAIFI, and the LSTM-predicted fault risk. The method was tested on the IEEE 69-bus radial distribution system with three fault-pattern scenarios. Results demonstrate that combining LSTM predictions with ABC optimization provides an adaptive and effective recloser placement strategy that reduces SAIDI and SAIFI in the test cases.

title: Research on Laser Charging Technology for Aerial Networking of UAV Swarms
authors: Li-juan Kan, Ji-hui Xu, Lin Li
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Focusing on the current needs of military preparedness, continuously optimizing the construction of the unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarm combat support system, and innovating agile support modes and methods are effective ways to solve the time and distance challenges in the application of UAV swarm combat. With the increasing maturity of UAV remote charging technology, using laser remote charging to extend endurance time has become one of the research hotspots in the aviation support field. This paper focuses on the technical solution level and conducts research from four aspects: laser power transmission methods, design of on-board and platform charging equipment, dynamic docking guidance technology, and charging procedure planning. The aim is to build an efficient, reliable, and scalable aerial charging technology system to improve support efficiency and ensure the generation and effective exertion of equipment combat capability.

title: Firefighter Floor Positioning Algorithm Integrating Constrained EKF and Asymmetric Discrimination
authors: Jinglong Li, Gang Yang, Tianle Cao, Zhongrong Zhao
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: In fire environments, existing floor positioning schemes for high-rise buildings have height estimation deviations, which easily lead to floor misclassification and abrupt positioning jumps, thus failing to meet firefighters’ rescue command requirements. To address this problem, a firefighter floor positioning algorithm integrating a state- constrained extended Kalman filter (EKF) and an asymmetric discrimination interval is proposed. The algorithm employs a support vector machine (SVM) to recognize stair ascent, stair descent, and planar motion, and uses staircase landing transitions together with zero-velocity updates (ZUPT) to linearize height error and construct an initial height estimate. As the core module, a state-constrained EKF is introduced. During planar motion, a constant-height constraint is imposed and ultra-wideband (UWB) and IMU measurements are fused to correct the height error. An asymmetric discrimination mechanism is also designed that incorporates the characteristics of height error and building geometry constraints, thereby increasing error tolerance and enhancing the stability of floor positioning. Experimental tests demonstrated that, compared with conventional algorithms, the mean height error and the root-mean-square error were reduced by approximately 57% and 59%, respectively. During motion on the target floor, the average standard deviation of floor positioning error decreased by 78.8%, which effectively improved the stability of the floor positioning system.

title: A Morphology-Aware Spatial-Frequency Network for High-Precision Bearing Defect Detection
authors: Qiang Chen
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Accurate and real-time bearing surface defect detection remains challenging in intelligent manufacturing due to complex backgrounds and heterogeneous defect shapes. To address these issues, this paper proposes BSM-YOLO, an efficient defect detection framework based on spatial-frequency synergy and enhanced multi-scale feature interaction. We design a spatial-frequency synergistic backbone to jointly model morphological diversity and periodic noise characteristics, where a gradient-separated multi-shape aggregation module captures heterogeneous defect features, and a spatial-frequency adaptive attention module suppresses background interference via phase-preserving modulation. Furthermore, a refined neck architecture incorporating dynamic adaptive interaction fusion and spatial-statistical refinement is developed to alleviate cross-scale semantic discrepancies and enhance feature robustness. In addition, a hybrid geometric constraint loss is introduced to improve bounding box regression by enforcing orientation and boundary consistency, leading to more accurate localization of elongated defects. Extensive experiments on an industrial bearing dataset show that BSM-YOLO achieves an mAP@0.5 of 96.3%, and a Recall of 92.0%, outperforming the baseline and several state-of-the-art methods. With only 3.63M parameters and 11.3 GFLOPs, the proposed method demonstrates its effectiveness and practicality for real-time industrial inspection.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)

title: Design of an AI-Assisted Robotic Arm Workstation for Elderly Care
authors: Yu-Rou Tu, Shan-Xi Yang, Tsai-Yen Wei, Ying-Hsiu Hung, Yu-Chiao Tu, Shin-Chi Lai, Jeng-Dao Lee
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: As Taiwan officially enters a super-aged society in 2025, the shortage of long-term care personnel is becoming increasingly severe. How to improve care efficiency through smart technology has become an urgent issue. This study proposes an “Intelligent Long-Term Care Service Collaborative Robotic Arm System” that integrates voice recognition, AI agents, robotic arm collaboration, smart image tracking, and real-time communication platforms to assist caregivers in resident scheduling, health monitoring, identity recognition, needs communication, and interactive games. The system enables voice control through wearable devices and uses the Chat Generative Pre-trained Transformer Application Programming Interface (ChatGPT API) to convert and interpret commands. A robotic arm then performs data acquisition, reminders, and assistive operations. At the same time, Node-RED and the LINE platform provide real-time information feedback. Practical application scenarios demonstrate that this system can effectively reduce the workload of caregivers, enhance resident safety and interaction experiences, and has the potential to be expanded to smart wards, warehouse logistics, and smart agriculture environments.

title: Liv-AR: A Web-Based Augmented Reality Platform for Immersive NCERT History Education
authors: Vani E S., Atharva S. A.
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: The National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) textbooks for Classes 6–10 primarily use static 2D illustrations to depict historical monuments. While informative, these visuals limit students’ ability to grasp spatial relationships, architectural symmetry, and scale. This paper introduces Liv-AR, a zero-install, browser-based WebAR platform designed to address these limitations by transforming printed textbook-style markers into interactive 3D models of four NCERT referenced monuments: the Hampi Stone Chariot, Taj Mahal, Red Fort, and Sanchi Stupa. Built using A-Frame and AR.js, Liv-AR incorporates a persistent bottom-right information button that presents multilingual fact panels in English, Hindi, and Kannada. Hosted on Vercel, the platform operates smoothly on commonly used budget and mid-range smartphones (4–6 GB RAM), with enhanced performance on devices equipped with 6 GB RAM or higher. A pilot study with 10 students showed a statistically significant 22% improvement in knowledge retention (p < 0.05). A review of 18 studies highlights key gaps in NCERT alignment, accessibility, and multilingual support—areas directly addressed by Liv-AR. The paper presents implementation details, performance findings, user evaluation results, limitations, and potential future enhancements, including offline PWA support and integration with national platforms such as DIKSHA.

title: Decentralized Trust Establishment for Digital Credentials using Blockchain Technology
authors: G.Rajesh Chandara, Vallabhaneni Lakshmi Pujitha, Vadranam Bindu, Pasupuleti Tiru Ganesh, Kondapalli Kamal Prakash
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The demand for dependable academic credential verification systems has surged because digital education and global mobility have experienced rapid development. The existing verification system which relies on centralization leads to excessive verification times and high expenses while providing in-sufficient security against fraudulent activities and data breaches and system breakdowns. This paper presents a blockchain-based decentralized credential verification system which addresses these existing challenges through its development. The system enables authorized educational institutions to issue academic certificates as tamper-proof digital credentials recorded on a distributed ledger. The system prevents credential modifications and forgery attempts, which enables users to maintain data accuracy and establish permanent trust. Students receive distinct identification codes which enable them to securely access and distribute their credentials, while employers and universities can authenticate certificate validity without needing intermediaries. Smart contracts are used to automate credential issuance and verification processes, which leads to decreased need for human work and lower business expenses. The decentralized system design delivers better system transparency and security and system availability because it does not depend on central database systems. The system provides academic institutions with a solution that enables both system growth and interoperability to support their extensive academic operations. The experimental results from the implementation show that the developed solution achieves better verification performance while stopping unauthorized credential access. The proposed blockchain-based approach provides an academic and professional environment to secure and transparent and efficient system which replaces traditional credential verification methods.

title: Deep Learning Model for Early Lung Nodule Classification
authors: Chitra K, Nagaveni KM, Harsha DV, Princy Leona P, Charitha R, SowmyaRani M E
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer related mortality worldwide, with about 1.8 million deaths each year, mainly due to late stage diagnosis. Low dose Computed Tomography screening supports early lung nodule detection, but manual review of 200 to 400 CT slices per patient in high volume clinical settings is time consuming and error prone. This study presents a deep learning framework based on transfer learned ResNet50 for automated five class lung cancer classification from axial CT images. The pipeline includes DICOM preprocessing, lung windowing, data augmentation, U Net based lung segmentation, and classification into normal, adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and large cell carcinoma classes. Experimental results on a prepared lung CT dataset show strong and stable classification performance. The model learns discriminative visual patterns from lung tissue and nodules, enabling reliable separation of healthy and cancer affected scans. Evaluation includes class wise performance analysis, confusion matrix assessment, and training convergence analysis to measure generalization across image categories.

title: Incorporating Meteorological Data and Pesticide Usage for Crop Yield Prediction Using Machine Learning
authors: Chhatre Shital Vitthalrao, K. Shilpa, Pillutla Naveen Reddy, Rukkaya Sultana, Rapolu Sai Rani, Puram Arun
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The honest estimation of crop yield is an essential part of contemporary agricultural planning, as it allows the making of right decisions related to food security, resource management, and mitigation of risks. Traditional statistical methods have a tough time dealing with the intricate and nonlinear interactions existing among weather, agricultural inputs, and crop output. In order to overcome this constraint, the authors of this paper introduce a yield prediction system based on machine learning that not only adds meteorological and pesticide usage data to the forecast but also increases the accuracy thereof. The proposed solution handles both types of features, featuring crop and region as the categorical and average temperature, precipitation, and pesticide usage as the numerical ones. The categorical inputs are changed into the matrix form using One-Hot Encoding technique, while the numerical ones are standardized through feature scaling method. The Random Forest regression model is applied to the task of discovering non-linear relationships between the features and giving the output of quantity of yield in continuous form. The model is made available through the web-based application underpinned by Flask that allows for real-time prediction based on the user inputs. The experimental evaluation uses historical data about agriculture and climate, and is enriched by exploratory data analysis and correlation studies whose purpose is to check how the yield of a crop is affected by each of the factors. The findings prove that the model proposed is very effective in capturing the differences in yields among various crops and areas, thus providing reliable predictions. The fusing of the factors of environment and pesticide powerfully fortifies the model above that based on the input parameters alone. The system that researchers created functions as a precise agricultural decision-making tool which first enables current operations and then allows for future expansion through new data acquisition.

title: Compliant, High-Strain Capacitive Stretch Sensors for Soft Robotics and Wearable Electronics
authors: Peter S. Shan, Aaron D. Mazzeo
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: With the development of soft robotics, wearable electronics, and soft exosuits, there is a need for mechanically compliant sensors that can measure strains that are much larger than the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 1 \%$</tex> strain-to-failure of a typical metallic strain gauge. Here, we propose and demonstrate a novel silicone-elastomer stretch sensor that uses closely interdigitated microchannels filled with liquid metal to form a variable capacitor. By measuring the change in capacitance between two stretched microchannels in the elastomeric sensor, we can determine strain. These stretch sensors can be easily manufactured with soft lithography using a 3-D printed mold. They are also extremely compliant <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\simeq 0.080 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{N})$</tex>, allow for high strain to failure <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(>600 \%$</tex>), and are highly customizable for different sizes and applications. We characterize the performance of prototype sensors with 1-mm-wide channels in terms of sensitivity, linearity, and hysteresis performance. We also demonstrate some potential applications of the sensor as a glovemounted device to detect finger flexion, and as a capacitive touch sensor.

title:
An Accessible Framework for Real-Time PM
                    <sub>2.5</sub>
                    Forecasting Using Neural Networks and Gaussian Plume Modeling
authors: Henry Cantor, Ayan Dalmia, Anusha Iyer
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: PM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{P M}_{2.5}$</tex> is one of the most dangerous pollutants worldwide, linked to major health risks, with increasing danger as wildfires and global warming exacerbate pollution levels. This study takes advantage of smoke being a major carrier of PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf>, using the movement of smoke plumes to track PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf> levels in various locations. We developed three multi-layer neural networks, incorporating CNNs, LSTMs, and Sequential layers for specialized geospatial and temporal analysis. These models use complex Gaussian plume modeling and aggregated data from numerous national databases to produce accurate PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf> forecasts at specified locations 1,3, and 7 days into the future. The developed models had high levels of predictive accuracy, achieving up to 88 % accuracy and a maximum average error of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.26 \mu ~\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$</tex>, an error margin so small that it does not affect major danger classifications. To make these predictions accessible, we developed an interface that allows users to input a location and receive real-time PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf> conditions and forecasts, alongside visualizations that demonstrate wind and air quality conditions in the 12 surrounding points used to make predictions at the specified location. This UI enables individuals to take preventative measures in advance of air quality hazards for data-driven public health protection.

title: CyberChain: Enhancing Document Security with Blockchain and AI
authors: Darshana A, Shaik Faiz, Tazian Anjum A, Sirisha D M, Manthana A
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Digital documentation systems face critical challenges in authenticity verification and tamper detection. Traditional centralized approaches lack cryptographic validation and remain vulnerable to sophisticated forgery. This paper presents CyberChain, an intelligent security framework integrating blockchain-inspired hash chaining with AI-driven document classification. The system employs OCR for text extraction, transformer models for semantic categorization, and AES-256 encryption for confidential storage. Each document receives a SHA-256 cryptographic fingerprint, creating immutable verification trails. Implementation demonstrates successful classification across certificates, reports, IDs, and invoices with 87 percent confidence, while offline architecture eliminates cloud dependency and ensures data sovereignty.

title: An Edge-Guided Structural Transformer Network for SAR Time Series Land Cover Mapping
authors: Kang Ni, Jiaxing Ren, Chunyang Yuan, Zhizhong Zheng, Peng Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Real-time and accurate interpretation of time-series SAR imagery is vital for land-cover monitoring. Nevertheless, the presence of speckle noise makes it extremely challenging to distill the essential information from redundant SAR time series. Current methods not only struggle to suppress temporal-feature redundancy but also tend to overlook the structural cues along land-cover boundaries that are crucial for boosting classification performance, leading to slower inference and sub-optimal accuracy. To address these challenges, we propose Edge-Guided Structural Transformer Network (EGSTNet), a lightweight and efficient land cover mapping network for time-series SAR imagery. The edge-guided convolution (EGConv) and axial separable large kernel attention (ASLKA), based on a lightweight design, utilize a re-parameterizable multi-branch convolutional module and a large-kernel attention mechanism to capture multi-order gradient features and model global spatiotemporal characteristics along both spatial and temporal dimensions in SAR time series. Notably, an implicit semantic graph style is incorporated into implicit semantic graph Transformer (ISGT) to facilitate efficient joint modeling of spatiotemporal and structural information of land covers. Experimental results on three benchmark time-series SAR land cover mapping datasets show that EGSTNet achieves mIoU scores of 35.87%, 48.16%, and 50.27% on the Slovenia, Brandenburg S1, and Brandenburg S2 datasets, respectively, with 17.10M trainable parameters and an inference speed of 105.53 FPS.

title: Mapping AI Literacy Expectations in Technical and Professional Communication: Evidence From 137 AI-Referenced Job Postings
authors: Shijie Liu, Jianfen Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Professional Communication
abstract: Background: This exploratory study examines the emerging requirements of artificial-intelligence (AI) literacies reflected in technical and professional communication (TPC) job postings and provides empirical insights into how employers articulate these literacies in hiring practices. Literature review: We situate this study within the existing scholarship regarding job postings analysis in TPC, AI in TPC, and AI literacy. Research questions: How do employers reference AI literacy in TPC job postings? How are AI literacy dimensions distributed across the TPC job description domains? Methodology: We collected job postings published between March 1 and June 10, 2025, across ten major online recruitment platforms, focusing on US-based positions that fall into six TPC job categories that explicitly referenced AI. This process yielded 137 eligible postings for analysis. Employing a multistage and mixed-method design for data analysis, we coded AI literacies using the AI literacy framework and coded the job descriptions using the four TPC job description domains. Results: Our quantitative findings show growing integration of AI literacy into TPC roles, with uneven emphasis on specific AI literacy dimensions across TPC positions. Employers prioritize practical applications of AI tools, while ethical considerations receive little attention. Our topical modeling analysis also indicates that AI literacy is an integrative skill and functions as an embedded capability in TPC jobs. Conclusion: This study has significant implications for TPC educators. AI literacy should be taught as an integrative skill that augments human expertise. TPC programs should embed AI instruction across the curriculum while continuing to emphasize the core competencies essential to professional communication.

title: Open-Source Client Modules Supporting Time Transfer with Satellite- and Decrypted Network-Based Precise Point Positioning [Roadmap for Measurement and Applications]
authors: Runzhi Zhang, Rui Tu, Jihai Zhang, Xiaochun Lu
publish: IEEE Instrumentation & Measurement Magazine
abstract: Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) now provides global users with high-precision positioning, navigation, and timing services. Precise point positioning (PPP) technique has been developed and utilized for international atomic time link computations. However, PPP relies on high-precision satellite products to correct satellite-related errors in measurement observations output by a GNSS receiver. To support applications, precise satellite products were provided either via network transmission or through direct satellite broadcast in real-time. In recent years, network-based products, which are encrypted, internationally standardized, and transmitted in binary format, and satellite-based products, which are fragmented, customized, encoded, and transmitted in binary format, have made it more difficult for users to reliably and continuously recover them. At present, real-time open-source PPP software does not provide these two supports, which limits the application of real-time PPP, particularly for stable and continuous real-time PPP time transfer research and implementation. In this study, two supporting software packages have been developed and released as open source, designed to be embedded into mainstream open-source C/C++ PPP software. RTStreamHub is a support package for receiving and processing encrypted network-based products, designed to ensure reliable, real-time reception of securely transmitted satellite binary products. HASPPP is a dedicated software for Galileo satellite-based PPP, providing integrated support for recovering complex satellite-based products and implementing PPP, while also offering interfaces for seamless integration into mainstream C/C++ software for PPP time transfer applications. The real-time PPP time transfer precision of both network- and satellite-based products was analyzed using the PPP model embedded within the software HASPPP. Taking fiber-optic link results as a reference, the PPP time transfer precision is 125.9 ps based on network-based satellite products and 133.8 ps based on Galileo satellite-based products. The performance of these software packages has been validated, providing support modules for mainstream C/C++ PPP software.

title: TwinRL-Onco: A World Model-Empowered Digital Twin Framework with Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Venetoclax Resistance Trajectory Prediction and Adaptive Therapy Optimization in Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia
authors: Zi Wang, Chenghao Ge, Yufan Hu, Destin Bai, Wei Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL) presents considerable therapeutic obstacles due to the development of treatment-resistant disease, especially with BCL-2 inhibitors like venetoclax, and is among the most challenging haematological malignancies to treat. A critical unmet computational need is the ability to predict patient-specific treatment resistance trajectories and to develop optimal adaptive therapy schedules based on a patient's history and experience with treatments. To address these issues, we have developed TwinRL-Onco, a novel computational framework that fuses digital twin technology, driven by world model-based simulations, with hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL), to predict in silico treatment response and optimize the adaptive therapy process. TwinRL-Onco consists of three synergistic components: (1) a Variational Recurrent State-Space Model (VRSSM); (2) a hierarchical policy structure with two levels of control; and (3) a resistance trajectory predictor that uses Monte Carlo rollouts generated from the world model to simulate treatment response. TwinRL-Onco achieved an AUROC of 0.917 for 12-month simulated prediction of progression, an improvement of 4.6 percentage points over the DreamerV3 algorithm and 7.0 percentage points over the LSTM-Traj algorithm. TwinRL-Onco is an emphasis on an artificial intelligence-based online environment for hypothesis generation; all on-site metrics indicate inventory-only consistency and need actual clinical validation before any conclusions regarding clinical prediction.

title: LGIEA: A Local-Global Information Guided Evolutionary Algorithm for Influence Maximization
authors: Haipeng Yang, Chuang Liu, Xinxiang Xu, Yuanyuan Ge, Yao Lu, Lei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The influence maximization (IM) problem has attracted the attention of many researchers. However, most of the existing algorithms do not fully use the comprehensive information (local and global information) of the network, which causes the performance of these algorithms to decrease on some networks. To this end, this paper proposes a local-global information guided evolutionary algorithm (LGIEA) for IM problem in social networks. In LGIEA, the local-global information of the network is fully utilized to find the nodes with high potential influence during the evolutionary optimization. Specifically, local information pertains to the neighborhood surrounding the node, whereas global information is associated with the shell value of the node. By leveraging the aforementioned local-global information, we propose an evolutionary algorithm that constructs a novel local-global metric and employs a search space reduction strategy to guide population evolution. Additionally, we design strategies for initialization, crossover and mutation to improve the evolutionary process. Experimental results on 12 real-world networks and four simulated networks verify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed LGIEA in comparison with nine representative baseline algorithms and four variants of LGIEA.

title: SINdex: Semantic INconsistency Index for Hallucination Detection in LLMs
authors: Samir Abdaljalil, Parichit Sharma, Erchin Serpedin, Rachad Atat, Hasan Kurban
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across diverse domains, yet they are prone to generating factually incorrect outputs-commonly known as “hallucinations.” Among existing mitigation strategies, uncertainty-based methods are particularly attractive due to their ease of implementation, independence from external data, and compatibility with standard LLMs. In this work, we introduce a novel and scalable uncertainty-based semantic clustering framework for automated hallucination detection. Our approach leverages sentence embeddings and hierarchical clustering alongside a newly proposed inconsistency measure, SINdex, to yield more homogeneous clusters and more accurate detection of hallucination phenomena across various LLMs. Evaluations on prominent open- and closed-book QA datasets demonstrate that our method achieves AUROC improvements of up to 9.3% over state-of-the-art techniques. Extensive ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of each component in our framework.

title: Key-Driven Identity Protection and Secure Traceability for Face-Swapping
authors: Miaomiao Wang, Sheng Li, Xinpeng Zhang, Guorui Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Face swapping replaces the identity of a target face with that of a source face while retaining non-identity attributes, and is widely used in privacy protection scenarios. However, removing identity information makes it difficult to support sub sequent recognition tasks. Although active tracing methods can determine content ownership, techniques such as watermarking rely on external data binding, which cannot establish identity associations in the feature space nor support authorization controlled identity verification. To address this issue, we propose a key-driven Identity Protection and Secure Traceability (IPST) framework for face-swapped images, which enables identity bind ing and controlled verification without exposing visual identity. The proposed method operates as a plug-in applied to face swapping results, introducing an implicit identity association in the feature space so that the target identity participates in the generation process while remaining visually imperceptible. Specifically, IPST consists of three stages. First, an identity transfer stage aligns the generated image with the target identity in the recognition space via feature-guided perturbations. Second, an identity hiding stage performs key-controlled latent space editing to map the identity into a concealed state, ensuring that the published image cannot be directly recognized. Finally, an identity recovery stage reconstructs the target identity under authorized conditions through inverse latent manipulation, enabling identity verification using face recognition models. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves stable identity binding while preserving visual quality, and shows strong robustness against unauthorized recovery. Moreover, the framework is model-agnostic and can be seamlessly integrated with existing face swapping systems, making it suitable for practical deployment.

title: Influence of geometry and polarization parameters on piezoelectric response of polypropylene piezoelectrets with an arch structure
authors: Zehai Ruan, Zhiming Shi, Xiaoqing Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Flexible piezoelectret sensors have significant advantages in the application of electronic skin to capture dynamic mechanical signals, showing promise in robotic perception. In this study, polypropylene (PP) piezoelectret films with an arched air-filled channel structure were designed and systematically investigated by theoretical analysis, simulation calculation and experimental verification. The results demonstrate that the piezoelectric properties of such piezoelectrets can be predicted via a capacitance energy method, and the films exhibit significant anisotropy in their piezoelectric response and excellent shape adaptability. Specifically, for piezoelectret film samples constructed from two 38 μm-thick PP electret films, with an arch height (apex-to-base distance) of 200 μm and a cell width of 2 mm, the piezoelectric d33 coefficient attains its maximum value when the surface potentials of the two films are set to ±1176 V, respectively. The anisotropic piezoelectric response in the films was successfully leveraged for multi-dimensional force sensing. When the applied force (~82 mN) slides along different in-plane directions, the sample yields markedly distinct piezoelectric responses. The motion perpendicular to the arched air-channel axis generates a periodic charge signal (~40 pC), while the motion parallel to the arched air-channel axis almost don’t generate discernible signal, manifesting a pronounced anisotropy. Therefore, taking into account the distance between the channels, the device can identify direction and speed of force sliding on it.

title: Beyond Latency: A System-Level Characterization of MPC and FHE for PPML
authors: Pengzhi Huang, Kiwan Maeng, G. Edward Suh
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Privacy protection has become an increasing concern in modern machine learning applications. Privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML) has attracted growing research attention, with approaches such as secure multiparty computation (MPC) and fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) being actively explored. However, existing evaluations of these approaches have frequently been done on a narrow, fragmented setup and have only focused on a specific performance metric, such as the online inference latency of a specific batch size. From the existing reports, it is hard to compare different approaches, especially when considering other metrics like energy/cost or broader system setups (various hyperparameters, offline overheads, future hardware/network configurations, etc.). We present a unified characterization of three popular approaches—two variants of MPC based on arithmetic/binary sharing conversion and function secret sharing, and FHE—on their performance and cost in performing privacy-preserving inference on multiple CNN and Transformer models. We study a range of LAN and WAN environments, model sizes, batch sizes, and input sequence lengths. We evaluate not only the performance but also the energy consumption and monetary cost of deployment under a realistic scenario, taking into account their offline and online computation/communication overheads. We provide empirical guidance for selecting, optimizing, and deploying these privacy-preserving compute paradigms, and outline how evolving hardware and network trends are likely to shift trade-offs between the two MPC schemes and FHE. This work provides system-level insights for researchers and practitioners who seek to understand or accelerate PPML workloads.

title: Comparison of HT and MT Intermittent Gait Patterns for a Self-Propelled Undulatory Hydrofoil
authors: Shuihui Li, Ye Chen
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Intermittent propulsion is a promising locomotion strategy for biomimetic underwater robots, as it offers potential advantages in speed regulation, energy utilization, and motion stability. In this study, a comparative investigation is carried out on two typical intermittent gait patterns, namely the half-tail-beat (HT) mode and the multiple-tail-beat (MT) mode, based on a self-propelled undulatory hydrofoil model. A two-dimensional computational fluid dynamics framework is employed to establish the coupled process of prescribed body undulation, hydrodynamic force generation, and self-propelled forward motion. To examine the influence of gait pattern on robotic swimming performance, the HT and MT modes are systematically compared over a duty-cycle range from 0.2 to 1.0. The mean swimming speed, cost of transport (COT), and relative velocity fluctuation ratio are adopted to characterize propulsion capability, energy expenditure, and motion stability, respectively. The results show that the two modes can achieve nearly identical mean swimming speeds over the entire duty-cycle range, but exhibit clear differences in energy cost and stability. Compared with the HT mode, the MT mode consistently produces a slightly higher COT and a larger fluctuation ratio, and this difference becomes more pronounced at smaller duty cycles. For the same gait pattern, both the mean swimming speed and COT decrease with increasing duty cycle, while COT is more sensitive to duty-cycle variation. This indicates that stronger intermittency helps enhance swimming speed, but also leads to a higher energetic cost.
