title: A Study on the Mechanism and Coordinated Control of the Conflict Between Excessive Short-Circuit Current and Insufficient Short-Circuit Ratio at the “Desert, Gobi, and Wasteland” New Energy Base
authors: Pengfei Song, Wenhui Zhai, Minghui Huang, Xiaoyu Tian, Wenchao Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Desert, Gobi, and wasteland regions possess extremely abundant wind and solar resources, offering immense potential for renewable energy generation. Once these “desert, Gobi, and wasteland” new energy bases are connected to the power grid, they will increase the short-circuit current of nearby 750 kV substations to levels approaching the circuit-breaking capacity of the switches. At the same time, on the new energy side, issues such as insufficient short-circuit ratio due to low short-circuit capacity and excessive transient voltage rise following AC and DC faults coexist, necessitating the deployment of a large number of distributed phase-shifting reactors. Based on the current contradiction between excessive short-circuit currents and insufficient short-circuit ratios in the “desert, Gobi, and wasteland” new energy bases, this paper first investigates typical power source organization schemes for such bases in the Northwest Power Grid. It analyzes the mechanisms by which voltage-source and current-source equipment enhance shortcircuit currents, concluding that the base provides approximately 20 kA of short-circuit current to the system. Based on the initial current level on the system side, the paper provides recommendations for the selection of constant-voltage and flexible DC systems; Next, the study analyzes and concludes that the high impedance resulting from step-up transformation from the renewable energy generation units to the collection stations is the fundamental cause of low short-circuit capacity at the grid connection points. Based on the two primary challenges faced by renewable energy sites-insufficient short-circuit ratio and transient overvoltage-the paper proposes rapid estimation methods and distributed phase-shifting reactor configuration schemes, respectively; Finally, using a specific operational DC transmission line in the Northwest Power Grid as a case study, the accuracy of the proposed method is verified, and safety assessments of short-circuit current, short-circuit ratio, and transient overvoltage are conducted for DC projects under the 15th Five-Year Plan.

title: Pushing physical limits and uncovering motion templates of spine-based quadruped locomotion via reinforcement learning
authors: Zhenshan Bing, Yulong Xiao, Yuhong Huang, Qing Shi, Long Cheng, Biao Hu, Gang Chen, Yang Gao, Fuchun Sun, Kai Huang, Alois Knoll
publish: IEEE Transactions on Robotics
abstract: Flexible spines are critical to the remarkable agility and speed of animals. Translating this biological advantage to quadruped robots presents a significant control challenge, particularly in coordinating the spine and limbs for maximal velocity. In this work, we utilize reinforcement learning (RL) to develop high-speed locomotion for a bioinspired mouse robot with a lateral flexible spine. The resulting controller achieves motor performance that demonstrably surpasses non-spined and model-based methods. More importantly, our analysis reveals the principles behind this performance: the emergence of two distinct motion templates. For high-speed walking, the robot learns a “whip-like” spinal oscillation to increase leg swing frequency, while for agile turning, it adopts a dynamic “bend-and-straighten” pattern. These findings demonstrate the capability of RL to not only generate high-performance controllers but also to produce emergent strategies that, upon analysis, reveal underlying principles of high-speed, spine-driven locomotion.

title: EdgeCaps: Pruned Capsule Networks for RF Vital Sign Measurement in Real-time
authors: Prisila Ishabakaki, Michael Mollel, Hira Hameed, Hasan Abbas, Muhammad Imran, Qammer H. Abbasi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Radio frequency (RF) sensing enables unobtrusive, non-contact monitoring of vital signs and has been increasingly enhanced by deep neural networks (DNNs), which excel at extracting discriminative features from complex RF signal representations. These advances have significantly improved the accuracy and robustness of RF-based health monitoring; nevertheless, state-of-the-art DNN models remain computationally intensive and unsuitable for deployment on low-cost, resource-constrained edge devices. As a result, most existing RF sensing systems offload data to high-performance platforms, leaving real-time embedded deployment underexplored. We propose EdgeCaps, a framework that compresses a high-capacity Capsule Network (CapsNet) into a compact student model optimized for edge deployment through structured pruning and knowledge distillation. EdgeCaps transfers knowledge from a 33.7 M-parameter teacher to a 1.59M-parameter student, achieving 95.5% accuracy–within two percentage points of the teacher, while reducing parameters and computational complexity by 95%. We validate the system through continuous respiration monitoring on a Raspberry Pi 3, where the student model achieves mean inference latency below 0.3 s, RAM usage of 18%, and core temperature under 44°C, compared with the teacher’s 0.4–0.5 s latency, 22% RAM usage, and 51°C. These results demonstrate that EdgeCaps enables real-time, energy-efficient, and thermally stable RF-based vital sign monitoring directly on embedded devices. By combining CapsNet’s representational power with model compression and knowledge distillation, this work establishes a novel pathway toward scalable, low-cost, and intelligent health monitoring systems at the edge.

title: Investigation on the Equilibrium Law and Equilibrium Coefficient Model of Methanol in Oil-Paper Insulation of Transformer
authors: Song Cheng, Hangxu Li, Wei Sun, Yun Chen, Wei Lu, Liang Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The equilibrium behavior of methanol (MeOH) between the oil and paper phases plays a critical role in evaluating the aging state of oil–paper insulation. Existing studies generally assume a constant oil–paper equilibrium coefficient for MeOH; however, this assumption neglects the effects of insulation paper aging on its microstructure and chemical properties. In this study, eight insulation paper samples with different aging degrees were prepared to investigate the variation in MeOH equilibrium behavior. Experimental results show that aging significantly alters the paper microstructure and reduces hydroxyl group content, leading to a substantial reduction in MeOH adsorption capacity. Specifically, the adsorption capacity of aged paper decreases by up to 90.22% compared with that of new paper. Based on these findings, a variable equilibrium coefficient model dependent on the degree of polymerization (DP) is proposed. Furthermore, thermal aging experiments demonstrate that when the DP decreases to approximately 460, the total MeOH concentration declines due to esterification reactions. These results demonstrate the proposed model and provide improved support for accurate assessment of oil–paper insulation aging.

title: Hypersharpening Using Inter-cluster Sample Equalization and Intra-cluster Low-rank Constraints
authors: Jiaxun Li, Xiaolin Han, Huan Zhang, Weidong Sun
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Hyperspectral remote sensing plays an irreplaceable role in fine earth observation tasks, where the complete preservation of fine and rare spectral features is the core premise for subsequent quantitative applications. Hypersharpening, which enhances the spatial resolution of low-spatial-resolution hyperspectral (LH) images using the corresponding panchromatic (Pan) image over the same scene, is a critical technique to bridge the inherent spatial-spectral tradeoff of hyperspectral imaging. Dictionary learning based hypersharpening is a powerful way to achieve this goal by optimizing an objective that minimizes the sum of representation errors for all pixels. However, it may lead to larger fusion errors for small-sized clusters, which is not conducive to subsequent applications, such as the small and weak spectral target detection or classification. Faced with this issue, a Sample Equalized Hypersharpening method is proposed in this letter. Specifically, an inter-cluster sample equalization model is proposed to balance the number of spectral samples across clusters. Then, a subpixel-shift decomposition strategy is introduced to generate successive equalized subpixel-shifted sub-Pan images consistent with the LH resolution, and to transform the hypersharpening problem into a series fusion problems between the above sub-PAN and LH images. Finally, a joint optimization process, driven by spatial continuity and spectral similarity, is applied under intra-cluster low-rank constraints, to accurately extract the spectral information from the spectral dictionary within the LH image and the spatial information from its coefficient matrix within the LH and Pan images. Extensive experiments against related leading-edge methods indicate that, our proposed method can reconstruct high-spatial-resolution hyperspectral images with superior performance. Notably, for small-sized clusters, it achieves 0.89dB higher peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) 0.80° and lower spectral angle mapper (SAM), effectively preserving the rare fine spectral features of small targets.

title: A High-Gain Loop-Shaping-Based Real-Time Iterative Compensation Control Applied to Ultraprecision Wafer Stages
authors: Ze Wang, Peng Fang, Taotao Chen, Wenxin Qi, Min Li, Chuxiong Hu, Yu Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: In the semiconductor industry, lithography machines require improved throughput and precision under high dynamic motion conditions, where the accuracy of the scanning stage directly impacts lithography quality. Real-time iterative compensation (RIC) offers significant potential for reducing tracking errors due to its high control precision and online iteration capabilities. However, traditional RIC methods are highly sensitive to high-frequency components due to their online nature, making it challenging to properly adjust control loop gains and achieve higher precision. Additionally, for the fourth-order trajectories commonly used in semiconductor applications, different trajectory phases have distinct frequency characteristics, and the conventional RIC method employs a single feedforward strategy, limiting its control performance. To address these challenges, this article proposes a high-gain loop-shaping-based real-time iterative compensation (HGLS-RIC) method with time-varying feedforward parameters. This method utilizes a model-injection feedforward framework and enhances control gain through a loop shaping structure, effectively avoiding high-frequency errors. Furthermore, by setting appropriate real-time compensation parameters for the constant-velocity and acceleration/deceleration phases of the trajectory, compensation accuracy is further improved. Experiments on a gantry-type linear motor motion platform, across three scenarios, demonstrate the high control precision and robustness of the proposed online method.

title: Fractional and Fractal Theory for Progressively Scaled Pharmacokinetic Compartment Models with Anomalous Diffusion
authors: K B Shadiya Nasrin, J. Kokila, Yong-Ki Ma
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Accurate characterization of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination (ADME) remains a fundamental challenge in pharmacokinetics, as conventional integer-order compartment models often neglect memory effects and anomalous diffusion inherent in biological systems. To overcome this, we develop and analyze fractional-order oral and intravenous pharmacokinetic models with exact solutions in terms of Mittag-Leffler functions that reflect increasing physiological complexity. In addition, parameter estimation is carried out to quantify the underlying kinetic processes and validate the proposed formulations using experimental data. Numerical results show improved fitting of drug concentration profiles. The other part of this study focuses on the application of fractal kinetics to the aforementioned models. The numerical analysis and parameter estimation highlight the enhanced flexibility and descriptive power of fractal approaches, providing a generalized framework for optimizing drug dosing and therapeutic strategies.

title: Orchestrating Data Collection and Computation in Green IoT Networks
authors: Junfei Zhan, Tengjiao He, Kwan-Wu Chin, Benyu Chen, Fei Song
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Future Internet of things (IoT) networks will host applications that involve data collection and computation tasks on one or more servers. To this end, this paper proposes the first mixed integer linear program (MILP) to schedule and embed applications on energy harvesting nodes, where it optimizes (i) the sampling time of devices, (ii) whether to run an application, and (iii) the energy usage of devices, gateways and servers. To ensure applications are run often, we adopt the maximum age of service (AoS) metric, and set the MILP’s objective to minimize the maximum AoS or min-max AoS of applications. This paper also proposes two novel solutions: (i) a receding horizon control (RHC) based method, and (ii) a solution that greedily embeds applications according to their AoS. The results show that the min-max AoS of RHC and greedy approach is respectively 1.07x and 1.13x higher than MILP.

title: Critically Damped Output-Feedback Positioning System for PMSM Servo Drives With Model-Independent Observer and Disturbance Observer via Pole-Zero Cancellation
authors: Seok-Kyoon Kim, Chao Xu, Choon Ki Ahn
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This study develops a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{q}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-axis current loop-free positioning technique for permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs), integrating a specially structured proportional-integral (PI) controller, a model-independent cascade observer, and a fourth-order disturbance observer (FO-DOB). The method effectively handles PMSM parameter and load uncertainties and remains practical for industrial use. The core contribution lies in applying pole-zero cancellation (PZC) to both estimation and control loops: 1) the tailored gains of the observers induce PZC, yielding first-order estimation error dynamics independent of the PMSM model; 2) the filtered position output by the observer drives a FO-DOB to achieve triply-damped disturbance estimation; and 3) the combined observer and FO-DOB construct the PI-type controller, whose gain structure assigns a critically damped closed-loop response through PZC. Experimental validation is conducted on a 1-kW PMSM dynamometer.

title: Fast Robust Dual Dynamic Programming for Multi-Stage Robust Intra-Day Microgrid Scheduling
authors: Zhilu Liu, Jianfeng Zheng, Jinpei Lu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Microgrid (MG) plays a vital role in integrating renewable energy and improving local energy management. However, the presence of renewable generation and energy storage introduces both uncertainty and inter-temporal coupling into the MG's operation. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multi-stage robust optimization model for intra-day MG scheduling. The proposed framework captures the sequential decision-making process and incorporates the non-anticipativity property, ensuring that decisions at each stage rely only on currently available information. To efficiently solve the problem, a fast robust dual dynamic programming algorithm (FRDDP) is employed. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Research on Transmission Line Image Monitoring Technology Based on Ultra-High-Pixel Imaging
authors: Minghao Zhang, Mengxuan Li, Peng Li, Bin Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Current transmission line monitoring devices generally adopt CMOS imaging modules with a resolution not exceeding <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 6}$</tex> megapixels. Limited by lens performance parameters, they can only realize condition monitoring and inspection of transmission line corridors via visual monitoring. At a typical span distance of 300 meters, it is difficult to accurately identify defects on the opposite tower body, such as vibration damper slippage and insulator damage. This research takes the fully domestic, independently controllable Vertical Charge Transfer Imaging Device (VPS) as the core, focusing on the application of hundred-megapixel camera modules. Through chip integration, lens design, device integration and field tests, a novel intelligent image monitoring device for transmission lines is developed. It achieves 1 cm resolution at a distance of 300 meters, and innovatively realizes tower body condition inspection based on on-tower image monitoring.

title: Proactive Rolling Optimization-Based Scheduling Method for Off-Grid Flexible Synthetic Ammonia System
authors: Jiaqing Zhou, Ziyue Pan, Zhe Wu, Yufei Pan, Wenjie Huang, Muyang Li
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Off-grid scalable synthetic ammonia system is a potential solution that can be used to curb the irregularities of the production of renewable energy sources to help foster the greater use of renewable energy. This paper aims at streamlining the operational plan of such a system, by suggesting a preemptive rolling optimization-based scheduling plan. The methodology uses hourly outputs and consumption rates of wind power, photovoltaics, hydrogen production, hydrogen storage, energy storage and synthetic ammonia as variables of the decision. The optimization problem is solved by using a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) algorithm and the constraints of equipment and minimizing electricity curtailment and maximizing the production of synthetic ammonia. The model is tested with the real data on the wind and solar within a typical week in one of the regions in Northeast China which shows that it is adaptable. The offered strategy allows optimizing the system planning on the level of intelligence, thus offering a solid technical support to the massive implementation of renewable energy and the green transformation of the chemical industry.

title: Power Optimization Allocation Strategy for Wind-Storage Frequency Regulation Considering Stability and Economic Efficiency
authors: Zhixuan Zhao, Jiajun Ma, Qiancun Chen, Ning Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy reduces system inertia and exacerbates frequency fluctuations. Traditional frequency regulation strategies often overlook economic costs, making it difficult to coordinate stability with efficiency. To address this, this paper proposes a power optimization allocation strategy for wind-storage frequency regulation. Frequency regulation allocation coefficients are introduced to transform the direct optimization of regulation outputs into a coupled “coefficient-power” relationship, thereby reducing model complexity. An improved whale optimization algorithm incorporating nonlinear convergence factors, adaptive weights, and Levy flight is developed to enhance adaptability and convergence. Simulations under step/continuous load disturbances demonstrate that the proposed strategy effectively improves system frequency regulation capability while reducing costs, with the improved algorithm exhibiting faster convergence and stronger adaptability.

title: Hierarchical Coordinated Control of Source-Grid-Load-Storage in Highly Resilient Power Grids
authors: Xuejiao Jiang, Lei Zhong, Jingxu Yang, Lei Gao, Zheng Fu, Yansong Sun, Lukun Zeng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: The integration of large-scale renewable energy sources (RESs) and the widespread application of power electronic devices have posed significant challenges to the resilience and stability of modern power systems. To address these issues, this paper proposes a hierarchical coordinated control for source-grid-load-storage (SGLS) systems in high-resilience grids. Within this framework, a dual-layer optimization model is established. The source and grid layers are optimized to minimize total operational costs while proactively incorporating a resilience margin factor to enhance system adaptability, and the load and storage layers focus on mitigating power fluctuations. A comprehensive set of technical constraints-including power balance, nodal voltage limits, state of charge, and stochastic output limits for wind and photovoltaic power-is incorporated to ensure system security. The model is solved using an improved Particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm to derive the optimal SGLS dispatch strategy. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method improves nodal power flow balance, achieves rapid voltage recovery under fault conditions, and enhances computational efficiency by over 57 % compared to conventional methods.

title: A Continuous Wavelet Transform Framework with Frequency-Enhanced Convolutional Attention for Bearing Fault Classification
authors: Yang Wang, Xinzhe Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Motor bearing fault classification is a critical component of intelligent operation and maintenance for industrial equipment. Traditional methods rely on handcrafted features and shallow models, leading to insufficient utilization of frequency-domain information and inherent limitations that restrict classification accuracy. This paper proposes a fault classification method based on continuous wavelet transform (CWT) and a frequency-domain enhanced channel attention convolutional neural network (FECAM-CNN). Specifically, CWT is employed to obtain time-frequency representations of the signals; discrete cosine transform (DCT) and channel attention mechanisms are then used to enhance key frequency-domain features. The extracted features are subsequently fed into a least squares support vector machine (LSSVM) optimized by ISABO for classification. Experimental results on the Jiangnan University bearing dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a classification accuracy of 98.12% under complex operating conditions.

title: BidHub: A Flask-MongoDB based Real-Time Auction Platform
authors: Rishab Ramesh Nair, Ayush John Daniel, Hari Haran K, Gurupriya M
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: This paper presents BidHub, a web-based auction platform using Flask and MongoDB that enables secure bidding between buyers and sellers. The system provides automated auction management with user authentication via Flask-Session, MongoDB-based data persistence, and automated winner determination. Images are stored as binary data with base64 encoding. The platform demonstrates successful integration of lightweight web frameworks with NoSQL databases for operational auction systems accessible to small businesses without complex infrastructure requirements.

title: Vision-Energy Coupled Robotic Dry Ice Cleaning with Adaptive Trajectory Planning for Runway Prism Lights
authors: Kezheng Sun, Yongyong Sun, Wu Yan, Yangmin Ou, Zhaoyang Liao
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Embedded runway lights, particularly centerline and touchdown zone lights, are critical for aircraft safety during landing and takeoff. However, the recurring accumulation of aircraft tire rubber on prism surfaces significantly degrades their optical performance. Traditional manual cleaning is laborintensive and inefficient, while existing automated systems often employ fixed-parameter cleaning, leading to resource waste and suboptimal utilization of narrow maintenance windows. This paper proposes an intelligent, vision-guided dry ice cleaning system that integrates a six-axis robotic arm with localized contamination assessment. We develop a Threshold-based Area Ratio (TAR) model to quantitatively score the cleanliness of vertically partitioned prism zones. Furthermore, an Effective Energy Flux Model (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E_{\text {eff }}$</tex>) is established to bridge the gap between visual scores and physical cleaning intensity. Based on this, a Loop Index Compensation (LIC) algorithm is implemented for adaptive trajectory planning, enabling a “clean-on-demand” workflow. Experimental results using a ColdJet PCS 60 system demonstrate that the proposed method restores prism cleanliness to over 0.75 while reducing dry ice consumption by approximately 25 % and shortening operational time by up to 44 % compared to conventional fixed-parameter methods.

title: HOTP - High-conf Only Taken Predictor for Efficient Superscalar Multi-level Branch Predictor
authors: Bhawandeep Singh Harsh, Jose Renau
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Branch prediction in superscalar processors faces a fundamental trade-off between prediction latency and accuracy. Ideally, a designer would like a single, very large predictor that can make multiple high-quality predictions in one cycle, but such a design only works at low frequency. Traditional multi-level or override predictors provide fast restart after mispredictions but sacrifice accuracy and effective capacity due to redundancy between the slow and fast predictors. Ahead predictors tolerate prediction latency by using older history as the predictor index. The reduced history affects accuracy, and the large predictor affects restart latency. This latency–accuracy tension limits performance in wide-issue superscalar designs. We propose a biased overriding multi-level hierarchy with three predictor levels (L0, L1, L2). L0 uses a High-Confidence-Only Taken (HOTP) predictor that only predicts high-confidence taken control-flow instructions. L1 and L2 are TAGE-based predictors sharing a bimodal table with biased updates. This design achieves fast 1-cycle restart for common branches while maintaining high accuracy for difficult predictions. Our evaluation shows that HOTP $+\mathrm{L} 1+\mathrm{L} 2$ achieves $3.4 \%$ higher IPC than TAHEAD and $3.2 \%$ higher than a traditional 2bit $+\mathrm{L} 1+\mathrm{L} 2 \mathrm{nb}$ hierarchy, demonstrating that properly designed multi-level hierarchies can surpass continuous-prediction approaches. For superscalar implementations requiring multiple predictions per cycle, we size a superscalar TAGE variant (BATAGE) using Optuna-based search with varying table sizes and aspect ratios. Combining HOTP, biased training, and this optimally sized predictor yields $3.7 \%$ IPC improvement over traditional 3-level predictors (2bit+L1+L2nb), offering concrete design options for modern open-source superscalar cores.

title: Broad-Band Nolen Beamforming Matrix Inspired by Groove Gap Waveguide Technology for Multi-Beam Antenna Arrays in mmWave Satellite-Oriented Space Multiplexing Systems
authors: Mohammad Alibakhshikenari, Bal Virdee, Yazeed Mohammad Qasaymeh, Ali Zidour, Muhammad Akmal Chaudhary, Chan H. See, Patrizia Livreri, Nisar Ahmad Abbasi, Mohamadariff Othman, Takfarinas Saber, Ernesto Limiti
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: This work presents the design and experimental validation of an integrated single-layer 4×4 Nolen beamforming matrix (NBM) implemented in groove gap waveguide (GGW) technology as an enabling front-end for multi-beam satellite gateway systems. The matrix is designed for operation within the 30–40 GHz upper Ka-band region, with optimal impedance matching and beamforming performance centered around 38 GHz, offering low-loss planar routing suitable for compact mmWave front-end architectures. The network combines broadband H-plane directional couplers (3.04, 4.77, and 6.0 dB) with fundamental and delay-compensation phase shifters to equalize amplitudes and maintain progressive phase. A WR-28-compatible input-feeding transition enables practical interfacing without disturbing the internal phase states, and a linear 4×4 slot-array antenna is integrated to demonstrate wide-angle multi-beam radiation. In contrast to prior Nolen matrices, including recent GGW implementations, this work introduces a fully single-layer GGW realization with parallel routing and built-in delay compensation, achieving phase error within (±10°), near-equalized amplitudes, and simplified fabrication within a compact footprint. In particular, compared to the recent GGW-based Nolen matrices, which employ the multilayer configurations and report only simulation results, the proposed work demonstrates the first experimentally validated single-layer GGW Nolen matrix with integrated feeding transition and radiating array. Measurements confirm return loss better than −15 dB, port-to-port isolation exceeding 15 dB, amplitude imbalance within ±1.2 dB (around 6 dB nominal output), and phase error within ±10° with stable phase progression around 38 GHz.

title: A Novel Method to Evaluate Interfacial Pressure in Cable Joints using Dynamic Mechanical Analysis
authors: Darshan Prakash Borthakur, Alok Ranjan Verma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Interfacial pressure between Cross-linked Polyethylene (XLPE) and Liquid Silicone Rubber (LSR) in cable joints is one of the crucial parameters for ensuring its reliable operation over a long period. The paper presents a novel method for estimating the interfacial pressure between LSR and XLPE in a high-voltage cable joint, explicitly accounting for the viscoelastic relaxation of the polymers. Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) is utilized for understanding the viscoelastic behaviour of LSR and XLPE. Storage modulus (E’) obtained from DMA measurement has been considered to obtain the elastic modulus (E) variation over a typical service span of 30 years of a cable system. This variation of E has been incorporated into a Finite Element Method (FEM) based model to estimate the interfacial pressure. Interface pressure at XLPE/LSR interface consists of two components i.e. radial stress (σ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</sub>) and hoop stress (σ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">h</sub>). These components have been estimated at 90 °C i.e. maximum continuous operating temperature for XLPE–insulated cables. Both σ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</sub> and σ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">h</sub> are observed to decrease from an initial installation pressure of 0.2 MPa to ∼0.01 MPa at 90 °C over 30 years duration. The direction of stress changes at the interface may lead to cable joint failures. The analytical technique to estimate the interface pressure has been validated using an experiment. LSR and XLPE samples are thermally aged at 90 °C for 45 days, and E and interface pressure measurements have been done. It has been observed that E and interface pressure variation measurements are coherent with the analytical method. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed methodology in estimating the interface pressure in cable joints and tracking its variation over time in operational conditions.

title: Automated Refactoring for Conditional Branching
authors: Yang Zhang, Xi Zhang, Chunhao Dong, Chaoshuai Li, Grant Meredith
publish: IEEE Transactions on Services Computing
abstract: Conditional branching provides a fundamental structure for executing a specific branch based on the value of a boolean expression at run-time. However, repeated or nested conditional branching can lead to increased complexity. Furthermore, fall-through semantics in conditional branching result in uncontrollable jumps. Refactoring conditional branching manually is error-prone, time-consuming, and tedious. There is a critical need to provide automated refactoring support for conditional branching. To this end, this paper presents ReBrancher, an automated refactoring approach to eliminate repeated or nested conditional branching. Firstly, ReBrancher parses source code into an abstract syntax tree and walks through conditional branching statements. Secondly, it removes redundant fall-through semantics by static program analysis and an automaton. The automaton is constructed from a control flow graph to match patterns of conditional branching. Finally, it converts a conditional branching into a switch expression and removes the fall-through semantics. ReBrancher was evaluated on nine real-world projects involving 25,137 conditional branchings. Experimental results show that a total of 1,790 conditional branching constructs are refactored within an average of 18.62 seconds per project. Furthermore, ReBrancher reduced the average cyclomatic complexity by 4.41% and removed 1,249 code smells, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving code quality.

title: Information of Opportunity-aided VI-SLAM for Enhancing Accuracy in Perpetual GNSS-Denied Environments Without Loop Closure
authors: Rongling Lang, Cailun Wei, Ya Fan, Yongkang Qu, Honglei Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Classical visual-inertial simultaneous localization and mapping (VI-SLAM) systems provide accurate relative motion estimation but are plagued by accumulated drift in long-term GNSS-denied environments where loop closures are unavailable. To address this fundamental challenge, we propose a novel framework that leverages “Information of Opportunity” (IOP)—random and heterogeneous diverse external signals including terrestrial radio signals, Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite observations or map data to achieve drift-less geo-localization. Our system integrates three core components: (1) an enhanced visual-inertial front-end with information-theoretic feature selection( FS) that adaptively selects informative visual features for robust and efficient tracking; (2) a versatile IOP processing pipeline that acquires and aligns absolute positioning cues (e.g., from LEO pseudoranges or image-to-map matching) into a unified global reference frame for enforcing global drift-free constraints; and (3) a tightly-coupled optimization back-end that jointly infers IOP-derived global constraints with local visual-inertial measurements within a unified factor graph, enabling continuous drift-less localization. Extensive evaluations on the KITTI Odometry dataset, GVINS dataset and real-world urban driving experiments, conducted with stereo-infrared cameras, IMU, and Iridium LEO receiver, demonstrate that our method effectively suppresses cumulative drift and achieves globally driftless localization.

title: Self-SIDNet: A Self-supervised Network for Winter Arctic Sea Ice Drift Extraction from AMSR2 Passive Microwave Data
authors: Peng Jiang, Tiantian Feng, Yushi Yang, Yuxuan Hu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate monitoring of sea ice drift is essential for Arctic climate studies, navigation safety, and modeling polar ocean-atmosphere interactions. Passive microwave radiometers, such as Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer 2 (AMSR2), provide wide-swath, all-weather observations, making them valuable for drift extraction. However, existing template matching methods often produce sparse drift fields and rely on manually tuned thresholds, while conventional optical flow algorithms exhibit limited performance in terms of extraction accuracy. In this study, we propose a novel deep learning framework, Self-SIDNet, for extracting dense and accurate Arctic sea ice drift fields from AMSR2 89 GHz brightness temperature data. A cycle consistency loss is introduced as a self-supervision signal that enforces forward-backward drift coherence, which improves the performance of the method without relying on ground-truth labels. Self-SIDNet integrates photometric consistency, smoothness regularization, and cycle consistency into a unified loss function, and applies a sea ice concentration mask to restrict learning to ice-covered regions. Experiments show that Self-SIDNet achieves RMSEs of 6.17 and 6.75nbsp;cm/s for horizontal (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$u$</tex-math></inline-formula>) and vertical (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$v$</tex-math></inline-formula>) components, consistently outperforming traditional methods, including the traditional template-matching-based continuous maximum cross-correlation (CMCC) method and the traditional optical flow-based Farneback method. Notably, it provides full spatial coverage of the drift field, resolving the precision-coverage trade-off in CMCC. Comparisons with the National Snow and Ice Data Center (NSIDC) Polar Pathfinder product show high correlation and minimal bias, confirming the reliability of the extracted drift fields, while providing higher spatial resolution for fine-scale drift monitoring. These results demonstrate the potential of self-supervised deep learning for sea ice drift extraction from passive microwave data and suggest its applicability for large-scale, long-term monitoring in polar regions.

title: Ultrasound Slice-to-Volume Registration via Artifact-Suppressed Ultra-Feature Learning
authors: Zixin Zhang, Deqiang Xiao, Long Shao, Jingfan Fan, Danni Ai, Tianyu Fu, Yucong Lin, Yuanyuan Wang, Hong Song, Ping Liang, Ying Gu, Jian Yang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Accurate slice-to-volume registration (SVR) is essential for ultrasound (US)-guided liver interventions. Existing methods typically extract features from slice and volume images to estimate pose parameters. However, the inherent challenges of liver US imaging such as rib shadowing, gastrointestinal artifacts, and poorly visualized vasculature often compromise registration accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose the Artifact Suppressed Ultra-Feature guided SVR (ASUF-SVR), which enhances pose estimation by simultaneously suppressing image artifacts and improving the robustness of feature extraction. The framework integrates two key modules: 1) The Ultra-Feature Extraction (UFE) module, which is a dual-branch design tailored to mitigate the low signal-to noise ratio and low contrast of US images, enabling reliable anatomical feature extraction from both slice and volume data for pose prediction; and 2) the Artifact-Suppressed Evaluation (ASE) module, which supervises similarity measurement and encourages UFE to focus on true anatomical structures rather than artifacts. We validate ASUF SVR on datasets with varying initial offsets and across different organs. Experimental results demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines both quantitatively and qualitatively. By delivering superior accuracy in SVR, ASUF-SVR minimizes the risk of mis targeting during liver intervention, thereby enhancing over all clinical safety.

title: Robust Model Predictive Control of a Four-Switch Buck-Boost Converter for Fast Dynamic Response with Guaranteed ZVS
authors: Gaoxiang Chen, Ka-Hong Loo
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Although four-switch buck–boost (FSBB) converter provides high efficiency, compact structure, and zero-voltage switching (ZVS) capability, its dynamic performance and robust-ness under parameter variations remain insufficiently explored. This paper proposes a unified continuous-control-set model predictive control (CCS–MPC) framework that incorporates ZVS-oriented modeling, stability-based control parameter design methodology, and adaptive disturbance compensation. A hybrid compensation scheme is proposed, combining an extended state observer (ESO) with a least-mean-square (LMS) adaptive filter. This scheme enhances disturbance rejection and mitigates the bandwidth–stability trade-off in conventional ESOs. Experimental results validate that the proposed control achieves steady-state convergence within several switching cycles, maintains full ZVS with 98.5% efficiency, and preserves low computational complexity comparable to PI control. These results confirm that the proposed CCS–MPC offers a practical solution for high-efficiency, fast-response DC–DC converters operating under model inaccuracies.

title: Transition-Region Co-Optimization for Enhanced dV/dt Ruggedness in 4H-SiC MOSFETs
authors: Zihan Zhang, Lei Yuan, Kaiyu Chen, Xiaowen Wang, Xuesong Liu, Tongxiao Hou, Enhuai Guan, Ruijie Yang, Guibao Wang, Miao Yu, Yuming Zhang, Renxu Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Under high-speed switching conditions, silicon car bide (SiC) metal–oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistors (MOSFETs) are susceptible to reliability failures induced by high dV/dt stress, which has emerged as a critical bottleneck limiting their high-frequency and high-efficiency applications. This article presents a systematic investigation of the failure behavior and underlying physical mechanisms of 1200 V 4H SiC MOSFETs under high dV/dt conditions. Transient electric field concentration across the thin gate oxide in the transition region is identified as the primary trigger of dV/dt-induced failure, based on double-pulse testing combined with EMMI, FIB, and SEM failure analyses. Further TCAD transient simulations reveal that both an excessive thin-gate-oxide length and dynamic incomplete-ionization effects contribute to severe transient gate oxide electric-field intensification during fast voltage ramping. Based on these insights, a co-optimization strategy centered on electric-field redistribution is proposed and experimentally validated by shortening the thin-gate-oxide length and reducing the surface doping concentration in the transition region. The optimized device can operate reliably at dV/dt exceeding 330 V/ns, with no failure observed even under the extreme condition of Rg = 0 Ω, representing more than a threefold improvement over the original device. Furthermore, continuous-pulse stress tests demonstrate stable gate leakage current after 2.5 × 106 high-speed switching cycles under dV/dt stress above 300 V/ns, indicating no observable gate-oxide degradation. Large-scale statistical measurements on 1200 V devices confirm that key static parameters, including threshold voltage, on-resistance, and breakdown voltage, remain unchanged, while HTRB testing at 175°C for 1000 h verifies that the proposed optimization does not compromise long-term static reliability.

title: Transformer-Enhanced Machine Learning Framework for Advanced Palm Biometric Recognition
authors: SYeda Adan Fatima Gilani, Muhammad Fasih Uddin Butt, Zirwa Khan, Arshad Ali Khan, Wadood Abdul
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Palmprint (PPR) and palmvein recognition (PVR) have emerged as reliable biometric modalities due to their rich structural information and resistance to impersonation. In this study, we design a dual-modality recognition pipeline that evaluates both palm-based traits using a combination of conventional machine learning (ML) classifiers and recent deep feature extractors. Classical models, including Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forests (RF), and the lightweight MobileNetV2 network, serve as strong baselines, producing accuracies of 98.8%, 98.8%, and 99.4% for PPR; and 95.5%, 97.5%, and 96.8% for PVR. To explore more expressive feature representations, we further employ EfficientNet-B3, Swin Transformer, and Vision Transformer (ViT). EfficientNet-B3 achieves a training accuracy of 94.8% and a validation accuracy 92.3%. The Swin Transformer further improves performance reaching 98.5% validation accuracy with a precision of 98.7%, an AUC-ROC of 0.996, and an inference time of 12 ms per image, indicating efficient inference performance. ViT also demonstrates stable validation performance under the adopted protocol, achieving 99.44% accuracy during training and 98.11% on validation data. To leverage the strengths of both CNN and Transformers models, a hybrid design is developed in which EfficientNet’s spatial feature maps are combined with Swin Transformer’s hierarchical attention features, resulting in more stable and discriminative representations. Finally, the complete recognition system is deployed through a Streamlit-based interface for real-time palm verification, demonstrating the feasibility of proposed approach under the adopted evaluation setting. Overall, the results indicate that merging traditional ML pipelines with contemporary CNN and Transformer-based approaches leads to strong closed-set identification performance of both PPR and PVR systems.

title: CMPCC: Cross-modal Point Cloud Completion Model Fusing Single-view Images and Partial Point Clouds
authors: Liming Zhang, Chenxi Sun, Yuxing Shi, Tingcheng Zhang, Jinfeng Jia, Lu Gao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Point cloud completion is a core task in 3D computer vision, aiming to recover the complete 3D shape of an object from partially incomplete point clouds collected by 3D scanning sensors. In practical scenarios, 3D scanning sensors are affected by factors such as occlusion, insufficient illumination, and resolution limitations, leading to frequent regional missing in the collected point clouds. However, most existing main-stream methods rely on unimodal shape priors and directly infer missing regions from local point clouds through encoder-decoder architectures. This easily results in the loss of local geometric details and inconsistency in global structure, making it difficult to meet the requirements of high-precision applications. To address the above issues, we reconstruct point cloud completion as a cross-modal collaboration task and propose a cross-modal point cloud completion model fusing single-view images and partial point clouds (called CMPCC). The model integrates complementary information from monocular images and local point clouds to achieve high-quality completion with global structure fidelity and refined local details. CMPCC consists of two stages: (1) the first stage is coarse-grained point cloud reconstruction, which uses CNN to extract global features from single-view images and generates a coarse-grained complete point cloud through deconvolution to ensure consistency between the overall contour and the real object. Then, a probability-based patch segmentation strategy is adopted to divide the coarse point cloud into several local patches, and patch-level features are extracted to lay the foundation for detail generation. (2) The second stage is dense point cloud generation, which uses DGCNN to extract fine-grained geometric features of local point clouds, capturing the surface texture and structural relationships of objects. Through a cross-attention fusion module, image features and point cloud features are fused to generate a global style code that constrains the consistency of the global structure. A multi-patch generator is used to fuse coarse-grained patch features with fine-grained geometric information, generating local patch point clouds with rich details. Finally, the patches are stitched together and sampled via FPS to obtain the complete point cloud. Experiments on the ShapeNet-ViPC and ModelNet-MPC datasets show that CMPCC significantly outperforms unimodal/cross-modal baseline methods: on ShapeNet-ViPC, the average F-Score reaches 0.869 (7.3% higher than XMFNet), and the average CD is 1.066×10⁻³ (22% lower than XMFNet); on ModelNet-MPC, CMPCC achieves an average CD of 1.267×10⁻³ and an average F-Score of 0.845, reducing the average CD by 16.4% and increasing the F-Score by 6.8% compared with XMFNet, and reducing the average CD by 9.0% and increasing the F-Score by 5.8% compared with EGIInet, demonstrating strong cross-dataset generalization and performance superiority of the model.

title: Continuous-Time Opinion Dynamics on Heterogeneous Multiple Interdependent Topics
authors: Yan Chen, Wenbing Zhang, Guanglei Wu, Shuqi Zhai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: In this article, we propose a continuous-time multidimensional opinion dynamics model over a social network, incorporating heterogeneous logic matrices that capture interdependencies among multiple topics. The social network comprises an influence network with cooperative and antagonistic relationships and a family of logic networks, where the influence network reflects the interpersonal interactions among individuals. In the continuous-time setting, we show that the connectivity of the social network is fully determined by the connectivity of the influence and logic networks. By employing the structural balance theory, we establish a modulus consensus of the opinion dynamics on strongly connected networks. Moreover, if the influence network contains a directed spanning tree or the logic matrices are reducible, we derive sufficient conditions for exponential convergence of the proposed model. Simulation results are provided to demonstrate and validate the analytical conclusions.

title: LMFF-Net: Lightweight Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Object Detection for Underwater IoT Networks
authors: Xiushuai Xu, Zhibin Xie, Qian Li, Xin Shu, Changbin Shao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: With the advancement of Underwater Internet of Things (U-IoT) networks, underwater object detection has become crucial. However, detection performance is severely hindered by underwater image degradation, small object scales and weak textures, as well as the stringent resource limitations of deployment platforms. Meanwhile, existing detectors rely on heavy backbones and standard downsampling, which not only increases computation but also discards fine-grained spatial information, resulting in low precision for small objects and suboptimal deployability. To address these challenges, a lightweight multi-scale feature fusion network named LMFF-Net is proposed. Utilizing RepGhostNet as the feature extraction network and incorporating an enhanced bidirectional feature pyramid structure, the proposed LMFF-Net effectively reduces the number of parameters and computational cost while maintaining detection accuracy. In the feature pyramid, a multi-scale object enhancement (MSOE) module is designed, which synergistically combines spatial multi-scale convolutions with spatial-frequency attention mechanisms. This synergy enhances feature discriminability and improves small object representation. Furthermore, a channelized spatial fusion (CSF) module is constructed to achieve lossless downsampling of feature maps by leveraging the spatial-to-depth transformation principle, thereby maximizing the retention of fine-grained spatial information in deep networks. Experimental results on the DUO dataset show that LMFF-Net achieves an mAP@0.5 of 84.7% with only 1.73 M parameters, significantly outperforming other classic object detection models. Additionally, generalization experiments on the URPC2020 and RUOD datasets also demonstrate the excellent generalization ability of the proposed model.

title: From Keystrokes to Semantics: Acoustic Side-Channel Attacks on Typed Text Using Deep Neural Networks and Large Language Models
authors: Benjamin Quattrone, Youakim Badr
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Acoustic Side-Channel Attacks exploit keyboard acoustics to infer typed input. Existing evaluations predominantly focus on keystroke or character accuracy, which is well suited for attacks requiring exact symbol recovery. However, such metrics are insufficient for assessing semantic information leakage in naturally occurring, unconstrained text. This work presents EchoStrike, an end-to-end ASCA pipeline that combines a deep neural acoustic classifier with multi-stage decoding, including language model refinement, to reconstruct long-form typed text from passive audio recordings. Using SKAID (Synchronized Keystroke and Acoustic Inference Dataset) to support realistic long-text evaluation, we report keystroke classification performance under strict cross-participant testing and quantify reconstruction quality using complementary syntactic and semantic metrics. Under this setting, the acoustic model achieves 76.34% keystroke accuracy on an unseen participant, and staged decoding improves long-text reconstruction, yielding a Sentence-BERT semantic similarity of 82.19% in a representative long-form evaluation. We further compare how signal processing choices, model architectures, and decoding strategies affect performance, and discuss implications for threat modeling where semantic comprehension, rather than exact character recovery, drives real-world risk.

title: Communication-Efficient Dual-Branch Transmission with Semantic Masks and Quantized Features for V2X Cooperative Perception
authors: Di Wu, Jiankun Peng, Yuming Ge, Shuangzhi Yu, Ke Xu, Chunye Ma
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In IoT-enabled V2X cooperative vehicle–infrastructure perception, roadside units must transmit informative representations to vehicles over bandwidth-limited and latency-sensitive links, making it challenging for single-modality transmission to reduce communication payload while preserving detection accuracy. This paper presents a communication-efficient V2X cooperative 3D object detection framework, termed QMDet (Quantization-and-Mask based Detector). In the proposed design, the roadside unit jointly transmits a low-bit compressed feature map and a semantic mask to the vehicle. The feature branch performs learnable joint channel–spatial compression followed by INT8 quantization. In parallel, the semantic branch conducts contextual modeling and generates a semantic mask, providing explicit semantic priors to compensate for information loss caused by compression and quantization. Furthermore, a gated fusion module is introduced to adaptively integrate vehicle-side features, decompressed roadside features, and semantic priors via dynamic weighting, thereby mitigating cross-view/domain discrepancies. Experiments on the DAIR-V2X-C dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces the RSU-to-vehicle communication payload by 24% while maintaining detection accuracy, achieving gains of 0.11% on AP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3D</sub>@0.5 and 0.13% on AP<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BEV</sub>@0.5. These results indicate that QMDet improves communication efficiency without sacrificing cooperative detection accuracy.

title: Detection Method for Mechanical Deformation of Autotransformer Winding Based on Frequency Response and DATN Algorithm
authors: Lijun Zhou, Hutao Dai, Anzhi Fu, Xiaobo Zhang, Weiheng Liu, Guochao Qian, Dongyang Wang, Lei Guo
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Autotransformer is one of the core electromechanical equipment in high-speed railway, of which winding mechanical deformation is the primary cause of operation failure. Frequency response analysis (FRA) is an effective method for detecting winding mechanical deformation. For improving generalization capability of detection method based on FRA, this paper studies a discrepancy analysis based on deep learning and result interpretation method for FRA curves. Firstly, a differential signal extraction network is developed to capture the discrepancy information between healthy and faulty curves, improving the effective utilization of FRA curve data. Subsequently, a combination of Multi Kernel Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MK-MMD) and adversarial training is incorporated into the network to guide the feature extractor in learning domain invariant features. Finally, the differential adversarial transfer network (DATN) diagnostic algorithm is proposed to achieve identification of winding mechanical deformation faults. Experimental results demonstrate that the DATN algorithm achieves an accuracy of 96% in the source domain and 90% in the target domain.

title: Scattering Aware Episodic Adaptation for Few-Shot HRRP ATR Using Large Language Models
authors: Lingfeng Chen, Panhe Hu, Shuanghui Zhang, Xiangfeng Qiu, Zhen Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: High-resolution range profile (HRRP) provides compact one-dimensional electromagnetic scattering representations but faces challenges due to signal ambiguity and the scarcity of labeled data for unseen target classes. Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated potential by leveraging pre-trained semantic knowledge via In-Context Learning (ICL). However, a fundamental modality gap persists: the semantic space of frozen LLMs, evolved from natural language, is inherently misaligned with the non-Euclidean manifolds of electromagnetic scattering, resulting in a lack of sensitivity to fine-grained signal discriminators. To bridge this gap via manifold alignment, we propose Scattering-aware Episodic Adaptation via Semantics (SEAS), a unified framework that enhances the reasoning capabilities of LLMs with domain-specific signal alignment. Specifically, we conceptualize a hybrid episodic adaptation mechanism that combines the implicit, attention-based reasoning of frozen LLMs with an explicit, gradient-based outer loop facilitated by Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). To bridge the gap between radar signals and textual semantics, we introduce the Scattering-Aware Episodic Construction (SEAC) pipeline, which transforms HRRP measurements into fidelity-validated instruction episodes. Extensive empirical validation demonstrates that SEAS effectively aligns internal semantic representations with radar signal manifolds. Notably, SEAS enables Qwen3-8B to outperform current LLM-based HRRP ATR baselines including DeepSeekV3.2-Exp and Qwen3-max, while our scaled-up variant achieves a 31.6% relative improvement over the strongest baseline.

title: A Single-Motor Vibration-Driven Microrobot with Terrain Adaptability for Robust Cross-Material Locomotion
authors: Peng Liu, Chenghao Wang, Lingqi Tang, Yao Li, Bing Li
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Vibration-driven microrobots for confined space applications (e.g., pipeline inspection, disaster relief) suffer from poor trajectory accuracy due to inherent sensitivity to surface friction variations, and conventional fixed-parameter PID controllers fail to tackle nonlinear dynamics like abrupt friction transitions and initial heading deviations-key limitations in unstructured environments. This paper presents a 2.9 g ultralight vibration-driven microrobot integrated with a fuzzy adaptive control strategy for robust locomotion on heterogeneous surfaces. Systematic experiments conducted on wood, acrylic, aluminum and cross-material composite surfaces show that the proposed control reduces the trajectory error to ~25 mm on low-friction surfaces and significantly suppresses error accumulation under cross-material transition conditions, outperforming traditional PID control. This work addresses the core challenge of friction sensitivity in vibration-driven microrobots to advance their development, and provides a solution for their reliable operation in real-world dynamic surface scenarios.

title: Crop and Weed Detection in Agricultural Fields Using Few-Shot Object Detection
authors: Mohanapriya S, Dinesh K, Kumaravel T, Keerthana S, Malini R, Mounisa V
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Application of artificial intelligence has gained a significant momentum in determining the future of food industry with crop and weed detection playing a significant role in ensuring better productivity and more sustainable farming methods. Nonetheless, the vast majority of current approaches are based on big annotated datasets, which is a serious constraint in real-life applications in agriculture where such datasets are rare, and weed species are not equal across the regions. To meet this challenge, the current paper suggests a new combination of YOLOv8 with Few-Shot Object Detection (FSOD) to obtain high-quality crop and weed detection with a small number of labeled samples, that is, based on transfer learning by using pre-trained YOLOv8 models to extract discriminating visual features, i.e., shape, texture, and spatial patterns. Generalization and overfitting are alleviated by using data augmentation methods. Crops are seen as base classes in this system because they are always available, and weeds as novel classes to enable the ability to quickly adapt to newly introduced or rare species of weeds with minimum annotation. The experimental findings also prove that the proposed methodology is highly accurate in terms of detection, which proves its efficiency in the practical use in agricultural fields. In general, the YOLOv8-FSOD framework has lessened the reliance on big annotated datasets and is beneficial in the provision of effective real-time precision agriculture.

title: Interlayer and Band-Engineered Charge Trap Flash Enabling Low Voltage and Reliable Quad Level Operation
authors: Sanghun Jeon, Sangho Lee, Giuk Kim, Yunseok Nam, Seungyeon Chang, Jinho Ahn
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: Developing charge-trap flash that can operate reliably at low voltages while supporting quad-level storage requires both controlled charge placement and stable switching behavior throughout repeated cycling. In this study, we introduce a device architecture that combines a thin interfacial layer inside the HfZrO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> stack with a charge-trap layer designed to reshape its energy-band profile. Measurements confirm that inserting the interlayer alters the internal polarization response of the dielectric, yielding a broader programmable window, lower program and erase thresholds, and improved step-wise programming characteristics. The modified charge-trap layer further contributes to stable performance by restricting excessive electron injection, resulting in more uniform program and erase transitions even after extended cycling. Collectively, these engineering approaches enable a memory window of 13.6 V, strong incremental programming efficiency, robust disturb immunity, and sustained quad-level storage. A qualitative interpretation based on switching-energy considerations suggests that the interlayer helps maintain a more uniform polarization evolution, while the engineered trap layer reduces interface-driven variation, supporting a dependable platform for future low-voltage quad-level flash technologies

title: Strain Engineering for Next-Generation FDSOI MOSFET, Probing CESL Relaxation with Xe, Ar, and Ge Ion Implantation
authors: L. D. Mohgouk Zouknak, B. Mohamad, K. Romanjek, L. Lachal, Z. Chalupa, M. Charbonneau, W. Vandendaele, V. Mandrillon, F. Mazen, A. Anotta, N. Gauthier, D. Mariolle, L. Brévard, L. Brunet, Ph. Rodriguez, B. Duriez, M. C. Cyrille, C. Fenouillet-Beranger
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: The complexity and cost associated with dual CESL (Contact Etch Stop Layer) integration in advanced CMOS nodes present significant challenges. CESL strain relaxation offers an attractive and cost-effective alternative. In this work, we demonstrate that an appropriate choice of implanted species and process parameters can enable the complete relaxation of a 1.4 GPa SiN tensile CESL in the pMOS region, without inducing any morphological degradation. Electrical characterization of 28 nm FD SOI nMOS reveals that Xe implantation induces the most pronounced stress relaxation and electron mobility degradation, corresponding to a full recovery of pMOS performance. Importantly, this is achieved while preserving device integrity, with no measurable impact on gate or source/drain resistances.

title: A Near Zero-DC Readout Circuit for CMOS Image Sensors
authors: Tonny Do, Robert Johansson, Ruben Gomez-Merchan, Trung Thanh Nguyen
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: This paper presents a 12-bit CMOS image sensor column readout circuit that A/D converts by charging the vertical scan line (VSL) to analog supply voltage VDDH, then ramp down VSL until the pixel source follower (PSF) starts conducting current, which triggers the comparator to flip. The resolution is 1600x1200 and the column pitch is 2.625µm. The readout circuit average power consumption is 422µW at a frame-rate of 30fps with a read noise (RN) of 0.469 mV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</inf>. This results in a FoM2 of 3.21 V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</inf> · fJ. The readout circuit has been taped out in a 22-nm CMOS process and measurements have been acquired from packaged parts.

title: Implementation of a Lightweight Compression Architecture for Real-Time UHD Video Integration on FPGA
authors: Bing-Yi Lin, Shanq-Jang Ruan, Yu-You Xie
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: High-resolution video integration is essential in real-time applications such as surgical navigation, industrial monitoring, and UHD broadcasting. However, merging multiple HD video streams into a unified 4K display remains challenging due to high bandwidth and memory demands. This work presents an FPGA-based system incorporating a low-latency, lossless compression framework. Unlike the other implementations, the proposed Zone-based Positional Compression Encoding (ZPCE) is implemented entirely in RTL and operates in a memoryless, single-pass, where compression is achieved by reducing transmitted data words while preserving a fixed width at interface. These features eliminate the need for frame buffering or complex control logic and makes it well-suited for lightweight FPGA systems. The architecture integrates four 1080p SDI inputs into a single 4K HDMI 2.0 output while supporting multiple SDI standards (3G/6G/12G). Experimental results show efficient compression with low usage and latency. This work offers a scalable solution for real-time UHD video integration across latency-sensitive domains such as medical imaging, industrial vision, and professional video systems.

title: Next Generation Electronics Enabled by Growth-based Monolithic Integration of 2D Materials
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)

title:

                    <sub>2</sub>

                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Transistors for Next-Generation Electronics
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)

title: > 60 GHz Antenna-in-Package with Integrated Lead-Frame Thermal Path and Low-Dk/Df Molding for Multi-Chip Systems
authors: Hao-Che Kao, Che-Sheng Yang, Po-Kai Chiu, Chih-Hao Lin, Meei-Yu Hsu, Tao-Chih Chang, Yu-Min Lin
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: A > 60-GHz antenna-in-package (AiP) module with heterogeneous PA/LNA integration was realized using a molded-cavity chip-embedding structure. Stud-bump flip-chip bonding and laser-assisted soldering enable accurate die alignment and reliable mmWave interconnection. A low-loss molding compound (k = 2.17 W/m·K, Dk = 3.10, Df = 0.0022, CTE = 11.5 ppm/°C) and a co-designed thick-Cu lead-frame heat spreader form an efficient thermal path, achieving 0.574 °C/W thermal resistance and a junction temperature of 76.87 °C. The antenna provides 10.4 dBi peak gain with 58–62 GHz bandwidth, confirming high efficiency for >60-GHz links. The proposed architecture offers a scalable AiP platform with integrated thermal management and reliable heterogeneous die packaging for mmWave applications.

title: Symptom-Based Disease Prediction Using Machine Learning and Chatbot Interface
authors: B.Vijaya Mounika, Bhusa Devi, Jagarapu Greeshma, Chekka Dhilleswara Rao, Patchipulusu Mohan Sai Durga Rajeev, Polamarasetty Gnaneswar Tirumal
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: The rapid advancement of healthcare technologies has created opportunities to improve disease diagnosis and patient awareness. Early recognition of disease is often difficult in primary healthcare, especially for individuals who do not have immediate access to medical professionals. To address this issue, this study develops a symptom-based disease prediction system that uses machine learning and delivers results through a conversational chatbot. Multiple classification models, including Decision Tree, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, Naive Bayes, and Logistic Regression, were trained using a curated symptom–disease dataset enriched with severity scores and precautionary information. The system enables users to report symptoms interactively and receive probable disease outcomes along with explanatory descriptions and safety recommendations. Experimental evaluation shows that Random Forest and Decision Tree models achieve consistently high predictive performance. The proposed system is intended as an assistive and educational tool for preliminary health awareness and does not substitute professional medical diagnosis.

title: Tracked Unmanned Ground Vehicle Navigation Based on A* Algorithm
authors: Wang Wen, Liying Zhang, Siyang Wen, Yacong Hu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: Efficient path planning is crucial for tracked unmanned ground vehicles (T-UGVs) in complex environments, such as PV installation sites. However, traditional A* algorithms often yield unsmooth paths and ignore the unique steering characteristics of tracked platforms. This study proposes an improved A* algorithm that refines the evaluation function by incorporating a central steering cost model and a dynamic heuristic weighting scheme. Additionally, a redundant point removal technique and fourth-order Bézier curves are employed to smooth the trajectory. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method reduces search time by 66% to 98% and significantly improves path quality compared to traditional A*, Dijkstra, and ACO algorithms. The resulting paths are more kinematically feasible and stable for T-UGVs.

title: Robust Stabilization for Nonlinear Networked Control Systems with Intelligent Estimator
authors: Amin Zarei, Yashar Mousavi, Saeed Tavakoli, Paweł Skruch, Pritesh Narayan, Saleh Mobayen
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: This research explores a novel, simple, and robust control method for nonlinear networked control systems (NCSs) with unknown uncertainties in the strict-feedback form. A mathematical strategy is developed to control the output of NCSs by creating a stable error dynamic in a general form. This approach generates a list of formulas based on the state equations of nonlinear NCSs and derivatives of the system error. By calculating the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N$</tex>-th derivative according to the final state equation, a robust control command is introduced to strongly mitigate the lumped uncertainties. Additionally, an intelligent estimator is employed to approximate the nonlinear functions in this methodology using fuzzy logic systems (FLSs). The Lyapunov stability of the method is proven, and the effectiveness of this robust control approach is evaluated through an illustrative example in MATLAB software.

title: Co-Design of Dynamic Decoupling and Robust Control for High-Speed Magnetic Bearing Systems
authors: ZHANG Panpan, HE Yajue, SHEN Zhihuang, CAI Sijie, SHEN Aoda, HUANG Junfeng
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: To address the degraded performance of conventional decoupling approaches induced by dominant gyroscopic cross-coupling phenomena in high-speed active magnetic bearing-supported flexible rotor systems, an eigenstructure assignment-based dynamic decoupling methodology was developed. Based on the fully decoupled model, an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{H} \infty$</tex> control synthesis was implemented and validated through comprehensive experimental testing on a dedicated high-speed test rig. Experimental results confirm that the proposed decoupling methodology effectively mitigates gyroscopic cross-coupling under extreme operating conditions. The designed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{H} \infty$</tex> controller not only maintains stability margins at elevated rotational speeds but also demonstrates exceptional robustness, superior disturbance attenuation characteristics, and enhanced synchronous vibration attenuation performance across the entire operational range.

title: Energy Efficient Navigation of Autonomous Mobile Robots Using Euler Spiral Based Trajectories
authors: Tran Viet Thanh, Enock William, Uchiyama Naoki
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: This paper presents a new approach to energy-efficiently navigate an autonomous mobile robot in a structured environment, based on Euler spirals and jerk limited acceleration profiles. Euler spirals, also known as clothoids, are curves that have curvature proportional to the curve length. The approach was evaluated using an novel energy consumption model of the robot, which identification and verification were conduct using dynamics model of each motor of the real robot. The constant curvature and other smoothening strategies were compared and Euler spirals were found the most energy-efficient. The results show that the proposed approach can reduce the energy consumption of the robot by 95.86% compared to the constant curvature approach.

title:
Total Ionizing Dose Effects on 28nm Microchip RT Polarfire
                    <sup>®</sup>
                    FPGA SRAM Physical Unclonable Functions
authors: Mairely Urias, Alex Cai, Krunal Dave, Nadia Rezzak, G. Richard Newell
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: This paper provides the findings of gamma radiation effects on the Radiation Tolerant PolarFire<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">®</sup> Physical Unclonable Functions (PUF). The results support suggestions that smaller technology nodes (as shown comparing the 28 nm PolarFire FPGA to larger nodes) show higher resistance to Total Ionizing Dose (TID) changes to the power-up state of static random-access memory (SRAM) [1], demonstrating space applicability in providing radiation-tolerant security primitives used for device authentication and device-specific key generation [2]–[4].

title: Characterization of SEEs on Gemini APU In-Memory Processors Under Heavy Ion Radiation
authors: Diego Wildenstein, Matthew Gruber, George Williams, Neil Sampson, Alan D. George
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: Processing-in-memory (PIM) devices, also known as compute-in-memory devices, can provide several benefits to space vehicles. Due to their unique architectures, they provide high-performance computation at a relatively low power usage profile. While such benefits make these processors attractive for space use, they are not the only important factor for consideration. Processors must also be tolerant to the hazards of the space environment; in particular, flight hardware must be resilient against space radiation effects. This research details the first heavy ion radiation testing of an in-memory computing architecture. In particular, we examine the robustness of the Gemini-I Associative Processing Unit (APU) to radiation-induced single-event effects (SEEs) when exposed to heavy ion radiation. By determining the frequency of upsets in different regions of the device, it is possible to characterize how faults manifest and propagate throughout different architectural elements of the device. The data gathered in this experiment will inform future fault-mitigation strategies for PIM architectures.

title: TID Induced Parametric Degradation of Commercial-Off-the-Shelf Electronics and their SEE Responses
authors: Manzar Mahmud, Benjamin Torres-Kulik, David M. Hiemstra
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: To characterize the radiation susceptibility, two commercial-off-the-shelf electronic components were tested to gamma (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{60} \text{CO}$</tex>) irradiation for total ionizing dose response and to 105 MeV proton irradiation for single event effects.

title: Total Dose and Single-Event Effects Testing of the ISL75054 Ultra-Low Noise LDO
authors: M. L. Campanella, W. H. Newman, N.W. van Vonno, D. Wackley, L. G. Pearce, C. E. Thomson, E. J. Thomson
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: We report the single event effects and total ionizing dose test results for the ISL75054 ultra-low noise lowdropout regulator.

title: Dynamic Optimal Power Flow for an Isolated Microgrid Using a Cheetah Optimizer
authors: Masoud Ahmadipour, Hussein Mohammed Ridha, Muhammad Murtadha Othman, Mohammad Lutfi Bin Othman
publish: 2026 IEEE 6th International Conference in Power Engineering Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: In this research, the dynamic optimal power flow (DOPF) problem for islanded microgrids integrating renewable energy sources (RES) is solved using a bio-inspired Cheetah Optimizer. The proposed algorithm emulates the adaptive hunting behavior of cheetahs through dynamic pursuit, speed modulation, and strategic resting phases, enabling an effective balance between exploration and exploitation during the optimization process.
