Unlike approaches that heavily rely on the current best solution, the Cheetah Optimizer conducts broad exploration in the early stages and gradually intensifies exploitation as convergence proceeds, thereby mitigating premature convergence. Its adaptive movement mechanism allows efficient handling of the nonlinear and high-dimensional characteristics of islanded microgrids. The performance of the proposed method is validated on a real islanded 15-bus microgrid network, and simulation results demonstrate its superior solution quality and convergence robustness compared with existing optimization techniques.

title: The Aerospace Corporation's Compendium of Recent Radiation Testing Results
authors: S. C. Davis, R. Koga, K. Lee, J. L. Taggart, G. Tzintzarov, A. W. Wright, M. A. Willhoff
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: Radiation testing was performed on several commercial components to determine the response of these components to the space radiation environment. Testing was mostly focused on SEE from protons and heavy ions.

title: Seed Classification Using Deep Learning and Image Processing
authors: K Kavyashree Karanth, Anantha Murthy, Harshitha G M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: This work presents a machine learning system designed to classify fruit and vegetable seeds using image processing and deep learning. A custom dataset was built with seed images from different plant families, and data augmentation was applied to help the model generalise better and manage uneven class distribution. The classification model uses MobileNetV2, a lightweight convolutional neural network, which was fine-tuned on the dataset. Training was done with the Keras framework, and images were preprocessed in real time using ImageDataGenerator. To make the system easy to use, a web interface was developed with Gradio, allowing users to upload a seed image and receive instant predictions.The model consistently outperformed conventional classifiers like SVM, k-NN, and Random Forest with respect to accuracy and reliability, and it demonstrated strong performance on the validation set. The end product is a fast and easy-to-use seed classification tool that can benefit agricultural technology platforms, farmers, and researchers.

title: High-Efficiency Stratospheric Aerosol: Radiative Modeling of Silver-Doped Titania Nanoparticles for Solar Radiation Management
authors: Naga Sai Dhiraj Kasam
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Climate change trajectories project irreversible impacts, bringing attention to Solar Radiation Management, specifically Stratospheric Aerosol Injection (SAI). Conventional aerosols (e.g. SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, sulphates) have efficiency limitations like short suspension and low scattering. This study evaluates Ag-doped TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> as an alternative by simulating radiative transfer via the Rapid Radiative Transfer Model. At 130 nm particle radii and equal concentrations, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Ag}_{-1 \mathrm{O}_{2}}$</tex> outperformed the lead conventional solid phase aerosol contender (pure TiO2) in reductions of surface heating rate and net fluxes. However, ozone loss and vapor feedback may increase due to stratospheric heating. This work highlights a novel efficient aerosol for this application but SAI and novel aerosols require further studies via robust and precise modeling to reduce uncertainties in climate sensitivity.

title: End-to-End Causal Inference with LLM Agents via a Structured Framework
authors: Aaron Belikoff, Matthew Lamborne
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Causal inference is the task of quantifying the effect of one variable on another. It requires both quantitative reasoning skills and common-sense causal knowledge of the world. Previous methods have attempted to utilize LLMs for causal inference, relying on their world knowledge and reasoning ability. However, it is hard for LLMs to bridge the gap between causal knowledge from the semantic information in a dataset and practical causal inference. Existing approaches directly provide information to the model on causal structure and appropriate inference method. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end approach that aims to allow LLM agents to conduct causal inference without manual annotations. We propose a 6 -step reasoning framework that encourages the model to do causal inference end-to-end. The framework operates in identification, planning, and tool calling phases, for which we integrate tools from existing causal reasoning libraries. We evaluate our framework using Qwen3-14B on QRData, a benchmark covering both numerical causal inference and multiple-choice causal discovery tasks. We also introduce a modified version of the benchmark without explicit instructions about method use or variable roles to assess end-toend capability. In general, we show that our framework is stronger, outperforming the baseline set by GPT-4 with ReAct tool calling despite using a smaller model. In particular, it is better at causal inference with limited guiding information. These findings highlight the value of structured causal reasoning frameworks in underspecified settings.

title: $\text{IMA}_{i}\text{Gen}$: A Cross-Modal Image-to-Music Generation with a Non-Deep Learning Core
authors: Ellie L. Zhang, Callie C. Liao, Duoduo Liao
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: As generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) rises, music generation has nonetheless been unexplored, particularly image-to-music, and relies on deep learning methods. Existing neural-based models depend on large datasets, raising concerns about copyright infringement and increased costs for performance improvements. In contrast, we propose an innovative cross-modal Image-toMusic Artificial intelligence Generation (IMA <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }_{i}$</tex> Gen) framework powered by a novel, non-deep learning music core, minimizing copyright infringement risks by utilizing a creative approach. The framework first generates latent rhythmic features from an input image using Large Language Models (LLMs) and follows with a purely algorithmdriven music core to automatically derive musical features, constructing symbolic music from rhythmic information. In this pilot study, we developed a web tool, GenAIM, based on this image-to-music framework that generates melodies adhering to music theory and rhythmic conventions. Experimental results show that our approach achieves an average music key confidence score of 85.3%, demonstrating its ability to produce diverse, human-like compositions. Therefore, this tool serves as a reliable copilot for composers as well as entertainment, advancing AI in music generation by bridging visual and auditory art forms across modalities. Click on the web link to run our web tool, GenAIM, to generate music from images.

title: Financial Benefit of Interchangeability of Power Transformers on the Eskom Standard Plinth
authors: Malusi Mathonsi, William Lehobye
publish: 2025 IEEE AFRICON
abstract: The replacement of the failed major substation equipment at National Transmission Company South Africa (NTCSA), substations are critical to maintain the South African grid code with respect to adherence to contingency plan and it must be managed within the limited time. The NTCSA transformer specification plays a key role to ensure that the same size capacity transformers are manufactured within the range of specified dimensions, and they can fit onto the plinths without any modifications alternatively, the substation design shall ensure that the plinth covers all possible scenarios, i.e., the possible future replacement with the highest dimensions. The study details the best recommended practice for the utilities to minimize such costs.The recommendation of the study is that the NTCSA specification must restrict the overall size of the transformer for each size such that the transformer from any manufacture can be installed on the standard plinth, standard bund wall and the engineering design office must design the plinth to accommodate the transformer with the highest dimensions for each capacity size. It further recommends that the panel of contractors must be appointed to be ready to address all the transformers that are about to fail based on asset health index. The study found that reasonable cost savings for more than 12% can be achieved. This can be drastically reduced by ensuring interchangeability, which emanates from the fact that there will be no civil work and design fees will be eliminated.

title: An AI/ML Framework for Proactive Cyber Threat Detection in Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles
authors: Oludolapo. E Ogunfowora, Aarti Panday
publish: 2025 IEEE AFRICON
abstract: Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have become indispensable across sectors ranging from logistics to defense, but their increasing reliance on software controlled cyber physical systems leaves them highly susceptible to sophisticated cyberattacks. Current cybersecurity mechanisms are predominantly reactive and fail to address emerging multivector threats such as GPS spoofing, sensor manipulation, and adversarial machine learning exploits. This paper proposes an adaptive, and protocol agnostic intrusion detection system (IDS) leveraging hybrid artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) techniques tailored for UAV environments. The framework integrates Kalman Filter driven anomaly detection with supervised and reinforcement learning algorithms, enabling continuous adaptation to evolving threats with minimal computational overhead. Blockchain based logging is integrated into the IDS pipeline to further strengthen resilience against adversarial attacks designed to deceive AI models. The framework is evaluated in a virtual UAV testbed replicating real-world flight and communication dynamics, using benchmark datasets (e.g., CSE-CIC-IDS2018) augmented with UAV specific attack scenarios. Preliminary results demonstrate improved detection accuracy and adaptability to novel threats, compared to conventional IDS solutions. The proposed framework has the potential to significantly advance proactive cybersecurity in UAV systems while remaining deployable on resource constrained platforms.

title: GAN-Based Detection of Synthetically Generated Satellite Imagery
authors: Vaishnav Anand, Ivan Rodriguez
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: The proliferation of artificially manipulated satellite imagery poses threats to decision-making processes across urban planning, disaster response, and national security. Zhao, 2021 [1] provides a clear documentation of how realistic manipulated geospatial data can be, and how, to the untrained eye, it is almost unnoticeable. Despite this growing threat, research targeting geospatial deepfake detection remains severely limited, with most studies fixating on facial or general image detection. This research presents a novel Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) framework designed to detect and counteract geospatial deepfakes, leveraging adversarial learning principles to develop an AI-powered defense system with the ability to distinguish authentic satellite imagery from sophisticated deepfakes. Using the SpaceNet 7 multi-temporal satellite imagery dataset with over 70,000 authentic images across different terrains, we introduced a specialized GAN architecture that we trained on a 70/30 test split, consisting of a generator network that produces <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$128 \times 128$</tex> grayscale images from 100-dimensional noise vectors, and a discriminator network that classifies imagery authenticity through the LeakyReLU activation and dropout regularization. This model was trained for 7,000 epochs using binary cross-entropy loss optimization and Adam optimizer with a batch size of 16. The experimental results demonstrated that this GAN-based detection framework achieves 88% classification accuracy across varied geospatial forgery typologies, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6 - 2 3 \%}$</tex> improvement over general-purpose deepfake detectors for satellite imagery. The discriminator was successfully able to identify artificial manipulations by finding subtle statistical anomalies in spectral and textural distributions that characterize synthetic content. This work recognizes and addresses a gap in the literature where geospatial deepfakes have received minimal research attention compared to other domains, though their potential impacts are far more consequential.

title: Machine Learning-Based Real-Time Cardiovascular Risk Assessment for Spaceflight: A Multi-Biomarker Approach Using Inspiration4 Mission Data
authors: Rahul Gupta, Shail Gupta, M Windy McNerney
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Early cardiovascular risk detection during spaceflight requires innovative monitoring approaches that can identify physiological stress before clinical symptoms manifest. We developed CardioPredict, a machine learning-enabled point-of-care diagnostic system using biomarker data from SpaceX Inspiration4 civilian astronauts to predict cardiovascular risk in microgravity environments. Our approach addresses the unique challenges facing civilian spaceflight participants who may lack the extensive physical conditioning of professional astronauts, making them more susceptible to rapid cardiovascular adaptations. Through advanced feature engineering of inflammatory, thrombotic, and metabolic markers, our Elastic Net model achieved 97.6% prediction accuracy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=0.976 \pm 0.015$</tex>) with 0.52 risk unit precision. Metabolic dysregulation markers (Fetuin A) and acutephase inflammatory responses emerged as primary predictors, with temporal biomarker dynamics proving crucial for early detection. This technology platform enables the development of miniaturized lab-on-chip devices for real-time biomarker monitoring, with immediate applications for space medicine and terrestrial critical care environments where rapid cardiovascular risk stratification is essential.

title: Automated Framework for Intelligent Interview Preparation and Evaluation
authors: Niveditha M, Swetha M D, M Siva Rama Krishna, Harsha Bharadwaj, Sarthak Sengupta, Vinay R Srihari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Candidates find it challenging to recognize and strengthen their flaws because the interview preparation process frequently lacks organized feedback and individualized examination. In this paper, a unique application that uses large language models (LLMs) to offer individualized interview coaching is proposed, along with an automated framework for intelligent interview preparation and evaluation. The solution makes use of Gradio for UI creation and Ollama for local AI processing using Google's Gemma 3:1b model. The program creates role-specific interview questions in real time, offers thoughtful response recommendations, and provides thorough feedback with score systems. This system runs completely offline, guaranteeing total data privacy and having no operating expenses, in contrast to cloud-based alternatives. In order to balance creativity and relevance, the implementation uses temperature-controlled text production and supports professional responsibilities. The evaluation's findings show high user satisfaction with an average response and feedback generating time of less than 500 milliseconds. By showcasing the viability of implementing complex language models on consumer-grade hardware for useful applications, this research adds to the expanding field of accessible AI-powered educational aids.

title: Simulating Survival in Glioblastoma: Clinical Decision Tool Based on MGMT and IDH1 Biomarkers
authors: Sarayu Ganganalli
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) remains one of the most aggressive and fatal brain tumors, with a median overall survival of less than 15 months. There are biomarkers such as MGMT promoter methylation and IDH1 mutation which have been recognized as important prognostic markers for GBM; however, these measures are rarely integrated into real-life decision-making tools. The aim of this project was to build on previous work by developing a survival prediction model based on machine learning, using simulated clinical data derived from 500 synthetic GBM patients. The clinical data used for the synthetic patients included clinical characteristics such as age, sex, tumor size, KPS score, treatment type, and biomarker status. A Random Forest Regressor was used to make predictions on the time to overall survival and was deployed using an interactive app that was built using Streamlit. The synthetic nature of the dataset allows for quick simulation of survival outcomes across many potential patient profiles, allowing for a meaningful representation of the technology to link molecular data with real utilization in clinical predictive modeling.

title: A Low-Cost PWM-Controlled Driver Circuit for Bioelectronic Medicine
authors: William Penhall, Dhaval Solanki
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Bioelectronic stimulation is a rapidly growing method of neural therapy. Momentary surges of low-current electrical signals can help treat psychological illnesses over time. In this paper, we developed a precise pulse width modulation (PWM)based current controller that uses both single-supply and dualsupply power rails. We successfully prototyped a design on a breadboard which included: 1) A PWM-voltage DC-DC converter and 2) a non-inverting amplifier stage. Its design was then constructed and tested on a printed circuit board (PCB) to verify precision and accuracy. Simple and space-efficient designs such as our current controller aim to offer a low-cost alternative to bioelectronic labs. The global need for this industry grows every year as drug prices surge, and funding such an endeavor becomes challenging. After careful testing, our PCB responds accurately to signals from a microcontroller, and outputs voltages reliably close to their calculated values. Measurements fall within uncertainty of the oscilloscope's voltage readings, which contain an uncertainty of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.5 \%$</tex>.

title: LATTA: Langevin-Anchored Test-Time Adaptation for Enhanced Robustness and Stability
authors: Harshil Vejendla
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) aims to adapt a pretrained model to distribution shifts using only unlabeled test data. While promising, existing methods like Tent suffer from instability and can catastrophically forget the source knowledge, especially with small batch sizes or challenging corruptions. We argue that this arises from overly deterministic updates on a complex loss surface. In this paper, we introduce Langevin-Anchored Test-Time Adaptation (LATTA), a novel approach that regularizes adaptation through two key mechanisms: (1) a noisy weight perturbation inspired by Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics (SGLD) to explore the local parameter space and escape poor local minima, and (2) a stable weight anchor that prevents the model from diverging from its robust source pre-training. This combination allows LATTA to adapt effectively without sacrificing stability. Unlike prior Bayesian TTA methods, LATTA requires no architectural changes or expensive Monte-Carlo passes. We conduct extensive experiments on standard benchmarks, including Rotated-MNIST and the more challenging CIFAR-10-C. Our results demonstrate that LATTA significantly outperforms existing methods, including Tent, CoTTA, and EATA, setting a new state-of-the-art for self-supervised TTA by improving average accuracy on CIFAR-10-C by over 2% while simultaneously reducing performance variance.

title: Enhanced Accuracy in Residential Security Using AI-Driven Multi-Model Face Recognition
authors: P.Kalyanasundaram, P. Meenakshi Devi, N Saravanan, Abishek M, Gokulnath S, Pranesh R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Aim: Residential surveillance systems frequently suffer from false alarms, particularly in "No Human" scenarios caused by illumination variation, background complexity, and model uncertainty. This work aims to improve human presence validation and reduce false positives in practical home security environments using a confidence-aware adaptive multi-model face recognition framework. Materials and Methods: The study was conducted using a dataset of 1,500 images collected from public datasets and real-world surveillance-style scenes. The dataset includes images captured under different lighting conditions and background variations to represent realistic residential scenarios. The system’s effectiveness was measured using standard evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Group 1 – Existing Method: The baseline system employed a single MobileNetV2-based convolutional neural network for classification of Admin, Intruder, and No Human categories. This approach represents conventional lightweight CNN-based surveillance systems. Group 2 – Proposed Method: The proposed system utilizes a confidence-aware adaptive multi-model framework integrating FaceNet, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNetB0. An adaptive decision fusion strategy based on model confidence scores was introduced to stabilize predictions and reduce classification uncertainty, particularly in No Human scenarios. Results: Experimental results demonstrate substantial improvement in No Human detection accuracy, classification stability, and overall system reliability compared to the single CNN baseline 88%. The proposed system achieved 98% accuracy while significantly reducing false alarms. Conclusion: The findings confirm that confidence-aware adaptive multi-model decision mechanisms enhance robustness and practical applicability of AI-based residential surveillance systems, offering a reliable solution for false alarm reduction in real-world security environments.

title: TruthLens: A ResNet CNN Approach for Detecting Manipulated Media
authors: Kavyashree G M, Smitha Rai, Madhura J, Santhosh M, Prathima Mabel J, Bharath B C
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Deepfakes are falsely created images videos or audio prepared in the techniques of Artificial Intelligence (AI) exclusively in GANs(Generative Adversarial Networks). The lines between authentic and synthetic media are more hard to draw due to the level of realism portrayed by them. Deepfakes are hence associated with the spread of pseudoscience detect on-line fraud and reputational harm. TruthLens, an AI-powered deepfake recognition system, has been developed as a wide-ranging solution to analyze and endorse chromatic content using radical deep-learning approaches, explored the various approaches. A fusion manner coalescing ResNet-18 for deep three-dimensional feature taking out and an Autoencoder centered incongruity recognition contrivance to pinpoint elusive variations in facial qualities, unification provinces, illumination shapes, and gesture subtleties. Computer vision models like CNN examine spatial inconsistencies within images and videos .This model presented key progress in deep fake detection, outlines the strengths and limitations of current methods and highlights areas where continued research and innovation are urgently needed.

title: DeepLymphNet: A Deep Learning and approach for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia Detection
authors: Minal K Deore, M Brindha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Death due to cancer is still proceeding that lead to delayed diagnoses or missed detection. Metastasis is a significant contributor to the cancer fatality rate. Early detection of or recognition carcinogens is critical to facilitate early diagnosis and treatment. Some common cancers include breast, prostate and lung cancer and they are usually diagnosed using a variety of imaging modalities such as Computed Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) or an X-ray. Although pattern recognition techniques and diagnostic tools such as ABCD (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) works exist these solutions might not achieve the best results. From this, there is large opportunity for healthcare automation; especially in the prediction and prevention of deadly diseases like cancer. This work presents DeepLymphNet, a concurrent deep learning based framework for classification and detection of ALL. Through employing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using different types of activation functions such as Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU), Attention-based ReLU (AReLU), Swish, Mish and Exponential Linear Unit -ELU, the proposed model achieved a record 100% accuracy. Overall, the DeepLymphNet framework yields better results than classic diagnostic techniques and proposes a new fully automatic solution for analyzing leukocytes samples. This work shows the strong capability of deep learning techniques in medical image analysis, which provides a fast and cost-effective way to diagnosis relying on human experience that is susceptible to error. By minimising the chances of a false positive diagnosis, the new approach may speed up cancer detection and enhance survival prospects.

title: Learning for Statistical Channel Fingerprint-Based Positioning in Massive MIMO System: Static Mapping and Dynamic Tracking
authors: Xinrui Gong, Xiaofeng Liu, Xu Dong, Chen Sun, Xiqi Gao, Xiaohu You
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Accurate, low-latency, and infrastructure-light positioning is a core requirement for 6G integrated positioning and communications (IPAC), yet remains challenging when robustness to fast fading and applicability to both static and dynamic scenarios are required. This paper proposes a unified massive MIMO positioning framework built on Beam-Domain Statistical Channel Fingerprints (BDSCFs), which are long-term, location dependent angular–delay energy matrices that are temporally stable and naturally available at the base station. BDSCFs serve as a unified representation that bridges fingerprint-based localization and continuous tracking. For static localization, we reformulate positioning as a conditional distribution learning problem and develop a BDSCF-Feature Extraction Conditional GAN (BFGAN), in which the generator maps latent noise and BDSCFs to 2-D coordinates while the discriminator enforces data-consistent sampling. The training objective combines ad versarial loss with a geometric regularizer; we show theoreti cally that the adversarial term minimizes the Jensen-Shannon divergence between generated and true conditional distributions, while a Lipschitz-type bound on the generator preserves spatial consistency. For dynamic tracking, we design a Transformer based tracker that operates on short temporal sequences of BDSCF tensors. By applying patch embedding and multi-head self-attention, the tracker learns motion-aware spatio-temporal features and enables low-latency regression under an offline training/online-inference paradigm. Geometry-based simulations demonstrate that BFGAN surpasses deterministic regression baselines and benefits from denser reference grids and more antennas; the Transformer attains 95% of errors ≤ 2 m and shows the expected sensitivity to sequence length. The BDSCF (BFGAN + Transformer) pipeline bridges fingerprinting and continuous tracking, offering a practical path toward accurate positioning in 6G IPAC systems.

title: Robust Target-Sensitive Region Segmentation via Adaptive Frequency Fusion and Dynamic Optimization
authors: Hua Lu, Guoheng Huang, Qiaorui Che, Bingo Wing-Kuen Ling, Zhipeng Zheng, Xiaochen Yuan, Xuhang Chen, Zhoule Feng, Chi-Man Pun, Qingjian Ye
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Target-sensitive region segmentation plays a crucial role in environmental understanding and perceptual decisionmaking. Its accuracy and robustness directly affect the reliability of smart devices in consumer electronics, such as autonomous vehicles and augmented reality glasses, as well as in medical consumer electronics, like intelligent endoscopic lesion segmentation systems. However, in complex scenarios, common cross-domain challenges, such as boundary ambiguity, semantic confusion, and high-frequency interference, often arise. Current mainstream methods overly rely on spatial-domain feature modeling, which limits their ability to explore implicit cross-scale semantic correlations in the frequency domain, thereby reducing segmentation robustness under heterogeneous interference. To address these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Frequency Fusion Dynamic Optimization Network (AFFDO-Net), which integrates three key modules: 1) The Frequency-Adaptive Enhancement Fusion Module (FAEFM), which dynamically fuses high- and low-frequency image information through a frequency-domain decoupling strategy, improving both global localization and edge-sharpening capabilities; 2) The Dynamic Response Optimization Module (DROM), which utilizes a Progressive Channel Self-Attention Mechanism (PCSM) and a Positional Information Mapping Module (PIMM) to dynamically optimize the fused features, alleviating intra-class inconsistency and boundary offset caused by the fusion of high- and low-frequency information; 3) The class-large kernel attention wavelet convolution module (SWC-LKA) embedded in the decoder, which mitigates noise interference, such as reflection artifacts, through its frequency-space dual-domain filtering characteristics, significantly enhancing the detail retention capability of the segmented regions. We conducted experiments on five publicly available datasets and one private dataset (Colps), and the results demonstrate that our network outperforms existing methods.Our code is available at https://github.com/LLH45/AFFDO.

title: Uncertainty Modeling for Domain Generalization in Cross-Scene Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Bin Pan, Lan Ma, Ruiying Li, Xia Xu, Zhenwei Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Domain generalization is a promising approach for cross-scene hyperspectral image classification. Existing domain generalization-based methods usually neglect uncertainty in feature statistics, which may make the trained model vulnerable to statistical changes. Uncertainty modeling may solve this problem by explicitly characterizing the distribution of feature statistics. However, introducing it directly faces two obstacles: properly describing uncertainty is challenging, and the convergence of the entire model is not guaranteed. To address the above issues, in this paper, we propose an uncertainty modeling method for domain generalization (UMDG) in cross-scene hyperspectral image classification, which consists of a newly designed framework and a convergence theorem. The framework develops two strategies to model uncertainty in input and label spaces, respectively. In the input space, we propose a variational style augmentation approach to model the explicit distribution of feature statistics across domains by sampling from a learned Gaussian distribution. To maintain consistency, in the label space, we introduce a label mixup strategy that leverages the same uncertainty to increase label diversity. Furthermore, to ensure convergence of the entire model, we establish a convergence theorem by demonstrating that the model converges in expectation to a stationary point. The major contributions of UMDG include designing an uncertainty modeling method for domain generalization in cross-scene hyperspectral image classification and providing a convergence theorem. Our experiments on three popular datasets have substantiated the significance of our work.

title: IoT-Based Adaptive Low-Power Microgrid for Villages with Ring Main Theorem
authors: Rohith S, S VIKASH, C. Gnana Kousalya, A. Pradhipa
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Rural people often experience weak electricity supply and high distribution losses so it creates a major issue for sustainable power supply. This paper proves IoT-Based Adaptive Low-Power Microgrid designed for village environments and enhanced using a Ring Main distribution topology. The system integrates solar energy generation and battery storage and low-power IoT sensor nodes that continuously monitor voltage, current, load variations, and line health across the network. Embedded and IoT regulates the processes of this real-time data and dynamically optimize the energy flow by treating as primary critical loads and applying Ring Main based routing to achieve the available system, fault acceptable range, and balanced power distribution. The proposed framework cannot automatically schedule the loads during energy-efficient periods. It also does not support energy prediction using past sensor data to detect voltage problems early. Simulation results can show improved stability, reduced transmission losses, and efficient utilization of solar and battery resources. Overall, the system provides a resilient, energy-efficient, and cost-effective solution for rural areas by providing electricity, showing that IoT, embedded systems, and the Ring Main topology can collectively enhance nextgeneration microgrid performance in village.

title: Array Scale Evaluations of High-k Inside Memory Hole (HIM) Structure for Advanced Z-scaling of 3D Flash Memory
authors: Takumi Moriyama, Naohiro Hosoda, Ryota Suzuki, Masanori Tsutsumi, Shunsuke Takuma, Masashi Iwasaki, Satoshi Shimizu, Fumie Kikushima, Tatsuya Ishikawa, Tatsuo Ogura, Katsuyuki Sekine
publish: 2026 IEEE International Memory Workshop (IMW)
abstract: Bit density of 3D flash memory has been improved by increasing numbers of word-line (WL) layers for vertical scaling (Z-scaling); however, this approach raises process cost. Though gate pitch scaling is essential to reduce the process cost, one of the issues for the gate pitch scaling is a deflection of dielectric between WLs (WL dielectric) during gate replacement process. In this study, a High-k Inside Memory Hole (HIM) structure at array scale was evaluated to overcome this issue, where a high-k layer is formed inside the memory hole (MH) instead of via slits. The HIM structure reduces final thickness of WL dielectric, thereby enabling further gate pitch scaling. Triple Level Cell (TLC) Vth distributions after 3k program/erase cycles were demonstrated in the HIM structure with a rectangular gate shape. High temperature data retention (HTDR) of the HIM structure were comparable to those of the conventional structure. The HIM structures enable 24% gate pitch scaling and 14% increase of the number of WL layers while maintaining total stack height. The HIM structures offer a promising solution for cost-effective Z-scaling in future 3D flash memory.

title: Materials to Systems Co-Optimization (MSCO) Modeling Platform for PPA projections from Novel Material and Process Innovations Targeting Next Generation DRAM Technologies
authors: Ashish Pal, Hojin Kim, Gregory Costrini, Ning Yang, Rohit Gupta, Subi Kengeri, El Mehdi Bazizi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Memory Workshop (IMW)
abstract: We introduce a novel Materials to Systems Co-Optimization (MSCO <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${}^{\text{TM }}$</tex>) modeling platform for DRAM technology to enable array-level PPA projection from dimension scaling material and process selection, as well as architecture configuration. Our DRAM MSCO platform incorporates access transistor electrical characteristics along with cell and array-level detailed parasitic resistances and capacitances. Using our DRAM MSCO platform, we evaluate array-level PPA impact for several scenarios such as access transistor electrical properties modification as well as WL and BL material configuration for different DRAM architectures.

title: Numerical Simulation of the Three-Phase Flow Characteristics of Solid-Liquid-Gas in the Filling Valve Based on CFD-DEM
authors: JiYangYang Ding, Junfang Ni
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: To reveal the three-phase flow pattern of solid-liquid-gas in the beverage filling valve, this paper studies the effects of particle size, flow rate and inlet angle based on the CFDDEM coupling model. The results show that particle size determines the fluid's followability; high flow rate enhances the group effect to improve the flow stability; the inlet angle controls the momentum loss, and 30° is the critical threshold for improving the flow. The research suggests that the inlet angle of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 0^{\circ}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 5^{\circ}$</tex> should be preferred and the particle parameters should be reasonably matched to achieve efficient and stable operation of the system.

title: TensorDynamic: Bridging Application- and Instruction-Level Fault Injection for DNN Tensor Core Execution
authors: Yuxiao Jia, Euijun Chung, Huanzhi Pu, Ben Feinberg, Hyesoon Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Deep neural network (DNN) inference relies heavily on Tensor Core operations, which are vulnerable to transient hardware faults in computation pipelines not protected by errorcorrecting codes (ECC). Prior fault injection work has explored both application-level and instruction-level effects on DNN accuracy. However, existing application-level approaches support only coarse perturbations and do not capture hardware execution details, while instruction-level approaches lack application-level context.To address this gap, we propose TensorDynamic, an application-aware instruction-level dynamic fault injection tool for Tensor Core execution in DNN workloads. TensorDynamic enables fine-grained fault injection into MMA (matrix-multiplyaccumulate) instructions during DNN execution. Across multiple models, we show that, under the same error injection rate and severity, application-level fault injection can produce substantially different inference outcomes from instruction-level fault injection. This result underscores the need for execution-aware fault injection when evaluating DNN resilience on GPU Tensor Cores.

title: A Hybrid Approach for Stock Market Sentiment and Price Movement Analysis Using Machine Learning
authors: Neeraja Veeramachineni, K. Shilpa, Soumya Sahu, Kummari Kumar, Hansini Sadak, K Bhagyasree
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The stock prices in the stock market are subject to changes in their past trading history, as well as, the mood of investors reflected in the financial news and social media channels. The traditional models which consist of technical and fundamental factors as mainly used in forecasting future price changes disregard the important role played by the public sentiment and the psychological factors which are important in short time variations. This paper aims at providing an integrated approach to combine the concept of sentiment analysis and past information on the price movements of the stock to provide improved forecasts on the price movements of stocks. The step of natural language processing is the most suitable to evaluate the sentiment score polarity of unstructured text on social media and financial news platforms and predict it with the help of both machine learning models and deep learning models on the basis of previous information about stock price changes. The collected information of the sentiment analysis and the past is then taken to the collective forecasting model to predict.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Neural Architectures for Collaborative Autonomous Intelligence
authors: Sandeep Soni, Rajvir Saini
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: HITL neural architectures have also emerged as a promising method to enhance the decision-making, flexibility, and safety of autonomous systems. Some of the difficulties that autonomous systems face are a lack of flexibility and transparency, plus the ability to adapt to unforeseeable environments. These shortcomings are especially evident in risky applications like autonomous driving, robotics, and the medical field. This study will present the new version of the HITL framework that would have assisted in overcoming these limitations by incorporating human feedback into the decisionmaking process in real-time. The architecture allows human learning through human expert participation in the strategic decision-making locations, enhanced edge case handling, and increased decision-making in dynamic and demanding environments. The system that was proposed was tested in three key areas, which were autonomous driving, robotics, and healthcare. The experimental findings indicate that the HITL system performs better than the fully autonomous system, with a decision accuracy of 92.5% as opposed to 85.0% of the autonomous system. Moreover, the HITL system demonstrates a 15% acceleration in learning rate, which is the positive influence of human feedback on the system to be able to adapt and learn. Although these results are promising, there are challenges associated with the integration of human feedback in real-time, and these challenges include feedback latency and scalability. This paper emphasizes the usefulness of HITL systems in enhancing autonomous decision-making and proposes future research directions to minimize the delays in feedback and make autonomous systems more robust and adaptable, which would result in more robust, ethical, and adaptable autonomous systems.

title: AI-Based Risk Assessment of Alzheimer's Conversion Using Brain Age Prediction Models
authors: D. Ganesh, M. Sunil Kumar, G Shireesha, Penta Kalyan Kumar, Y Venkata Krishna Reddy, Kandula Venkata Maheshwar Reddy
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Alzheimer's Disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disease, and early identification of risk converters is imperative to intervene effectively. Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) represents an intermediate stage between normal aging and Alzheimer's disease; however, the rate of conversion varies significantly across individuals. This paper introduces a hybrid framework using AI to forecast the conversion risk to Alzheimer's Disease through brain age modelling. The study has used structural MRI scans to predict brain age through a Vision Transformer model and computes the value of the Brain Age Gap (BAG) to diagnose abnormal brain aging. Moreover, Graph Neural Networks are used to demonstrate the connectivity pattern of the brain and diagnose structural alterations at the network level. Brain age and connectivity measures, along with other clinical variables, are incorporated into the DeepSurv framework to forecast conversion risk at various time intervals from MCI to Alzheimer's Disease. Also, the proposed method has been validated on the ADNI dataset to demonstrate that the proposed hybrid model effectively forecasts risk to Alzheimer's Disease in patients with MCI.

title: Research on Dual-Frequency Penetrating Radio Wave Detection Method and Application in Fault Structures of Coal Seam Working Face
authors: Rongxin Wu, Yao Pang, Zean Hu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Radio wave penetration technology is widely employed for geological anomaly detection in coal seam working faces; however, conventional single-frequency techniques exhibit limitations in fault detection, including insufficient sensitivity to weak fault anomalies and ambiguous fault boundary characterization. Although multi-frequency radio wave exploration has been attempted, data processing and imaging methodologies remain confined to single-frequency approaches, failing to fully exploit the potential advantages of multi-frequency data. To address these constraints and achieve more precise detection of fault structures in coal seam working faces, a radio wave penetration data processing method utilizing the dual-frequency absorption factor γ is proposed. Theoretical derivation elucidates the variation laws between attenuation constants of coal/rock media and radio wave frequency, establishing a computational model for the dual-frequency absorption factor γ based on the ratio of dual-frequency attenuation constant difference to frequency difference. The model demonstrates that γ exhibits more pronounced variation rates in the low-frequency band; consequently, employing γ for data processing and imaging requires at least one low-frequency band to yield stronger anomaly responses. COMSOL multiphysics simulations validate that γ exhibits comparable variation trends to single-frequency attenuation coefficients in coal seam, yet the anomaly amplitude in fault zone doubles relative to high-frequency attenuation coefficient, significantly improving detection sensitivity and response intensity for fault exploration, thereby substantiating the efficacy of utilizing γ for multi-frequency data processing. Field tests conducted at a fully mechanized working face in Zhangji coal mine of Huainan Mining Area, Anhui Province, demonstrate that γ enables precise delineation of fault-affected zone extent for faults with throw less than one-third of seam thickness. The experimental results reveal that, compared with single-frequency imaging, this method achieves more accurate fault boundary characterization and enhanced recognition precision of fault influence zones, showing high consistency with mining exposure results. This method effectively overcomes the deficiencies of single-frequency detection and provides a novel technical approach for fine detection of fault structures and multi-frequency data processing in coal seam working faces.

title: Refined State-error-based Model Predictive Control and Flight Testing of Eagle of Taihu 10: A Canard-Configured Tail-Sitter Technology Demonstrator
authors: Zhixiong Xu, Guangwei Wen, Yixin Hu, Sijia Li, Li Fan, Chao Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: As the size of tail-sitter electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft increases, significant challenges have arisen due to a lower thrust-to-weight ratio, more nonlinear aerodynamics during transition, and slower control response. To support the design and implementation of the flight control system for the large-scale tail-sitter eVTOL Eagle of Taihu 100 (ET100), a one-third-scale, 13 kg subscale technology demonstrator, ET10, was developed. This paper presents the control framework tailored for ET series. In this framework, a refined state-error-based model predictive control (MPC) scheme is proposed. By reformulating the translational dynamics, the coupling between translation and rotation is explicitly captured. Aerodynamic feedforward and efficiency-aware control allocation are integrated to mitigate model uncertainties. A PX4 software-in-the-loop (SITL) platform was constructed by integrating data from computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations and experiments to ensure a high-fidelity aerodynamic modeling. Flight simulations were first conducted to evaluate the improved algorithm in terms of tracking performance across various tasks. Next, flight tests of the ET10 were conducted, demonstrating the reliability and robustness of the scheme across the full flight envelope.

title: Design and Analysis of a High Torque Density FSPM Machine with Enhanced Low Pole-Pair Working Magnetic Field
authors: Shuangshuang Luo, Yu Zhao, Dawei Li, Guangyu Wang, Ronghai Qu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Benefited from the flux modulation effects, flux-switching permanent magnet (FSPM) machines have the advantage of high torque density. However, constrained by the flux barrier effects caused by the spoke-array PMs, the low pole-pair magnetic fields that feature stronger flux modulation effects and torque generation capability have been restricted. This paper aims to realize the efficient utilization of low pole-pair working magnetic fields by enhancing the excitation source and improving the transmission paths. On the one hand, the asymmetric stator teeth and PMs are adopted, which contain rich magnetomotive force (MMF) and permeance harmonics. These harmonics interact synergistically to produce the desired low pole-pair working magnetic fields. On the other hand, the flux bridges is introduced, which provides the low-magnetic reluctance paths for the working magnetic fields. In detail, the analytical model and working principle of the proposed FSPM machine are established and elaborated. Subsequently, the influence of key parameters on torque performances are investigated via finite element analysis (FEA). Furthermore, comprehensive performances comparison between the proposed FSPM machine and a conventional counterpart are conducted. The torque contributions of 1-pole-pair and 5-pole-pair harmonics are increased from 0 Nm and 3.82 Nm to 1.27 Nm and 4.38 Nm, respectively, leading to a 23.7% improvement in torque density. Finally, a 12/17 stator/rotor pole prototype is fabricated and tested.

title: Full-period one-shot prediction of Li-ion battery aging trajectories via multi-view encoding
authors: Jie Huang, Yi Yu, Shangkun Liu, Kuijie Li, Yu Zhou, Liqun Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Data-driven methods for battery aging trajectory prediction are limited in performance due to feature redundancy. To conquer this defect, this paper proposes a prediction framework via multi-view encoding. The method introduces three complementary views: capacity decay, material activity, and polarization shift, and uses independent encoders to extract aging information from each view. It then employs a multi-head attention mechanism to adaptively aggregate features, and finally generates the aging trajectory using a BiLSTM decoder. Experimental results show that, compared with early-fusion baselines, the proposed framework reduces MAPE by 47.5%, achieving a value of 1.05%. In addition, an R2 of 0.87 confirms that the model can accurately reconstruct the aging trajectory. Moreover, the model demonstrates excellent adaptability across aging stages, with prediction error decreasing from 1.16% in the early stage to 0.65% in the later stage. This work establishes a principled multi-view encoding paradigm and provides novel insights for achieving high-reliability state estimation in battery management systems.

title: OMARF: An Orchestrated Multi-Agent Resilience Framework for Autonomic Software-Defined Offshore Wind Industrial Networks
authors: Agrippina Mwangi, Mikkel Gryning, Elena Fumagalli, Madeleine Gibescu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Industrial networks are growing rapidly in scale and complexity, making them increasingly vulnerable to random faults and cyberattacks. Traditional resilience approaches often struggle to keep up, motivating the adoption of autonomic software-defined solutions that can detect, respond to, and adapt to disruptions in real time. This paper introduces the Orchestrated Multi-Agent Resilience Framework (OMARF), which integrates self-healing and self-defense agents to manage failures in the control-plane and data-plane components, mitigates thermally-induced network congestion, and defends against persistent cyber threats. Simulations demonstrated that OMARF-enabled networks achieve up to 28.3% lower path latency and higher throughput across diverse scenarios. Even under degraded conditions, the framework maintained a resilience index, RI(t), above 0.625, indicating stable and adaptive performance. Key mechanisms such as controller autoscaling, reinforcement-learning-based path recomputation, and adaptive cyber defense work in concert to sustain a resilient, self-adaptive network state. These results highlight the potential of OMARF to enhance the reliability and security of modern industrial networks.

title: End-to-End Through-Wall Respiratory Waveform Reconstruction for 60-GHz MIMO FMCW Radar
authors: Abdel-Kareem Moadi, Chandler Bauder, Vijaysrinivas Rajagopal, Aly E. Fathy
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: This paper presents an end-to-end deep-learning framework that reconstructs respiratory waveforms for a seated subject in through-wall scenarios using 60 GHz MIMO FMCW radar. Conventional through-wall vital-sign pipelines that include localization, spatial filtering, phase extraction, and downstream biomarker analysis, are fragile under wall-induced attenuation and dispersion. This usually promotes lowering the operating frequency yielding inaccurate localizations and noisy waveforms. To address these limitations while retaining the spatial resolution of mmWave radar, we develop a single deep neural network that operates directly on multi-channel range-domain I/Q data and outputs the corresponding time-domain respiration waveform, implicitly performing localization, spatial filtering, and temporal reconstruction. The model is trained and evaluated on 720 minutes of data from three separate subjects positioned at ranges of 1–2 m and azimuths within <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm \mathbf{60}^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> behind drywall, cement board, glass, and wood panels of varying thicknesses. Across all wall types, ranges, and angles, the network attains a mean cosine similarity of 0.92 between reconstructed and ground-truth waveforms and maintains 0.91 on a held-out glass wall material, indicating robustness to moderate changes in wall composition within this experimental setting. Relative to baseline methods, the proposed approach reduces waveform mean-squared error by at least 43% and decreases errors in derived biomarkers, breathing rate, inter-breath interval, and inhalation/exhalation ratio, by up to 69%. These results demonstrate that end-to-end learning on 60 GHz MIMO FMCW radar can provide accurate through-wall respiration waveforms suitable for downstream biomarker analysis while avoiding hand-engineered localization and phase-extraction stages.

title: Energy-aware Adaptive Topology Control for UOWSNs_supp1-3693790.pdf
authors: Chi Lin
publish: N/A
abstract: Underwater Optical Wireless Sensor Network (UOWSN) is a promising technology as it can achieve high-speed communication in underwater environment. However, affected by the uncertainty of complex underwater environment, the network topology of UOWSN is highly dynamic, making it difficult to quantify flexibility or further optimize the topological structure. Additionally, node mobility and energy constraints pose significant challenges to reliable communication. In this paper, we propose a mobility-aware and energy-efficient flexibility-based network topology evaluation model (ME-FEM) for UOWSNs. Then, a reinforcement learning model, termed ME-FEM-DRL, for optimizing the network topology based on ME-FEM is developed, which enables UOWSN to maintain an optimal topology when working in harsh underwater environments. Theoretical analysis proves the NP-hardness of the optimization problem and demonstrates that our algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(\log N)$</tex-math></inline-formula> with optimal parameter boundaries. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method can significantly improve the network flexibility. Compared with the five baseline algorithms in simulations, ME-FEM-DRL reduces normalized topology optimization time cost by 64% and extends network lifetime by 95% on average. Test-bed experiments verify the applicability and effectiveness in practical applications for detecting emergent events.

title: Sensor Data Quality Scoring for MEMS Accelerometers Via Spectral Anomaly Detection
authors: Tim Ruhland, Kilian Müller, Maximilian Lübke, Norman Franchi
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Low-cost MEMS accelerometers are widely deployed for industrial condition monitoring, yet their data streams lack intrinsic quality metrics. This can lead to false alarms caused by sensor artifacts rather than genuine process anomalies, and to diminished trust in condition monitoring systems. This work introduces a lightweight, unsupervised framework for computing a continuous Quality of Sensing (QoS) score. A feedforward autoencoder learns a spectral fingerprint from power spectral density (PSD) and time-domain features during nominal operation – the reconstruction error is then mapped to a normalized QoS parameter. The proposed approach isolates intrinsic MEMS degradation mechanisms. The framework is validated against physically grounded noise simulation: thermo-mechanical noise, vibration rectification error (VRE), and flicker-induced bias instability. The autoencoder detects all three degradation mechanisms, with VRE producing the steepest response. Experiments under laboratory shaker excitation, electric motor vibration, and idle conditions show that real operational data yield the optimal detection sensitivity. The lightweight architecture supports future edge-oriented deployment and improves the trustworthiness of Industrial IoT sensor systems.

title: Cross-Center Surgical Step Recognition in Standardized Training Tasks: Dataset, Baselines, and Transfer Analysis
authors: Georg Wolf, Alexandra Eberenz, Georg Männel, Felicia Bader, Michael Thomaschewski, Freschta Stöhrmann-Malekzada, Tobias Keck, Liv Arning, Nazila Esmaeili, Dennis Kundrat, Jannis Hagenah
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: Accelerating surgical training and improving patient safety requires automated assessment that delivers actionable, step-specific feedback. Standardized box-trainer exercises, with their inherently comparable action steps, offer a natural route towards this goal. Reliable detection of surgical steps is a necessary prerequisite, yet it remains largely unexplored for box-trainer exercises. Crucially, practical deployment requires methods that are invariant to center-domains, i.e. platform, instrument, and camera differences. Methods: We (i) formalize surgical steps for three box-trainer exercises and publicly release the first Multi-center Surgical Training dataset MiST-STEP with step annotations; (ii) benchmark cross-center step recognition with a prevalent two-stage pipeline; (iii) study two self-supervised pretraining schemes—MoCo v2 and a lightweight temporal-order task; and (iv) analyze three finetuning strategies while varying the amount of labeled target center data. Results: Using MiST-STEP (i), we find: (ii) single-center models lose up to 48 percentage points (pp) in macro-F1 on unseen centers; (iii) temporal-order pretraining outperforms MoCo v2 and cuts this deficit by two-thirds; and (iv) finetuning with just two labeled videos per target center removes most of the remaining gap. Conclusion: Our study provides the first comprehensive, cross-center benchmark for step recognition in surgical training and shows that the benefit of self-supervision is highly task-dependent. Our dataset is available at https://gitlab.cc-asp.fraunhofer.de/imte-public/liros/mist. Significance: Center-robust step recognition in box-trainer exercises is essential towards the development of automated feedback systems that aim to standardize training quality and ultimately improve patient outcomes.

title: Large Deviation Analysis for the Reverse Shannon Theorem
authors: Shi-Bing Li, Ke Li, Lei Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: We consider the problem of simulating a noisy channel using noiseless channels with unlimited shared randomness. This can be interpreted as the reverse problem of Shannon’s noisy channel coding theorem. In contrast to previous works, we employ Rényi divergence (with the parameter α ∈ [0,∞]) to measure the level of approximation, and we obtain the reverse Shannon theorem under this measure, which characterizes the Rényi simulation rate, the minimum communication rate required for the Rényi divergence vanishing asymptotically. Our derivation is done by a precise large-deviation analysis. When the communication rate is above the Rényi simulation rate, we provide a complete characterization of the convergence exponent for the Rényi divergence, called the reliability function. When the communication rate is below the Rényi simulation rate, we determine the linear increasing rate for the Rényi divergence, which implies the strong converse exponent for the order-α fidelity.

title: Truncated Sparse Regularization Adjustment Method for Radar Forward-Looking Azimuth Super-Resolution Imaging
authors: Jie Han, Wenping Song, Kang Huang, Yang Wu, Shiyue Shen, Dongsheng Liu, Haiyong Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: The ill-posed problem in radar forward-looking azimuth super-resolution imaging severely impairs the reliability of parameter estimation, which in turn impacts the application accuracy of marine detection and imaging, marine disaster rescue, ship navigation, and so on. For sparse ill-posed problem in radar forward-looking imaging, popular regularization methods lack adaptive regularization parameter selection based on MSE minimization that accounts for the joint effect on variance and bias. The truncated singular value decomposition (TSVD) method tends to misjudge medium-frequency signal as high-frequency noise, resulting in unnecessary signal truncation and the loss of valuable potential information. To address the above-mentioned problems, this study proposes a novel Truncated Sparse Regularization Method (TSRM) that integrates TSVD with sparse regularization. An equivalent weight matrix is constructed to simplify the proposed model. We derive the truncation condition equations for identifying the set of singular values to be truncated by comparing the MSE of the regularized solution with that of the TSVD solution for effective discrimination of medium- and high-frequency signals. Furthermore, a TSRM solution with the partial bias-correction is derived. According to the MSE minimization criterion, the bisection method is employed to adaptively determine the optimal regularization parameter. Extensive high-noise simulation experiments confirm the effectiveness and outperformance of TSRM, offering a feasible solution for ill-posed radar super-resolution imaging.

title: Quantum Hardware-in-The-Loop for Optimal Power Flow in Renewable-Integrated Power Systems
authors: Zeynab Kaseb, Rahul Rane, Aleksandra Lekić, Matthias Möller, Amin Khodaei, Peter Palensky, Pedro P. Vergara
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: Quantum computing has emerged as a promising computational paradigm to address unresolved challenges in the modeling and control of modern power systems. However, most existing studies focus on offline simulations, and a practical framework for validating quantum algorithms in real-time operational environments remains lacking. This study proposes a quantum hardware-in-the-loop framework that integrates a real-time digital simulator with quantum and quantum-inspired hardware to solve combinatorial power flow and optimal power flow formulations under dynamic operating conditions. The proposed framework is validated using the IEEE 9- bus test system and a modified version with integrated solar and wind farms. The results confirm successful integration and convergence within a predefined tolerance. The study also identifies key limitations and challenges, such as limited access to quantum and digital annealers and current scalability limitations, that must be considered in future developments. Nevertheless, the results highlight the potential of quantum computing to significantly enhance the modeling and control of future power systems with high penetration of renewable energy sources.

title: Metasurface-Assisted Ultra-Wideband Radar for High-Sensitivity Non-Contact Respiratory Detection
authors: Nurhayati Nurhayati, Usman Rizqi Iman, Fitri Yuli Zulkifli, Dewiani Dewiani, Azuwa Ali, Mohd Najib Mohd Yasin, Lilik Anifah
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Non-contact respiration monitoring using impulse-radio ultra-wideband (IR-UWB) radar is attractive because of its low transmitted power, fine range resolution, and clutter-rejection capability; however, practical performance at meter-scale standoff is often limited by weak radar–thorax coupling and clutter-dominated echoes. This paper presents a wearable, flexible metasurface that passively enhances the chest-return signature without modifying the radar hardware or increasing transmit power. The proposed metasurface is implemented as a 6 × 6 array of subwavelength unit cells on a 1-mm-thick polyimide substrate and is optimized for low-GHz IR-UWB operation centered at 7.29 GHz under multilayer tissue loading. Full-wave simulations confirm strong impedance matching, low insertion loss, stable broadside field reinforcement, and bending robustness under practical conformal conditions. In addition, the simulated 1-g averaged specific absorption rate (SAR<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1g</sub>) is only 0.0013W/kg under 1Waccepted power, which is far below commonly adopted exposure limits. Experimental validation using a commercial XeThru X4M06 IR-UWB radar at a 150 cm standoff demonstrates that the metasurface-assisted configuration yields more stable respiration-rate tracking and a clearer, more periodic breathing waveform than the baseline case without metasurface. The improvement is maintained for both standing and supine postures under identical radar settings. These results indicate that conformal, fully passive metasurfaces can improve the robustness and practical usability of IR-UWB respiration sensing at meter-scale distances while preserving the inherent low-power and safety advantages of UWB radar.

title: A Lithium-Ion Battery SOC Estimation Method Based on an Attention-Residual Bidirectional LSTM Network
authors: Bowen Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: State of Charge (SOC) is a critical variable in Battery Management Systems (BMS), and its estimation accuracy directly impacts energy management and safety. To address nonlinear dynamics, measurement noise, and long-range temporal dependencies during charging/discharging, this paper proposes an attention-residual bidirectional LSTM (AR-BiLSTM) for SOC estimation. The method takes voltage, current, and temperature as inputs, enhances key temporal representations via time-step attention and input residual projection, and outputs SOC through a regression head. Experimental results demonstrate that this method outperforms others in RMSE, MAE, MAPE, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> metrics, providing a reproducible and lightweight solution for SOC estimation.

title: An Improved Affine Optimization Method for Lowcarbon Dispatch in Multi-Energy Microgrids Considering Source-Load Correlation
authors: Yi Sun, Haotian Yang, Aoqi Gegen
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Under the dual influence of source-load uncertainty and correlation, multi-energy microgrids face challenges in achieving effective energy allocation during operation, thereby compromising operational safety and economic efficiency. To address these issues, this paper proposes an affine real-time optimization dispatch method that accounts for source-load correlation. By extending the deterministic economic dispatch model to the affine domain through affine arithmetic theory, the approach comprehensively considers uncertainties on both sides of the source-load relationship, overcoming the conservatism inherent in traditional interval optimization methods. To account for dependencies between sources and loads, identical noise elements are introduced to characterize source-load correlations, accurately depicting the true scope of their uncertainties. Case studies demonstrate that by incorporating source-load correlations, the proposed method resolves interval dependencies and the conservatism of traditional approaches. It more precisely characterizes uncertainty ranges, yielding more economical and less conservative scheduling solutions with significantly narrowed operational cost intervals.

title: Physics-Informed BiLSTM with Adaptive Gated Fusion for Lithium-Ion Battery State-of-Health Estimation
authors: Zhenkai Yan, Bin Yi, Xuanlin Zhang, Yuqi Ke
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate state of health estimation is critical for reliable battery management, but purely data driven models often generalize poorly and may violate physical degradation trends. This paper proposes a physics informed bidirectional long short term memory framework with adaptive gated fusion for lithium ion battery state of health estimation. The model combines health indicators from routine cycling data with a data branch and a physics branch, and learns to adjust their contributions according to the current degradation stage. A physics informed loss encourages monotonic and physically plausible predictions. Experiments on two public benchmark datasets show robust performance under both data scarce and data rich conditions. The results demonstrate that adaptive fusion improves accuracy, strengthens physical consistency, and provides interpretable degradation behavior.

title: Finite Element Simulation Analysis of Tritium Transport in Lithium-Lead Droplets Inside Vacuum Sieve Tray
authors: Bin Zhang, Yongpeng Lv, Peng Ran, Shixin Ruan, Zhaoting Huang, Cheng Yang, Zhihui Zheng, Kai Huang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Safe, efficient, and large-scale hydrogen isotope (tritium) extraction technology is one of the key prerequisites for the commercial application of fusion energy. In this study, a single-nozzle vacuum sieve tray (VST) device was modeled and simulated using the dilute-phase transport - multiphase flow level-set coupling model in COMSOL Multiphysics 6.3. Four nozzle models with diameters of 0.4 mm, 0.6 mm, 0.8 mm, and 1.0 mm were constructed. The internal gas-liquid two-phase flow behavior and tritium transport processes were systematically analyzed, and the tritium extraction efficiency was calculated. The results indicate that, under given operating conditions, the extraction efficiencies reached 96.4%, 92.7%, 86.6%, and 77.1%, respectively.

title: Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Optimization for Park-Level Integrated Energy Systems
authors: Xinyu Yang, Yu Guo, Sile Hu, Yuan Gao, Bingqiang Liu, Jiaqiang Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To address the multiple sources of uncertainty in park-level integrated energy systems (PIES), including the stochastic outputs of wind and photovoltaic generation as well as diversified multi-energy loads, a two-stage distributionally robust optimal scheduling model based on a Wasserstein ambiguity set is proposed. First, detailed mathematical models are established for the electrolyzer, the combined heat and power (CHP)-hydrogen fuel cell (HFC) thermoelectric coupling system, the methane reactor (MR) integrated with carbon capture and storage (CCS), diversified energy storage systems, and multi-energy loads, enabling an accurate representation of energy flows within the system. On this basis, Wasserstein ambiguity sets are constructed for renewable energy generation and multi-energy loads, leading to a two-stage distributionally robust optimization framework capable of characterizing multiple uncertainties. Finally, by leveraging strong duality theory, the proposed model is reformulated into a tractable mixed-integer linear programming problem. Case study results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively balances economic performance and robustness, and is capable of handling extreme operational scenarios in system operation.

title: Clinical XAI: A Tutorial on Concepts, Methods, and Modalities from Linear Models to LLMs
authors: Munib Mesinovic, Tingting Zhu
publish: ACM Computing Surveys
abstract: None

title: Computation–Accuracy Trade-Off in Service-Oriented Model-Based Control
authors: Hazem Ibrahim, Julius Beerwerth, Lorenz Dörschel, Bassam Alrifaee
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Representing a control system as a Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)—referred to as Service-Oriented Model-Based Control (SOMC)—enables runtime-flexible composition of control loop elements. This paper presents a framework that optimizes the computation–accuracy trade-off by formulating service orchestration as an A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">★</sup> search problem, complemented by Contextual Bayesian Optimization (BO) to tune the multi-objective cost weights. A vehicle longitudinal-velocity control case study demonstrates online, performance-driven reconfiguration of the control architecture. We show that our framework not only combines control and software structure but also considers the real-time requirements of the control system during performance optimization.

title: Brain Tumor Detection and Classification from MRI Scans Using Deep Learning
authors: Prashanshi Yadav, Sumit Shukla, Om Pandey, Nikita
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Brain tumors pose a significant threat to human life, where delayed or inaccurate diagnosis can have serious consequences for patient survival. Recent studies indicate that machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques combined with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) have become the most widely used approaches for automated brain tumor detection, segmentation, grading, and multiclass classification. A wide range of methods, including handcrafted texture descriptors, hybrid ViT-PCA-RF pipelines, entropy-based features, and advanced deep learning architectures such as CNNs, ResNets, CRNNs, WGAN-augmented networks, and transfer learning models including VGG, Xception, and EfficientNet, have consistently demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy, with many studies reporting performance in the range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$95-99 \%$</tex>. Despite these advancements, several challenges remain, including data imbalance, overfitting, limited interpretability, and the dependence on expert-annotated MRI datasets. This review consolidates recent computer-aided diagnosis (CAD)-based approaches, their architectural designs, and performance outcomes. It also highlights emerging trends in explainable AI, hybrid feature fusion, and robust data augmentation techniques, while outlining future research opportunities for developing clinically reliable and fully automated brain tumor diagnostic systems.

title: Secure-BSM: Blockchain-Assisted Collaborative Misbehavior Detection in Vehicular Networks
authors: M M Imran, Yi Zhu, Shiyong Lu
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication relies on periodic Basic Safety Messages (BSMs) to support collision avoidance and cooperative driving, yet remains vulnerable to attacks such as False Data Injection (FDIA), spoofing, replay, Denial-of-Service (DoS), and Sybil attacks. Existing misbehavior detection approaches often exhibit significant limitations, as centralized authorities suffer from high revocation latency and infrastructure dependency, while standalone machine-learning and plausibility methods struggle with high false positive rates due to a lack of collaborative verification. We propose Secure-BSM, a blockchain-assisted architecture that reformulates vehicular misbehavior detection as a collaborative consensus problem to address these limitations. Shifting away from fixed-infrastructure models, Secure-BSM employs local attack detectors first, followed by decentralized validation where vehicles act as lightweight consensus validators. This dynamic approach utilizes a local Pocket Ledger for rapid consensus and a global Root Ledger for tamper-resistant revocation. By aggregating evidence via smart contracts and enforcing limits through Verifiable Rate-limit Tokens (VRTs), the system minimizes false positives and mitigates DoS attacks. Evaluation using SUMO traffic simulations across six attack types demonstrates that collaborative consensus improves detection precision by over 10% and reduces false positives by 77%–85% across various attack scenarios, while the VRT mechanism contains DoS flooding in under 0.2 seconds.

title: Research Review on UAV-Based Air-Ground Small Target Detection and Tracking
authors: Shaoqiong Duan, Shixiao Wu, Xi Wang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: UAV air-to-ground small target detection and tracking is the core enabling technology for low-altitude intelligent perception, widely used in security monitoring, energy inspection, emergency search and rescue, agricultural monitoring and other engineering fields. Current research still faces key challenges: inherent feature scarcity of small targets, strong clutter interference from complex backgrounds, limited on-board computing resources, and insufficient detection-tracking synergy.
