title: Investigating a synergized game design teaching framework for student engagement and reflective design
authors: Peng Tan, Zhihang Guo, Yi Ji, Xiangshi Ren
publish: ACM Transactions on Computing Education
abstract: None

title: Bridging Extreme Data Gaps: SAR-Optical Fusion Guided by Dual-Temporal SAR Differences
authors: Zheng Ge, Xiaoqing Wang, Jie Wang, Ming Tao, Peng Qin, Xiaopeng Chao, Tianwu Lin, Yuting Zhu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Continuous and complete optical observations are essential for agricultural monitoring, phenology analysis, and land-surface assessment. In subtropical and tropical regions, persistent large-scale thick cloud cover frequently causes extended periods with few available optical image pixels, making traditional cloud removal and optical reconstruction methods that depend on spatially and temporally adjacent cloud-free data inapplicable. Similarly, existing SAR-optical fusion and multi-temporal optical approaches require partially cloud-free target imagery. As a result, they become ineffective in scenarios with extreme cloud coverage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\geq$</tex-math></inline-formula>80%) or during prolonged periods of optical data absence. To address this challenge, we propose TDSAR-DR, a dual-temporal SAR-difference-guided optical data reconstruction framework for reconstructing complete optical imagery at the target time without requiring any usable target-time optical observation as model input. Modulated Fusion (TV-SMF) module integrates reference optical information with time labels and temporal change cues extracted by a Time Difference Enhancement Module (TDEM). These dual-temporal SAR derived cues explicitly encode the direction and magnitude of land-surface dynamics, providing physically grounded guidance for a directionally informed and quantitatively consistent transformation toward the target time. Then, a SAR Guided Structural Refinement Module (SGR-M) exploits detailed SAR spatial cues to improve the structural fidelity and spectral consistency of the reconstructed target-time optical image, thereby producing robust results even when optical observations are missing for extended periods. Extensive experiments on subtropical farmland datasets show that TDSAR-DR achieves better qualitative and quantitative results under extreme cloud coverage, outperforming current SOTA SAR-optical fusion models. TDSAR-DR achieves a PSNR of 27.134 and SSIM of 0.947, surpassing other the state-of-the-art methods. More importantly, the framework enables reconstruct continuous cloud free time series, producing temporally consistent optical imagery when optical data are fully missing for long periods.

title: An Adaptive and Lightweight Hybrid Beamforming Design for Massive MIMO
authors: Maoxia Xu, Yuanhui Liang, Chan-Tong Lam, Qingle Wu, Benjamin K. Ng, Jin Hou
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Traditional deep unfolding-based hybrid beamforming designs for Joint Communication and Sensing (JCAS) often suffer from prohibitive computational overheads derived from extensive step-size multiplications and are limited by fixed task weights, which prevent the system from adapting to dynamic channel and sensing environments. To address these challenges, this letter proposes a lightweight intelligent beamforming algorithm comprising two synergistic modules. First, at the algorithmic architecture level, we propose a Multiplication-Free Projected Gradient Ascent (MF-PGA) mechanism that transforms step-size scaling multiplications into hardware-friendly shift-and-add operations based on an Additive Powers-of-Two (APoT) discretization scheme, while preserving the full-precision gradient computation essential for beamforming accuracy. Second, at the optimization strategy level, we propose a meta-learning-based Balancer Neural Network (BalancerNN) that dynamically generates optimal task weights according to real-time channel conditions. Step-size parameter storage requirements are reduced by 87.5%, and all step-size-related multiplications are eliminated during inference. Furthermore, by mitigating the dominance of gradients under high signal-to-noise ratios through dynamic weighting, the method improves sensing accuracy by approximately 30%, achieving a balance between communication and sensing.

title: Reduced-scale test method for dynamic responses of bundle conductor line following ice-shedding
authors: Bo Yan, Xinrui Zhou, Wei Zong, Huachao Deng, Jin Li, Ming Hua, Xiaoqian Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: A reduced-scale test method for dynamic responses of bundle conductor line following ice-shedding is proposed. In this method, hollow cylindrical electromagnets are used to absorb and release mass blocks modelling ice on and ice-shedding from sub-conductors, multiple cameras are utilized to capture the vertical, transverse and torsional vibrations, tension sensors to measure tensions in all sub-conductors, and three-component force sensors to measure the reaction force at hanging points. Without simplifying the bundle conductor line into a single one which was employed in previous works, the proposed reduced-scale model can satisfy the similarities of bending and torsion stiffness at the same time, model the torsional vibration induced by ice-shedding from sub-conductors, and record tension in each sub-conductor. Reduced-scale test platform for a twin-bundle conductor line was established and utilized to study the dynamic characteristics of the twin-bundle conductor line following partial, unzipping and asynchronous ice-shedding from sub-conductors. The method can be extended to investigate dynamic characteristics of bundle conductor lines following any pattern of ice-shedding.

title: CleanAll: Efficient Object Collection Route Planning from LiDAR Point Clouds Transmission in Cleaning Scenarios
authors: Muhammad Nawaz Khan, Sokjoon Lee, Ali Alqazzaz, Mana Saleh Al Reshan, Asadullah Shaikh, Mousa Alalhareth
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Due to technological advancements, today’s world is rapidly changing from traditional and legacy approaches to smarter and more autonomous techniques. Intelligent systems and super-connectivity make possible the era of pervasive services, where these services can be used by anyone, anywhere, and at any time (AAA). These advancements have completely transformed modern living, increasing convenience and providing greater comfort. Robotic cleaning is one such revolutionized area where self-driving robots and automated systems are used to navigate and clean dirt. 3D mapping adapts changes based on visual conditions to detect and remove waste obstacles from the location. These robots are useful for collecting waste and cleaning the floor. In this research work, we have proposed a LiDAR-based cleaning robotic system called ”CleanAll: Efficient Object Collection Route Planning from LiDAR Point Clouds in the Cleaning Scenario.” It is an automotive system that calculates an optimized path from its 3D point-cloud data to collect some objects through its object collector. CleanAll moves over the optimized path using point cloud data along with a dual-antenna GNSS receiver and an IMU. A defined data set is already used to compare and detect object shapes, and an extended Kalman filter is used to obtain the robot’s position. CleanAll is more responsive, and its quick decision-making makes it a better option for cleaning scenarios where dirt and trash are scattered throughout areas. The optimized path used by the robot is not only useful in finding the shortest path, but it also saves time and consumes the minimum energy by following these short paths for trash collection. CleanAll is evaluated in three different scenarios, collecting all objects (scattered/line-by-line) with an average standard error rate of 0.4593. To assess performance on other parameters, it has been tested with two standard datasets, ACIN and IPA. The TPRs have 0.75, and the FPRs have been recorded as 0.40 in ACIN, while the TPRs in IPA have 0.7 and 0.4 in the same scenario. This means that CleanAll performs better using the ACIN data set, achieving higher TPRs with lower FPRs. All these experiments illustrate that CleanAll performs better using the ACIN data set, which aims to recognize and clean trash more precisely and efficiently.

title: Collaborative Charging Optimization for Wireless Rechargeable Sensor Networks via Heterogeneous Mobile Chargers
authors: Jianhang Yao, Hui Kang, Geng Sun, Jiahui Li, Hongjuan Li, Jiacheng Wang, Yinqiu Liu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Despite the rapid proliferation of Internet of Things applications driving widespread wireless sensor network (WSN) deployment, traditional WSNs remain fundamentally constrained by persistent energy limitations that severely restrict network lifetime and operational sustainability. Wireless rechargeable sensor networks (WRSNs) integrated with wireless power transfer (WPT) technology emerge as a transformative paradigm, theoretically enabling unlimited operational lifetime. In this paper, we investigate a heterogeneous mobile charging architecture that strategically combines an automated aerial vehicle (AAV) and a ground smart vehicle (SV) in heterogeneous deployment scenarios to collaboratively exploit the superior mobility of the AAV and extended endurance of the SV for energy distribution. We formulate a multi-objective optimization problem that simultaneously addresses the dynamic balance of heterogeneous charger advantages, charging efficiency versus mobility energy consumption trade-offs, and real-time adaptive coordination under time-varying network conditions. This problem presents significant computational challenges due to its high-dimensional continuous action space, non-convex optimization landscape, and dynamic environmental constraints. To address these challenges, we propose the improved heterogeneous agent trust region policy optimization (IHATRPO) algorithm that integrates a self-attention mechanism for enhanced complex environmental state processing and employs a Beta sampling strategy to achieve unbiased gradient computation in continuous action spaces. Simulation results demonstrate that IHATRPO achieves a 51% performance improvement over the original HATRPO, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline algorithms while substantially decreasing sensor node mortality rate and improving charging system efficiency.

title: Decentralized Adaptive Delayed Control of Large-Scale Nonlinear Systems with Random Couplings and Biased Measurements
authors: Yuxin Guo, Jin Zhang, Chen Peng
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: We study the decentralized adaptive delayed control of large-scale systems, where the output with a constant but unknown bias that may be induced by a sensor error is available for the measurements. Different from the existing studies in the presence of constant and small coupling between the subsystems, we consider a random and large coupling modeled by an N-valued random variable. Under the premise that state-feedback can stabilize the subsystem for small enough coupling, we employ finite differences to approximate the unavailable states giving rise to a delayed output-feedback. Moreover, we design an adaptive law via the σ-modification method to handle the unknown bias. By applying the Lyapunov-Krasovskii approach, we present sufficient conditions for input-to-state stability (ISS) in terms of linear matrix inequalities (LMIs). Then a design criterion for the gains of the delayed feedback and adaptive law is presented, where the linear constraints involved in the design procedure are transformed into a minimization problem. Furthermore, we suggest the sampled-data implementation by using the consecutive sampled-data. The effectiveness of the proposed method is verified by two examples including eight inverted pendulums.

title: Global Token-Driven Multi-Scale Forecasting with Dual-Attention Fusion for Multivariate Time Series
authors: Rong Chai, Zhiqiang Fan, Caiyi Yang, Yanchen Liu, Hong Chen, Qianbin Chen
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Real-world multivariate time series often exhibit multi-scale temporal dynamics and intricate inter-variable dependencies, making long-term forecasting particularly challenging. In this work, we propose a global token-driven multi-scale forecasting framework with dual-attention fusion. To capture multi-scale periodic patterns, the input sequence is segmented into multi-scale patches based on candidate periods, with the dominant ones derived via fast Fourier transform. Global tokens are then introduced as shared representations to integrate information from the temporal and variable dimensions, and a multi-scale patch-token interaction module is designed to establish interactions between the patches and global tokens, enabling the capture and aggregation of temporal dependencies across different scales. A dual-attention fusion module, employing both self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms, is then proposed to capture intrinsic and context-aware variable correlation among variables. To integrate cross-variable and cross-scale information into patch representations, a global information fusion module is designed. Finally, a period-aware weighting approach is devised to adaptively fuse the multi-scale predictions. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art performance across various real-world datasets.

title: Inter-class Enhanced Feature Correlation for Adaptive Online Knowledge Distillation
authors: Jianping Gou, Hongfang Zhu, Lan Du, Weihua Ou, Wu Chen, Zhang Yi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Online knowledge distillation has emerged as a powerful technique for training multiple student networks concurrently through online learning, fostering their performance enhancement. However, existing methods in this domain predominantly concentrate on single feature maps at the feature knowledge level, overlooking the utilization of relational knowledge during the online distillation training process. We argue that relational knowledge, which encapsulates rich and valuable information, merits exploration. We believe that leveraging relational knowledge in online knowledge distillation can significantly improve model performance and enhance model generalization. Thus, we introduce a novel online knowledge distillation framework termed Inter-class Enhanced Feature Correlation for Adaptive Online Knowledge Distillation (ICOKD), which emphasizes the construction of feature correlations from an inter-class perspective. Specifically, we estimate the correlation between different inter-class samples within the feature spaces of each network and facilitate the transfer of relational knowledge between them. Before computing feature correlation, we employ a feature enhancement module to enrich features, thereby providing richer information for the calculation of inter-class sample correlation. Subsequently, inter-class samples are selected from the enriched features within each student network to calculate feature correlation, enabling students to learn from each other. Moreover, we design an adaptive distillation module at the logits level to facilitate effective learning in each student. By integrating these three modules into a unified training framework, we promote the generalization of student models. Experimental evaluations on various datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed ICOKD method, achieving a test accuracy of 74.21% on CIFAR-100 for ResNet32. Our code is publicly avaliable at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/9jiujiu9/ICOKD</uri>.

title: Anti-Interference Coverage Optimization for UWSNs in Time-Varying Underwater Acoustic Environments Based on the DynCov-BiS Algorithm
authors: Xinmiao Lu, Luyu Jia, Qiong Wu, Yipin Wang, Kangji Wang, Jiashuai Sun
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Underwater Acoustic Wireless Sensor Networks (UWSNs) are core support for key marine development fields. However, sensing degradation caused by the coupling of environmental factors such as sound speed profile variations and multipath effects severely restricts their stable operation. Existing power compensation studies have shortcomings including failure to consider multiple degradation triggers and lack of an end-to-end adaptation mechanism. To address this, this paper proposes the DynCov-BiS algorithm and constructs a closed-loop modular distributed power compensation system: it analyzes the degradation mechanism dominated by sound speed profile variations and establishes a quantitative model between degradation degree and energy loss; deduces dynamic minimum power demand and adjustment thresholds by integrating degradation indicators, remaining node energy and hardware constraints; and achieves power optimization through sound speed time-series prediction and distributed game-theoretic cooperation. Experiments show that the algorithm maintains a coverage rate of over 85% under multiple interference scenarios with low power consumption. Compared with fixed-power strategies and traditional algorithms, it exhibits superior performance in coverage stability, energy efficiency and network lifetime, effectively balancing sensing performance and node endurance.

title: The Application of Robotics and Automation in Smart Agricultural Manufacturing: Achieving Precision Operations and Maximizing Yield
authors: Yu Hai-Yang, Yang Tao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper introduces a novel approach to the application of robotics and automation within Smart Agricultural Manufacturing—an overarching industrial paradigm that elevates traditional precision farming into a fully autonomous, deterministically controlled production ecosystem. Rather than treating automated farming as a collection of isolated mechanical tasks, we introduce a scalable, hybrid multi-modal framework focused on robotic systems to optimize the entire production lifecycle, from automated soil preparation to robotic harvesting and post-processing. By integrating Cyber-Physical System (CPS) data streams-including real-time telemetry from autonomous vehicles, robotic actuator data, environmental sensors, and logistics-our system establishes a high-fidelity digital shadow of both the agricultural environment and the automated machinery operating within it. Tailored for large-scale robotic fleets, the framework utilizes hierarchical feature learning to segment critical autonomous operation phases.We propose a dynamic robotic task allocation algorithm, inspired by Dijkstra's shortest path, to engineer optimal ``field-to-factory'' automated workflows, minimizing operational latency and maximizing machine uptime. To handle the high-dimensionality of data from on-board robotic sensors (e.g., LiDAR, hyperspectral cameras), a novel geometric feature selection mechanism eliminates redundancy while preserving latent indicators of crop health and operational efficiency. These refined attributes are projected into a probabilistic state space, allowing individual robotic units or automated zones to exhibit distinct performance signatures. Central to our approach is the construction of an operational similarity graph, which facilitates community detection across the robotic network. These clusters reveal shared optimization strategies, such as superior robotic cultivation protocols and resilient automation configurations. Finally, a ranking-based recommendation engine synergizes local robotic sensor data with global cluster insights to drive prescriptive actions for the autonomous fleet. Validated on an extensive dataset of over 1 million records detailing robotic field operations, our framework achieves a superior average yield forecasting accuracy of 89.2% (a 14% improvement over leading baselines) and a Balanced Error Rate (BER) of 0.5665 for production clustering. These scale-independent metrics prove its efficacy in achieving precision operations and transforming traditional farming into a resilient, high-yield automated manufacturing system.

title: DetO-RAN: Dual-Timescale Resource Allocation for Flows in Deterministic Open RAN
authors: Kai Qiao, Hao Xiong, Qinding Wang, Zihao Zhang, Hongchao Wang, Weiting Zhang, Dong Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: Deterministic transmission and computation are essential for open radio access networks (O-RANs) to support latency-critical and computation-intensive applications. How ever, existing O-RAN and time-sensitive networking integrations mainly focus on deterministic guarantees in wired fronthaul transmission, while lacking a unified mechanism to coordinate wireless transmission, wired transmission, and computation. This limitation makes it difficult to provide bounded end-to-end latency under time-varying wireless channels and increasing computational demands, which reduces flow scheduling success rates and causes resource wastage. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical deterministic O-RAN framework, named DetO RAN, which ensures deterministic transmission and computation for flows through a unified queuing and resource allocation mechanism. DetO-RAN introduces a three-queue model (i.e., wireless, wired, and computing queues) to support transmission and computation of flows, and establishes a closed-loop resource allocation process based on model training, inference, and updating. Based on this framework, we formulate a multi-objective optimization problem aiming to maximize the flow scheduling success rate and resource utilization. Due to the time-varying wireless channels, strongly coupled resources, and network dynamics, traditional optimization algorithms are inefficient. Thus, we decouple the problem into a radio resource block (RB) allocation subproblem as well as a time slot and computing re source allocation subproblem. Furthermore, we propose a meta learning-based dual-timescale resource allocation (ML-DTRA) algorithm to solve them. Specifically, ML-DTRA performs RB allocation via a graph neural network at a small timescale and makes time slot and computing resource allocation decisions via deep reinforcement learning at a large timescale. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the ML-DTRA algorithm significantly improves the scheduling success rate by 12.7% and the resource utilization by 15.9% compared with state-of-the art benchmarks, showing its effectiveness in supporting end-to end deterministic transmission and computation while improving resource efficiency in dynamic O-RAN environments.

title: Three-Dimensional Geometric Information Based Grasping Points Computation and Trajectory Planning for Human-Mimic Robotic Arm
authors: Monika Vyas, Amit Kumar, Ashish Kumar Bhandari
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The computation of grasping points and trajectory planning are crucial phases for autonomous grasping of unknown real-time objects using robotic arms, particularly for multi-fingered human-like manipulators. These operations should be accurate and fast enough to match the real-time requirements. Although deep learning techniques yield satisfactory grasping performance but they are often limited to trained object classes and require heavy on-board computation. Existing geometric grasp planning approaches typically rely on simplified grippers or object symmetry assumptions, which limit their applicability to low degree-of-freedom (DOF) hands. In this paper, we present a fast and accurate geometry-based novel method to compute grasping points for a human like five-fingers robotic arm to grasp any random object. The proposed method explicitly computes thumb and finger grasping points using visible surface geometry extracted from RGB-D data without assuming object symmetry or object-specific models. A subdivision-based trajectory planning and smoothing approach is adapted and integrated into the framework to guide the fingertips smoothly toward the computed grasping points for stable grasping. A deep learning–based instance segmentation module is used only for object detection and masking, whereas grasp synthesis and trajectory planning are entirely geometry-driven. The proposed method is implemented and experimentally validated on a 20-DOF in-house developed robotic arm using a Kinect RGB-D camera. Experimental results demonstrate approximately 12% reduction in cumulative grasping point error and 17% improvement in grasp execution time compared with state-of-the-art methods. Statistical validation using a two-sample t-test confirms the superiority of the proposed method with 95% confidenc

title: Terahertz Band for High-Altitude Platform Station Communications: Performance Analysis, Potential, and Challenges
authors: Mikail Erdem, Khalid Qaraqe, Mohamed-Slim Alouini
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: The Terahertz (THz) band (0.1-10 THz) attracts attention due to its massive bandwidth, potentially enabling ultra-high data rate with ultra-low latency. High-altitude platform stations (HAPSs) are envisioned as nodes of a non-terrestrial network (NTN) that complements terrestrial networks, particularly for connecting the unconnected and addressing temporary needs, such as disaster relief. THz HAPS communication is a promising solution for NTN, since HAPS is quasi-stationary in nature, located in the stratosphere, thereby avoiding beam misalignment and molecular absorption loss issues in the THz band. In this paper, we provide an overview of THz band HAPS communication, considering HAPS-to-ground, HAPS-to-HAPS, HAPS-to-satellite, and HAPS-to-unmanned aerial vehicle links. We present simulation results of outage probability, ergodic capacity, and spectral efficiency under beam misalignment fading and turbulence fading in comparison to millimeter wave and sub-6 GHz bands. In addition, we summarize existing THz HAPS studies to reveal ongoing efforts. Moreover, we discuss open issues and research directions for THz band HAPS-related links. Overall, this paper draws a clear picture of THz HAPS communication by reporting its potential and challenges.

title: Reframing Participation: Inclusion and Democracy in Participatory Design
authors: Beatrice Villari, Peter Scupelli
publish: Proceedings of the 19th Participatory Design Conference 2026, Vol. 1: Full Papers
abstract: None

title: LPGNet: Learnable Prompt-Guided Network for Low-dose Contrast Computed Tomography Angiography Imaging
authors: Yuehuai Zhang, Zhan Wu, Tianling Lyu, Jiaxi Hu, Jianan Cui, Shangwen Yang, Yang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Iodinated contrast agents (ICAs) are essential in computed tomography angiography (CTA) for highlighting vascular structures. However, their clinical application is facing growing limitations due to the risks of contrast-induced nephropathy. Low-dose contrast agent CTA (LDCTA) vascular imaging provides a safer option by utilizing a smaller amount of contrast agent. Current LDCTA vascular imaging methods face challenges in maintaining stable vessel intensity and differentiating vessel boundaries from the surrounding anatomical background due to the complex topological structure. To address these challenges, we propose LPGNet, an unpaired vascular reconstruction framework that integrates Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) with anatomical intensity consistency constraints. The CLIP prior facilitates both differentiation between low-contrast/well-enhanced vascular images and identification of fine-grained structural details, thereby enhancing the optimization of the enhancement network. Learnable prompts guide CLIP to capture contrast-specific visual semantics, while an intensity constraint loss ensures enhanced aortic regions maintain clinically preferable intensity ranges. An iterative optimization strategy further refines both the enhancement network and prompts through progressive semantic alignment. Extensive experiments on both simulated and clinical datasets demonstrate that LPGNet outperforms state-of-the-art paired and unpaired methods in terms of vascular contrast, anatomical detail preservation, and diagnostic utility. These results highlight its potential as a practical solution for contrast-limited vascular imaging scenarios.

title: Green Synthesis of Multiphase Gd/Gd₂O₃ Nanoparticles Using Hazelnut Husk (Corylus colurna) Extract for Biomedical Applications CR-16
authors: Bahar Gungordu, Dilara Akkus, Nilay Gunduz Akdogan, Canan Bağcı, Ozan Akdogan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have attracted considerable attention in recent years due to their potential applications in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), targeted drug delivery, and cancer treatment. In particular, gadolinium-based nanoparticles are widely investigated as MRI contrast agents due to their high magnetic features and paramagnetic characteristics. In this work, Gd/Gd₂O₃ nanoparticles were synthesized by an environmentally friendly green synthesis approach, in which hazelnut husk extract was used as a natural reducing and stabilizing agent. During the synthesis, the hazelnut (Corylus colurna) husk, the outer leaf of the hazelnut shell, and a bio-waste extract were used as a reducing and stabilizing agent. The formation of a dual-phase crystalline structure consisting of Gd and cubic Gd₂O₃ with an average crystal size of approximately 21 nm was confirmed. The coexistence of metallic Gd and Gd₂O₃ phases effectively improves the magnetic response of NPs. FTIR spectra showed functional groups obtained from plant-derived biomolecules that play a role in NPs stabilization, and the formation of Gd₂O₃ was supported by Gd–O vibration bands. TGA and VSM results, respectively, showed the presence of organic residues from the green synthesis process and weak ferromagnetic behavior with low coercivity, supporting the soft magnetic properties of the synthesized Gd/Gd₂O₃ NPs. The cytotoxicity of Polyethylene glycol (PEG)-coated Gd/Gd₂O₃ NPs were evaluated on SH-SY5Y human neuroblastoma cells using MTT assay, and a concentration-dependent decrease in cell viability between 10–100 μg/mL was observed, revealing moderate cytotoxic effects at higher concentrations. These results indicate that the synthesized NPs exhibit promising potential for biomedical applications, including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), targeted drug delivery, and other nanomedicine-related applications.

title: Bilayer Stator BLDC Motor With Auxiliary Stator Driven by One Electronic Speed Controller for Torque Ripple and Vibration Reduction
authors: Mohammadreza Hojati, Subhas C. Mukhopadhyay, Richard Han, Foad Taghizadeh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Brushless DC (BLDC) motors driven by conventional six-step electronic speed controllers (ESCs) exhibit commutation torque ripple and vibration due to nonideal back-electromotive force and phase-current distortion. This article proposes a bilayer stator BLDC motor in which a mechanically phase-shifted auxiliary stator is introduced to generate a compensating torque component during commutation. Two drive configurations are investigated: a dual-ESC configuration with independent excitation of the main and auxiliary stators, and a single-ESC configuration in which phase-paired main and auxiliary windings are connected in parallel and driven by one three-phase inverter. The topology is evaluated using transient finite-element analysis, circuit-level interpretation, and hardware-based experimental validation. For the investigated six-slot/four-pole inrunner prototype, the proposed single-ESC configuration achieves an approximately 50% reduction in peak-to-peak electromagnetic torque ripple in simulation compared with a conventional BLDC motor under identical conditions. Experiments using the same fabricated motor reconfigured between conventional and proposed modes further show reduced no-load vibration at 500 and 1000 rpm under identical Hall-sensor-based six-step commutation conditions. Accordingly, the torque-ripple reduction claim is established primarily from simulation, while the experiments provide indirect hardware-based evidence consistent with the same mitigation mechanism. The results indicate that the proposed bilayer stator concept provides a practical structural approach to commutation torque-ripple mitigation, although the preferred drive configuration remains dependent on motor geometry, excitation strategy, and selected performance metric.

title: MMFormer: Macro-Micro Transformer for Small-Sample Classification of Mars Hyperspectral Image
authors: Tingrui Feng, Yali Wang, Chuan Fu, Bo Du, Fulin Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Mars hyperspectral image classification (Mars HSIC) provides invaluable information for accurate mineral identification and Martian geology studies. However, this task remains challenging owing to scarce labeled samples, high spectral similarity and environmental noise. Capturing both local details and global contexts is crucial for this task, yet Convolutional Neural Networks struggle with global dependencies, while Transformers tend to neglect fine local details and incur unnecessary computational burden. To address this issue, we propose MMFormer, a Macro-Micro Transformer tailored for Mars HSIC. With a dual-branch architecture, our method adopts the macro-kernel perception and micro-kernel modulation strategy to synergize global contexts with local details in both spatial and spectral domains. Specifically, the spatial branch adopts macro-kernel perception to capture long-range spatial dependencies, accompanied by micro-kernel modulation for local detail extraction. Meanwhile, the spectral branch utilizes an analogous strategy to perceive long-range spectral correlations and modulate fine-grained spectral variations simultaneously. To mitigate severe background interference, we introduce a Center Prompt Attention (CPA) module that injects a center-focused prompt to adaptively reweight attention toward the central pixel. To integrate these distinct feature representations, a Cross Weighted Fusion (CWF) module is constructed to dynamically fuse complementary spatial and spectral information. Extensive experiments on three Mars hyperspectral datasets demonstrate that MMFormer achieves strong performance and robustness under limited labeled samples and complex environmental conditions. The code is available at RUI-FT/TGRS 2026 MMFormer.

title: A Systolic Random Increment Memory Architecture for Unary Computing
authors: Hongrui Guo, Yongwei Zhao, Zhangmai Li, Yifan Hao, Tianrui Ma, Mo Zou, Chang Liu, Xinkai Song, Xiaqing Li, Zidong Du, Rui Zhang, Qi Guo, Zhiwei Xu, Tianshi Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Unary computing, whose core arithmetics (e.g., multiplication) require only simple logic gates, has enabled efficient DNN processing, especially on strictly power-constrained devices. However, unary computing still confronts the power efficiency bottleneck for buffering unary bitstreams. The buffering of unary bitstreams requires accumulating bits into large bitwidth binary numbers. The large bitwidth binary number needs to activate all bits per cycle in case of carry propagation. As a result, the accumulation process accounts for 32%-70% of the power budget. To push the boundary of power efficiency, we propose Cambricon-U, a systolic random increment memory architecture featuring efficient accumulation. By leveraging skew number data format, Cambricon-U only activates no more than three bits (instead of all bits) from each number per accumulating cycle. Experimental results show that Cambricon-U reduces 51% power on unary accumulation, and improves 1.18-1.45× energy efficiency over uSystolic, the SOTA unary computing scheme baseline, with -1.9%∼+0.77% area overhead. Further, we propose Cambricon-UX, which exploits bit-level sparsity in unary computing to save energy. Compared to Cambricon-U, Cambricon-UX achieves 1.28-2.54× energy efficiency.

title: Hierarchical PatchMatch Depth Estimation with Double-Layer MST Propagation for Urban 3D Reconstruction from Satellite Imagery
authors: Xuetao Zheng, Jingxue Wang, Xiao Xie, Jingyi Mao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Stereo matching is a crucial technique in 3D reconstruction because it generates complete and robust depth maps. However, most traditional methods are typically designed for general scenes or aerial imagery and transfer poorly to large scale urban satellite imagery. Due to the significant viewpoint differences in a satellite stereo pair, occlusions are more frequent in urban scenes with dense man-made buildings, making accurate disparity estimation difficult. This paper proposed HPM-DMST, a hierarchical PatchMatch-based depth estimation method with double-layer MST propagation for satellite imagery. First, a stereo image pyramid is generated via downsampling, and multi-scale cost volumes are built by applying robust matching costs at each pyramid level. To exploit building geometry and reduce ambiguity in weakly textured regions, the rectified images at each level are segmented into multiple superpixels, and these volumes are optimized by combining cross-based local multipoint filtering with cross-scale cost regularization (CLMF-CSCR), followed by multi-path cost aggregation. Then, at the lower-resolution levels, the Group Color Hierarchical PatchMatch Filter (GC-HPMF) rapidly estimates the disparity plane prior. Finally, the double-layer MST (DMST) propagation model propagates and refines these planes across both MST layers, and the final disparity map is obtained by minimizing the global energy function. Subsequently, the corresponding dense 3D point clouds and digital surface models (DSMs) are reconstructed via triangulation. Qualitative and quantitative experiments on US3D, WHU-Stereo, and GaoFen-7 satellite stereo pairs demonstrate that our method outperforms these compared stereo matching approaches, especially in maintaining depth discontinuities and managing weakly textured regions.

title: Biogeographical Trends of Benthic Habitats in Guam from WorldView-2/3 Observations Using Advanced Deep Learning
authors: Talha Mahmood, Bingqing Liu, Charles Sabin, Megan K. La Peyre, Coy A. LeBlanc, Lyndsay L. Rankin, Kevin J. Buffington, Karen M. Thorne, Cara Lin, Xu Yuan
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Benthic habitats, such as seagrass meadows and coral reefs, are vital components of shallow coastal ecosystems, but they are increasingly threatened by anthropogenic and climatic stressors. Traditional remote sensing and manual classification methods often struggle with fine-scale heterogeneity, label scarcity, computational inefficiency, and lack of operational capability for operational benthic mapping. To address these challenges, we introduce the Benthic Habitat U-Net (BH-UNet), a lightweight deep learning framework applied to high-resolution WorldView-2/3 multispectral imagery to map complex coastal benthic environments in Guam's Hagåtña Bay and Manell-Geus. The model integrates channel-wise attention and bilinear upsampling to enhance feature extraction while maintaining computational efficiency. Evaluated on a spatially and temporally independent datasets, BH-UNet demonstrates strong cross-site generalization, achieving patch-level Intersection-over-Union scores exceeding 0.98 and Dice coefficients above 0.99. These results demonstrate superior boundary preservation and broader adaptability compared to the baseline architectures, which exhibited significant overfitting and limited generalization. The efficiency of the framework enabled its application to time-series imagery, revealing interannual fluctuations in seagrass extent from 2010 to 2024. Notably, sharp declines in seagrass were observed in 2014 and 2015, which coincided with periods of strong marine heatwave intensity. These patterns highlight the value of high-temporal-resolution seagrass observations for capturing episodic ecological responses that would otherwise be difficult to detect, underscoring their potential for scalable and operational coastal monitoring. This work advances artificial intelligence (AI)-driven marine conservation by balancing computational efficiency with ecological precision, offering a pathway for adaptive management in data-limited coastal ecosystems.

title: Distributed Quantum Learning over Near-term Devices: Convergence Analysis and Security Design
authors: Atit Pokharel, Shaba Shaon, Thomas Morris, Dinh C. Nguyen
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Distributed quantum learning (DQL) has emerged as a promising paradigm to scale quantum-enhanced machine learning by interconnecting multiple quantum devices. However, for efficient real-world deployment, it is essential to characterize how DQL converges under practical scenarios while simultaneously safeguarding multi-device quantum infrastructures from evolving security threats. Addressing these aspects in an integrated manner is key to ensuring both performance and resilience in large-scale DQL systems. Therefore, this paper presents a new DQL study where our innovation lies in: (i) conducting a holistic convergence analysis for DQL under practical settings, i.e., partial device participation, non-convex loss functions, and heterogeneous data distributions, (ii) developing a novel multi-layered post-quantum cryptographic architecture with a quantum neural network-powered adaptive mechanism that monitors conditions, evaluates threats, and adjusts parameters across three National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)-compliant levels. Our theoretical framework and empirical validation reveal two key insights: (i) the derived convergence bound uncovers a fundamental trade-off between convergence rate, measurement shots, and the size of the participating device subset; and (ii) findings from our evaluations on a physical testbed modeling quantum control architectures expose the performance limitations of static post-quantum security, while confirming that our adaptive framework effectively mitigates these overheads to preserve overall system efficiency. Specifically, the hardware experiments demonstrate that our dynamic security mechanism reduces total security execution time by approximately 49% relative to static high-security baselines, while maintaining a threat detection accuracy of over 91%. Furthermore, extensive simulations validate our theoretical analysis, showing strong agreement between predicted and observed convergence trends. The coupling of these two stages ensures both theoretically grounded performance optimization and adaptive threat resilience, enabling efficient, secure, and scalable DQL deployment.

title: Analytical Framework for Power System Strength
authors: Ignacio Ponce, Federico Milano
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: This paper proposes a general framework to evaluate power system strength. The formulation features twelve indicators, grouped in three dynamical orders, that quantify the resistance of bus voltage phasors and their first and second order rates of change to sudden current injection changes. To quantify such changes, the paper introduces a novel finite differentiation technique, which we named Delta operator, able to properly capture “jumps” of algebraic variables and utilizes the recently developed concept of complex frequency. The paper also shows how the proposed framework can be systematically applied to any system device, and provides a variety of examples based on synchronous machines, converters, and loads models are given. Numerical results in a benchmark system validate the exactness of the formulation.

title: Enhanced Thermal Management in GaN Superlattice Castellated Field Effect Transistors
authors: Pharyanshu Kachhawa, Seungwon Park, Jagori Raychaudhuri, Jonathen Malen, Tariq Jamil, Tazbiul Hasan, Asif Khan, Jeong-kyu Kim, Mohamadali Malakoutian, Srabanti Chowdhury, James W. Pomeroy, Brian Novak, R. S. Howell, Shamima Afroz, Martin Kuball
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: AlGaN/GaN multi-channel Super Lattice Castellated Field Effect Transistors (SLCFETs) have shown great potential for next generation RF applications. We demonstrate a 2.44× reduction in thermal resistance reduction in AlGaN/GaN multi-channel SLCFETs by replacing the standard AlGaN buffer with a high thermal conductivity AlN buffer layer and adding a poly-crystalline diamond overcoat layer. The thermal conductivity of each material in the device stack was measured using time-domain and frequency-domain thermoreflectance techniques, including 223<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+21</sup><sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−18</sub> W/m·K for 2.2 μm-thick AlN buffer versus 38<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+5.5</sup><sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−5</sub> W/m·K for the reference 1.8 μm AlGaN buffer. Device thermal resistance was reduced from 29 mm°C/W to 11.9 mm°C/W by combining the AlN buffer and poly-crystalline diamond overcoat layer, determined using Raman thermography together with 3-D thermal simulation. The corresponding 2.44× increase RF power density capability enabled by improved thermal management can unlock the next-generation ultra-high-power RF devices.

title: Optimizing Server Allocation for Multi-Tenant GPU Inference with Dynamic Batching: A Queueing Theoretic Approach
authors: Ryota Okuyama, Yoshiaki Inoue
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cloud Computing
abstract: Efficient GPU resource management is essential for multi-tenant deep neural network (DNN) inference plat forms, where dynamic batching is widely used to improve throughput and energy efficiency. However, batching introduces a trade-off between latency and batch size, particularly when diverse applications compete for a limited server pool. Existing measurement-based tuning approaches often require re-profiling system performance under varying arrival rates, which renders them impractical for dynamic production environments. This paper introduces a queueing-theoretic framework for application aware server allocation under energy constraints. We model each application's server using a realistic dynamic batching policy and Erlang interarrival processes, arising from Poisson job arrivals and round-robin job dispatching. We derive closed-form approximations for expected batch size and job latency. These approximations enable a convex optimization that minimizes the maximum average latency across applications while satisfying batch-dependent energy efficiency constraints. The proposed method eliminates the need for exhaustive simulation or empirical profiling across different arrival rates, making it well-suited for dynamic, real-time server provisioning. Experiments on a real GPU server with ResNet-50 and RetinaNet demonstrate that our approach closely approximates optimal allocation strategies while significantly reducing computational overhead. The framework supports both server-based and serverless deployments and is readily applicable to modern DNN inference platforms.

title: Real Mine Data-Driven Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Coordinated Ventilation Fan Operational Scheduling
authors: Jinfeng Li, Qiang Suo, Qiyuan Liu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Ventilation fan operation scheduling is a core problem of mine-site operational management. This paper proposes a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL)-based scheduling framework that leverages historical operational data to improve decision-making and address key shortcomings of conventional evolutionary methods, including low efficiency, limited generalizability, and high operational complexity. Using a graph neural network architecture, a performance calculation model for combined multi-fan operating configurations is constructed by integrating historical observations with prior knowledge of fan characteristic curves. We formulate the scheduling of multiple fan ventilation units as a set of parallel decisions, constrained by airflow demand and operating rules. The objective is to reduce costs and increase efficiency while ensuring that the approach remains practical and simple to implement. To improve generalization, a MARL framework is developed that explicitly models variability in the operating conditions. A comparative evaluation against evolutionary algorithms shows that, among the reinforcement learning methods examined, the trained MARL approach adjusts reliably to fluctuating airflow delivery conditions and demonstrates strong generalization across scenarios. Relative to the observed operational schedule, the proposed approach yields substantial savings, reducing operating costs by more than CNY 43,900 and lowering energy consumption by over 47,000 kWh. Compared with evolutionary algorithms, the reinforcement learning-based approach produces decisions that better satisfy operational constraints and operational logic while substantially improving computational efficiency, achieving a planning speedup of more than 50 times.

title: Dual Time-Scale Framework for Joint Optimization of Service Caching and UAV Trajectory based on Self Attention Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Xuewei Zhang, Jiantao Li, Yuan Ren, Fan Jiang, Junxuan Wang, Jie Zeng
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Nowadays, Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)-assisted Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) has been recognized as a promising technique for flexibly handling computation tasks in 5G advanced and 6G networks. This paper investigates the joint optimization of service caching and computation offloading within a dual time-scale framework. We maximize the caching utility and minimize the task processing delay by jointly optimizing service caching policies, UAV flight trajectory, and computation offloading decisions. Specifically, for the long-term problem, we use the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model to predict user preferences, and propose a Lagrangian dual decomposition based algorithm. For the short-term problem, a self-attention based Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) algorithm is designed. Under the Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) framework, this algorithm integrates a multi-head self-attention mechanism with curriculum learning. Each UAV is regarded as an agent, and a self-attention encoder is integrated at the front-end of each Actor network. This enables the agent to dynamically capture the relative importance between itself and all users, and context-aware features are extracted to make more intelligent and trajectory designs. Through extensive simulation experiments, the long-term algorithm yields the performance improvements of 76.5% in cache hit rate and 66% in caching utility, as compared to the second best baseline. In dynamic scenarios, the short-term algorithm achieves a 16% reduction in total processing latency with respect to the proximal policy optimization policy.

title: PhishLumos: From a Single URL to Campaign-Level Phishing Mitigation
authors: Daiki Chiba, Hiroki Nakano, Takashi Koide
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Phishing campaigns increasingly evade automated scanners through cloaking and selective blocking, which prevents page retrieval and forces URL-by-URL triage. We present PhishLumos, a campaign-level mitigation system that starts from a single seed URL and treats evasion as a pivot signal. When content is missing, deceptive, or inaccessible, PhishLumos pivots to infrastructure evidence, including shared domains, IP addresses, certificates, and historical scan metadata. It consolidates observations into a typed property graph knowledge base with a deterministic, idempotent merge operator and provenance for auditable investigations. A supervisor agent coordinates specialized agents and synthesis agents powered by large language models to profile campaigns and generate empirically validated detection rules for deployment in existing controls. On 103 real-world campaigns (6,020 URLs), PhishLumos achieved a median campaign coverage of 100% and a median detection lead time of 192.8 hours (8.0 days) before expert verification, with a 0.1% false positive rate on 1,000 benign URLs. In a six-month in-the-wild study starting from 600 challenging seed URLs, the generated rules uncovered 192,407 additional URLs; 92.0% were later flagged as malicious by at least one engine in a multi-engine scanning service. Quantitative comparisons against four baselines, including a state-of-the-art LLM-based content classifier, show that under content inaccessibility any content-conditional method (LLM or otherwise) is bounded at 0.341 recall, whereas PhishLumos reaches 0.994 F1 by pivoting to infrastructure evidence.

title: Wheelchair Navigation Assistance via Braille Block Detection with a Single Omnidirectional Camera
authors: Masaki Sendo, Chinthaka Premachandra
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
abstract: We propose a system featuring an omnidirectional camera mounted on an electric wheelchair to automatically detect Braille blocks in all directions using machine learning. This system assists in navigating the electric wheelchair along the detected Braille blocks. Initially, Braille blocks are identified using You Only Look Once version 8 (YOLOv8) from images captured by an onboard omnidirectional camera. The system then determines the travel direction based on these detection results. Subsequently, it estimates the distance between the wheelchair and the Braille block. For this distance estimation, we introduce a novel method based on the equidistant projection technique used for generating omnidirectional camera images. Utilizing this information, the joystick is controlled to maintain a specific distance from the Braille blocks, aiding in the wheelchair's navigation. In this study, we developed a new mechanism compatible with various types of electric wheelchairs. This mechanism operates the joystick, enabling autonomous navigation without the need to interact with the internal systems of the electric wheelchair. During the verification experiments, the system achieved a 97.3% accuracy rate in detecting Braille blocks in both indoor and outdoor settings, a 100% success rate in determining travel direction, and a low average distance measurement error between the wheelchair and Braille blocks. Our findings confirm that the proposed system, with its algorithms and mechanics, effectively assists wheelchair users.

title: Research on the Integration of DCS and Test Data Acquisition System in Nuclear Power Plants
authors: Mengyue Sheng, Meng Guo, Xinxin Fan, Suyang Yan, Shuo Wang, Yue Guo
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Most of the in-service test data acquisition systems in nuclear power plants are independently deployed, using platforms separate from the nuclear power plant DCS system. When high-speed sampling tests are required on site, signals related to the DCS platform have to be connected to the test data acquisition system via hard wiring, which significantly increases the number of field cables and cabinets, and reduces the convenience of system operation. To solve the above problems, this paper conducts an integrated study on the DCS platform and the test data acquisition system from three levels: the level 1 controller, the level 2 data service, and the human-computer interaction system. The level 1 controller realizes the integrated acquisition and uploading of fast-sampling data and ordinary data by adding a fast-sampling data buffer and an ordinary data buffer. The level 2 data service optimizes the data structure to store ordinary data and fast-sampling data in the real-time database and historical database, completing the integrated integration at the data service level. By optimizing the plug-in design of the human-computer interaction system, the functions of the test data acquisition system are integrated into the level 2 human-computer interaction system of DCS, realizing the integrated operation of human-computer interaction. The integrated design scheme can effectively reduce the investment in hardware resources such as cabinets and servers, simplify the field wiring process, provide efficient and convenient operational support for various high-speed sampling tests in nuclear power plants, and improve the overall efficiency of test execution and data application.

title: Ambient Wind Impact on Air-Cooling Island Thermal-Flow Characteristics and Resultant Thermal Economy of Adjacent Dual Direct Air-Cooling Units Under Unequal Load
authors: Jinhan Xu, Huan Ma
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: With the continuous expansion of renewable energy grid integration under the new energy pattern, coal-fired units remain critical for power grid supply-demand balance. Direct air-cooling technology, with prominent water-saving advantages, is widely used in coal-rich and water-scarce regions, and dual parallel air cooling islands have become a common configuration. However, thermal-flow coupling between adjacent islands causes one unit's operating state to significantly affect the other, creating new research needs for deep peak regulation. This paper establishes a coupled simulation framework integrating unit thermodynamic systems and three-dimensional air cooling island computational fluid dynamics models. Taking the typical furnace front ambient wind of a coal-fired power plant as the condition, this study systematically analyzes the air flow distribution and heat rejection characteristics of the air-cooling island, and the thermal economy of the adjacent units under various combinations of wind speed and load. Results show that under furnace front wind, air flow stably distributes with higher inner and lower outer rates; low-flow zones expand inward with increasing wind speed. The high-load side benefits the low-load junction via thermal suction, while the adjacent island effect has limited depth and attenuates inward. Under such conditions, each unit's thermodynamic and economic performance is mainly determined by its own load. These conclusions provide a theoretical basis for cold-end optimal operation under dual-unit asymmetric peak regulation.

title: TMR Anti-Interference Method Based on Spatial Filtering for Multiple Unknown Quantities
authors: Tan Xie, Lijia Ren, Tingting Ding
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: To mitigate the impact of adjacent-wire interference and installation eccentricity on tunnel magnetoresistance (TMR) current sensors, this paper proposes a current measurement method based on spatial filtering. The proposed method constructs the geometric response model of the sensor array to the target conductor and interfering conductors, and imposes a unitresponse constraint for the target conductor together with zeroresponse constraints for the interfering conductors, thereby achieving separation of the target current from interfering currents. Meanwhile, known or calibrated conductor eccentricity parameters are incorporated to correct measurement errors. Simulation and experimental results show that the errors are less than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 1 {\%}}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 8 8 {\%}}$</tex>, respectively, verifying the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Optimal Dispatch of AA-CAES Considering State-of-Charge Dependency
authors: Xianfeng Zhang, Laijun Chen, Shasha He, Sen Cui, Shengwei Mei
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Traditional dispatch models for advanced adiabatic compressed air energy storage (AA-CAES) typically rely on fixedparameter assumptions, which ignore the non-linear impact of State-of-Charge (SoC) on performance and lead to inaccurate economic assessments. This paper proposes a refined optimal dispatch framework that explicitly incorporates SoC-dependent operating characteristics, including variable efficiency and power limits. A piecewise linear model is developed to capture the thermodynamic coupling between air reservoir pressure and system performance, transforming the scheduling problem into a computationally efficient mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) formulation. Case studies based on a 60 MW/240 MWh AA-CAES project demonstrate that the proposed strategy increases daily net profit by 25.0 % and total discharged energy by 38.8 % compared to conventional fixed-parameter benchmarks. The results indicate that accounting for SoC dependency significantly enhances the economic arbitrage potential and improves the asset utilization of AA-CAES plants in power market operations.

title: A Field Diagnosis and Repair Case of Ultra-High Voltage Converter Transformer High-Temperature Overheating Fault
authors: Jin Wang, Xin Wang, Wenfeng Liao, Sen Li, Jingyi Dong
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This paper presents a field diagnosis and repair process for a high-temperature overheating fault in an ultra-high voltage (UHV) converter transformer. Initially, oil chromatography data analysis from different transformer sections localized the overheating fault zone. Subsequent internal inspection after oil drainage identified the root cause: an incorrectly sized bolt in the valve-side end bushing terminal, which caused poor fastening of the lead wire. This led to increased contact resistance and high-temperature overheating under highload test conditions and operational currents.To resolve the fault, the terminal and standard bolt were replaced on-site, with concurrent removal of foreign particles generated by overheating. Post-repair, the transformer was successfully re-commissioned and has demonstrated stable operation, validating both the accuracy of the fault analysis and the effectiveness of the corrective measures. This case achieved on-site fault diagnosis and repair without requiring factory disassembly inspection, delivering significant time and cost savings. The methodology provides a practical reference for addressing similar converter transformer faults in power transmission systems.

title: Carbon Pricing, Green Certificates, and Capacity Compensation: Synergistic Effects on Absorption
authors: Nan Yang, Xiahui Zhang, Yexuan Jin, Kai Xia, Xiangwen Wu
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: This paper develops a policy combination optimization framework for clean energy absorption in the East China Grid, targeting the coordination challenge among carbon pricing, tradable green certificates, and capacity compensation mechanisms. The framework establishes parameterized logarithmic response functions for each policy instrument, introduces pairwise interaction terms to capture synergistic effects, and formulates an optimization model balancing absorption improvement against equivalent social costs under budget constraints. A case study validates the framework across three budget scenarios. Results show that under the medium budget, the optimal combination achieves 6.2 percentage points absorption improvement with a synergy term of 1.1 percentage points, exceeding the best single-policy scheme by 2.7 percentage points. The coordinated portfolio attains a policy cost-effectiveness ratio of 0.419 pp/100M CNY, demonstrating measurable advantages in both absorption performance and resource efficiency compared to isolated single-instrument approaches.

title: Optimization of Low-Carbon Transition Pathways for Power Systems Considering Renewable Energy Output Uncertainty
authors: Xiaoqing Yan, Qiuli Zhao, Xue Ma, Fang Li, Bo Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Driven by carbon neutrality targets, the intermittency and variability of wind and solar power introduce significant uncertainties into power system planning and operation, directly affecting transition costs, investment structures, and system reliability. In this paper, we develop an integrated optimisation model for power systems that couples long-term capacity planning with hour-level operational simulation, with a particular focus on characterising renewable energy output uncertainty. Sixteen fluctuation scenarios are designed, covering both shortfalls and surpluses in wind and solar power, to systematically assess the impacts of varying fluctuation intensities, durations, and intervals on system transition pathways. The findings reveal that: (1) short-term extreme fluctuations in wind and solar output have a limited impact on total system costs, indicating that a high proportion of renewable energy systems can maintain strong resilience given adequate energy storage configuration and enhanced system flexibility; (2) scenarios involving simultaneous reductions in both wind and solar power have the most significant impact on costs, highlighting the need to guard against compound fluctuation risks; and (3) longer fluctuation intervals enable system costs to be mitigated through cross-period dispatching, underscoring the critical role of temporal flexibility in addressing uncertainty.

title: Expert Representation-Driven LightGBM for Photovoltaic Power Forecasting
authors: Tiannuo Wang, Chaozhu Zhang, Minghui Liu, Jie Wu, Maohai Lin
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: As a critical component of clean energy systems, photovoltaic power generation continues to expand its grid-connected capacity with the advancement of carbon neutrality strategies. However, influenced by random environmental factors such as fluctuations in atmospheric transmittance, photovoltaic power generation exhibits significant non-stationary and intermittent characteristics. This makes high-accuracy photovoltaic power prediction crucial for enhancing system perception and accommodation capabilities. While existing prediction methods demonstrate advantages in nonlinear fitting, they are prone to accumulating prediction errors during abrupt environmental changes. To address the issues of poor prediction stability under sudden meteorological variations and the lack of closed-loop correction in single models, this paper proposes a multimodal collaborative prediction model based on expert model-driven LightGBM. To address the prediction instability caused by random disturbances, this paper introduces a closed-loop correction mechanism and achieves multi-modal prediction fusion through adaptive weight learning. Comparative experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves optimal results across all evaluation metrics, validating the effectiveness of deep collaboration between global physical laws and local data characteristics.

title: Carbon Intensity Sensitivity Analysis Method for Distribution Networks Based on the ABCD Model
authors: Xinyu Xu, Yuan Leng
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: Accurate sensitivity calculation is pivotal for achieving low-carbon economic dispatch in new-type power systems. However, existing research is primarily confined to voltage or current sensitivities, with limited exploration into carbon intensity sensitivity. Furthermore, conventional perturbation-based methods rely on nonlinear power flow iterations, which severely constrains online optimization efficiency. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an analytical carbon intensity sensitivity analysis method for distribution networks based on the ABCD parameter model. Specifically, analytical sensitivity formulations of nodal carbon intensity with respect to active and reactive power injections are derived. Case studies on an improved IEEE 33-bus system demonstrate that the proposed analytical method achieves a calculation speedup of more than 10 times compared to traditional numerical methods, while maintaining the prediction error strictly within the magnitude of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${10}^{-4}$</tex>. This facilitates high-fidelity linear prediction of carbon intensity within the perturbation interval. Moreover, the physical mechanism of the reactive power-induced carbon flow transfer effect is revealed. By leveraging the derived analytical sensitivity, precise carbon reduction at target nodes is achieved, providing a cost-effective control strategy for active distribution networks without the necessity of additional renewable energy integration.

title: Design and Application of a Web-Based Real-Time Management System for Massive Vector Data in Power Grid Engineering Feasibility Study and Design
authors: Yufeng Xu, Fang Wang, Mingwei Li, Shengkun Kaung
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Green Energy and Power Systems (ICGEPS)
abstract: In response to issues such as low import efficiency of massive vector data, weak online editing capability, delayed dynamic updates, and insufficient feature-level style control in the feasibility study and design of power grid engineering, a real-time management method for massive vector data aimed at the Web is proposed. A technical framework is constructed based on “sharded parallel import - unified object modeling - spatial attribute collaborative storage - unique ID-driven incremental updates - dynamic service publishing - front-and-backend collaborative rendering,” forming a full-process handling mechanism that covers data access, rendering display, online editing, and service linkage. This framework employs a layered scheduling architecture combined with stream vector file parsing, dynamic tile service generation, incremental updates, and multiuser collaboration mechanisms, achieving second-level import of large data volumes, dynamic updates, feature-level style control, visibility interaction capability, and smooth online editing. Application results indicate that this method effectively enhances the processing efficiency and interactive response capability of massive vector data on the Web, meeting the demands for online design and dynamic applications in complex engineering scenarios.
